description: Performs a one-way https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anova[ANOVA] test on the given lists of numbers. Results are returned in list form as {P value, F test statistic}.
search_text: ANOVA | [statistics] | ANOVA( <List>, <List>, ...): Performs a one-way https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anova[ANOVA] test on the given lists of numbers. Results are returned in list form as {P value, F test statistic}.

description: Returns the affine ratio λ of three collinear points A, B and C, where C = A + λ * AB.
search_text: AffineRatio | [geometry] | AffineRatio( <Point A>, <Point B>, <Point C> ): Returns the affine ratio λ of three collinear points A, B and C, where C = A + λ * AB. | Examples: AffineRatio((-1, 1), (1, 1), (4, 1))

description: Returns the angle between the direction vectors of two lines (result in [0,360°] or [0,2π] depending on the default angle unit).
notes: It is not possible to change the Angle Unit to Radian in GeoGebra 5.0 Web and Tablet App Version.
If the polygon was created in counter clockwise orientation, you get the interior angles. If the polygon was created in clockwise orientation, you get the exterior angles.
The point Rotate( <Point>, <Angle>, <Apex> ) is created as well.

Using a Direction allows to bypass the standard display of angles in 3__D__ which can be set as just [0,180°] or [180°,360°], so that given three points A, B, C in 3__D__ the commands Angle(A, B, C) and Angle(C, B, A) return their real measure instead of the one restricted to the set intervals.
See also Angle and Angle with Given Size tools.
search_text: Angle | [3d] | Angle( <Line>, <Line> ): Returns the angle between the direction vectors of two lines (result in [0,360°] or [0,2π] depending on the default angle unit). | Examples: Angle(y = x + 2, y = 2x + 3); Angle(Line((-2, 0, 0), (0, 0, 2)), Line((2, 0, 0), (0, 0, 2))); Angle(x + 2,  2x + 3)

description: Returns the angle between the line and the plane.
notes: It is not possible to change the Angle Unit to Radian in GeoGebra 5.0 Web and Tablet App Version.
If the polygon was created in counter clockwise orientation, you get the interior angles. If the polygon was created in clockwise orientation, you get the exterior angles.
The point Rotate( <Point>, <Angle>, <Apex> ) is created as well.

Using a Direction allows to bypass the standard display of angles in 3__D__ which can be set as just [0,180°] or [180°,360°], so that given three points A, B, C in 3__D__ the commands Angle(A, B, C) and Angle(C, B, A) return their real measure instead of the one restricted to the set intervals.
See also Angle and Angle with Given Size tools.
search_text: Angle | [3d] | Angle( <Line>, <Plane> ): Returns the angle between the line and the plane. | Examples: Angle(Line((1, 2, 3),(-2, -2, 0)), z = 0)

description: Conic: Returns the angle of twist of a conic section’s major axis (see command Axes). Vector: Returns the angle between the x‐axis and given vector. Point: Returns the angle between the x‐axis and the position vector of the given point. Number: Converts the number into an angle (result in [0,360°] or [0,2π] depending on the default angle unit). Polygon:* Creates all angles of a polygon in mathematically positive orientation (counter clockwise).
notes: It is not possible to change the Angle Unit to Radian in GeoGebra 5.0 Web and Tablet App Version.
If the polygon was created in counter clockwise orientation, you get the interior angles. If the polygon was created in clockwise orientation, you get the exterior angles.
The point Rotate( <Point>, <Angle>, <Apex> ) is created as well.

Using a Direction allows to bypass the standard display of angles in 3__D__ which can be set as just [0,180°] or [180°,360°], so that given three points A, B, C in 3__D__ the commands Angle(A, B, C) and Angle(C, B, A) return their real measure instead of the one restricted to the set intervals.
See also Angle and Angle with Given Size tools.
search_text: Angle | [3d] | Angle( <Object> ): Conic: Returns the angle of twist of a conic section’s major axis (see command Axes). Vector: Returns the angle between the x‐axis and given vector. Point: Returns the angle between the x‐axis and the position vector of the given point. Number: Converts the number into an angle (result in [0,360°] or [0,2π] depending on the default angle unit). Polygon:* Creates all angles of a polygon in mathematically positive orientation (counter clockwise). | Examples: Angle(x²/4+y²/9=1); Angle(Vector((1, 1))); Angle((1, 1))

description: Returns the angle between the two given planes.
notes: It is not possible to change the Angle Unit to Radian in GeoGebra 5.0 Web and Tablet App Version.
If the polygon was created in counter clockwise orientation, you get the interior angles. If the polygon was created in clockwise orientation, you get the exterior angles.
The point Rotate( <Point>, <Angle>, <Apex> ) is created as well.

Using a Direction allows to bypass the standard display of angles in 3__D__ which can be set as just [0,180°] or [180°,360°], so that given three points A, B, C in 3__D__ the commands Angle(A, B, C) and Angle(C, B, A) return their real measure instead of the one restricted to the set intervals.
See also Angle and Angle with Given Size tools.
search_text: Angle | [3d] | Angle( <Plane>, <Plane> ): Returns the angle between the two given planes. | Examples: Angle(2x - y + z = 0, z = 0)

description: Returns the angle of size α drawn from point with apex.
notes: It is not possible to change the Angle Unit to Radian in GeoGebra 5.0 Web and Tablet App Version.
If the polygon was created in counter clockwise orientation, you get the interior angles. If the polygon was created in clockwise orientation, you get the exterior angles.
The point Rotate( <Point>, <Angle>, <Apex> ) is created as well.

Using a Direction allows to bypass the standard display of angles in 3__D__ which can be set as just [0,180°] or [180°,360°], so that given three points A, B, C in 3__D__ the commands Angle(A, B, C) and Angle(C, B, A) return their real measure instead of the one restricted to the set intervals.
See also Angle and Angle with Given Size tools.
search_text: Angle | [3d] | Angle( <Point>, <Apex>, <Angle> ): Returns the angle of size α drawn from point with apex. | Examples: Angle((0, 0), (3, 3), 30°)

description: Returns the angle defined by the given points (result in [0,360°] or [0,2π] depending on the default angle unit).
notes: It is not possible to change the Angle Unit to Radian in GeoGebra 5.0 Web and Tablet App Version.
If the polygon was created in counter clockwise orientation, you get the interior angles. If the polygon was created in clockwise orientation, you get the exterior angles.
The point Rotate( <Point>, <Angle>, <Apex> ) is created as well.

Using a Direction allows to bypass the standard display of angles in 3__D__ which can be set as just [0,180°] or [180°,360°], so that given three points A, B, C in 3__D__ the commands Angle(A, B, C) and Angle(C, B, A) return their real measure instead of the one restricted to the set intervals.
See also Angle and Angle with Given Size tools.
search_text: Angle | [3d] | Angle( <Point>, <Apex>, <Point> ): Returns the angle defined by the given points (result in [0,360°] or [0,2π] depending on the default angle unit). | Examples: Angle((1, 1), (1, 4), (4, 2))

description: Returns the angle defined by the points and the given Direction, that may be a line or a plane (result in [0,360°] or [0,2π] depending on the default angle unit).
notes: It is not possible to change the Angle Unit to Radian in GeoGebra 5.0 Web and Tablet App Version.
If the polygon was created in counter clockwise orientation, you get the interior angles. If the polygon was created in clockwise orientation, you get the exterior angles.
The point Rotate( <Point>, <Angle>, <Apex> ) is created as well.

Using a Direction allows to bypass the standard display of angles in 3__D__ which can be set as just [0,180°] or [180°,360°], so that given three points A, B, C in 3__D__ the commands Angle(A, B, C) and Angle(C, B, A) return their real measure instead of the one restricted to the set intervals.
See also Angle and Angle with Given Size tools.
search_text: Angle | [3d] | Angle( <Point>, <Point>, <Point>, <Direction> ): Returns the angle defined by the points and the given Direction, that may be a line or a plane (result in [0,360°] or [0,2π] depending on the default angle unit). | Examples: Angle((1, -1, 0),(0, 0, 0),(-1, -1, 0), zAxis); Angle((-1, -1, 0),(0, 0, 0),(1, -1, 0), zAxis)

description: Returns the angle between two vectors (result in [0,360°] or [0,2π] depending on the default angle unit).
notes: It is not possible to change the Angle Unit to Radian in GeoGebra 5.0 Web and Tablet App Version.
If the polygon was created in counter clockwise orientation, you get the interior angles. If the polygon was created in clockwise orientation, you get the exterior angles.
The point Rotate( <Point>, <Angle>, <Apex> ) is created as well.

Using a Direction allows to bypass the standard display of angles in 3__D__ which can be set as just [0,180°] or [180°,360°], so that given three points A, B, C in 3__D__ the commands Angle(A, B, C) and Angle(C, B, A) return their real measure instead of the one restricted to the set intervals.
See also Angle and Angle with Given Size tools.
search_text: Angle | [3d] | Angle( <Vector>, <Vector> ): Returns the angle between two vectors (result in [0,360°] or [0,2π] depending on the default angle unit). | Examples: Angle(Vector((1, 1)), Vector((2, 5)))

description: Returns both angle bisectors of the lines.
notes: The second point is apex of this angle.
See also Angle Bisector tool .
search_text: AngleBisector | [geometry] | AngleBisector( <Line>, <Line> ): Returns both angle bisectors of the lines. | Examples: AngleBisector(x + y = 1, x - y = 2)

description: Returns the angle bisector of the angle defined by the three points.
notes: The second point is apex of this angle.
See also Angle Bisector tool .
search_text: AngleBisector | [geometry] | AngleBisector( <Point>, <Point>, <Point> ): Returns the angle bisector of the angle defined by the three points. | Examples: AngleBisector((1, 1), (4, 4), (7, 1))

description: Appends the object to the list and yields the results in a new list.
search_text: Append | [list] | Append( <List>, <Object> ): Appends the object to the list and yields the results in a new list. | Examples: Append({1, 2, 3}, 4)

description: Appends the list to the object and yields the results in a new list.
search_text: Append | [list] | Append( <Object>, <List> ): Appends the list to the object and yields the results in a new list. | Examples: Append(4, {1, 2, 3})

description: Transforms the object O so that point P of O is mapped to: point MP, if P is a 2D point and M is a 2 x 2 matrix point project(M(x(P), y(P), 1)), if P is a 2D point and M a 3 x 3 matrix: project is a projection, mapping point (x, y, z) to (x/z, y/z). point MP, if P is a 3D point and M a 3 x 3 matrix point NP, if P is a 3D point and M a 2 x 2 matrix: the matrix N is the completion or order 3 of M: given M = (formula) then N = (formula)
notes: This command also works with quadrics and images.
search_text: ApplyMatrix | [vector_and_matrix] | ApplyMatrix( <xref:/Matrices.adoc[Matrix]>, <xref:/Geometric_Objects.adoc[Object]> ): Transforms the object O so that point P of O is mapped to: point MP, if P is a 2D point and M is a 2 x 2 matrix point project(M(x(P), y(P), 1)), if P is a 2D point and M a 3 x 3 matrix: project is a projection, mapping point (x, y, z) to (x/z, y/z). point MP, if P is a 3D point and M a 3 x 3 matrix point NP, if P is a 3D point and M a 2 x 2 matrix: the matrix N is the completion or order 3 of M: given M = (formula) then N = (formula) | Examples: M={{cos(π/2),-sin(π/2)}, {sin(π/2), cos(π/2)}}; u = (2,1); ApplyMatrix(M,u)

description: Returns the circle arc of the given circle, whose endpoints are identified by the specified values of the parameter.
notes: Internally the following parametric forms are used:Circle: (r cos(t), r sin(t)) where r is the circle's radius.
Internally the following parametric forms are used:Ellipse: (a cos(t), b sin(t)) where a and b are the lengths of the semimajor and semiminor axes.
See also CircumcircularArc command.
search_text: Arc | [geometry] | Arc( <Circle>, <Parameter Value>, <Parameter Value> ): Returns the circle arc of the given circle, whose endpoints are identified by the specified values of the parameter.

description: Returns the directed arc (counterclockwise) of the given circle, with endpoints M and N.
notes: Internally the following parametric forms are used:Circle: (r cos(t), r sin(t)) where r is the circle's radius.
Internally the following parametric forms are used:Ellipse: (a cos(t), b sin(t)) where a and b are the lengths of the semimajor and semiminor axes.
See also CircumcircularArc command.
search_text: Arc | [geometry] | Arc( <Circle>, <Point M >, <Point N> ): Returns the directed arc (counterclockwise) of the given circle, with endpoints M and N.

description: Returns the circle arc of the given ellipse, whose endpoints are identified by the specified values of the parameter.
notes: Internally the following parametric forms are used:Circle: (r cos(t), r sin(t)) where r is the circle's radius.
Internally the following parametric forms are used:Ellipse: (a cos(t), b sin(t)) where a and b are the lengths of the semimajor and semiminor axes.
See also CircumcircularArc command.
search_text: Arc | [geometry] | Arc( <Ellipse>, <Parameter Value>, <Parameter Value> ): Returns the circle arc of the given ellipse, whose endpoints are identified by the specified values of the parameter.

description: Returns the directed arc (counterclockwise) of the given ellipse, with endpoints M and N.
notes: Internally the following parametric forms are used:Circle: (r cos(t), r sin(t)) where r is the circle's radius.
Internally the following parametric forms are used:Ellipse: (a cos(t), b sin(t)) where a and b are the lengths of the semimajor and semiminor axes.
See also CircumcircularArc command.
search_text: Arc | [geometry] | Arc( <Ellipse>, <Point M>, <Point N> ): Returns the directed arc (counterclockwise) of the given ellipse, with endpoints M and N.

description: Decides if the points are collinear. Normally this command computes the result numerically. This behavior can be changed by using the Prove command.
notes: See also AreConcurrent, AreConcyclic, AreCongruent, AreEqual, ArePerpendicular, AreParallel, IsTangent commands.
search_text: AreCollinear | [geometry] | AreCollinear( <Point>, <Point>, <Point> ): Decides if the points are collinear. Normally this command computes the result numerically. This behavior can be changed by using the Prove command. | Examples: AreCollinear((1, 2), (3, 4), (5, 6))

description: Decides if the lines are concurrent. If the lines are parallel, they considered to have a common point in infinity, thus this command returns true in this case. Normally this command computes the result numerically. This behavior can be changed by using the Prove command.
notes: See also AreCollinear, AreConcyclic, AreCongruent, AreEqual, ArePerpendicular, AreParallel, IsTangent commands.
search_text: AreConcurrent | [geometry] | AreConcurrent( <Line>, <Line>, <Line> ): Decides if the lines are concurrent. If the lines are parallel, they considered to have a common point in infinity, thus this command returns true in this case. Normally this command computes the result numerically. This behavior can be changed by using the Prove command. | Examples: AreConcurrent(Line((1, 2), (3, 4)), Line((1, 2), (3, 5)), Line((1, 2), (3, 6)))

description: Decides if the points are concyclic. Normally this command computes the result numerically. This behavior can be changed by using the Prove command.
notes: See also AreCollinear, AreConcurrent, AreCongruent, AreEqual, ArePerpendicular, AreParallel, IsTangent commands.
search_text: AreConcyclic | [geometry] | AreConcyclic( <Point>, <Point>, <Point>, <Point> ): Decides if the points are concyclic. Normally this command computes the result numerically. This behavior can be changed by using the Prove command. | Examples: AreConcyclic((1, 2), (3, 4), (1, 4), (3, 2))

description: Decides if the objects are congruent. Normally this command computes the result numerically. This behavior can be changed by using the Prove command.
notes: See also AreEqual, AreCollinear, AreConcyclic, AreConcurrent, ArePerpendicular, AreParallel, IsTangent commands.
search_text: AreCongruent | [geometry] | AreCongruent( <Object>, <Object> ): Decides if the objects are congruent. Normally this command computes the result numerically. This behavior can be changed by using the Prove command. | Examples: AreCongruent(Circle((0, 0),1),x^2+y^2=1); AreCongruent(Circle((1, 1),1),x^2+y^2=1)

description: Decides if the objects are equal. Normally this command computes the result numerically. This behavior can be changed by using the Prove command.
notes: AreEqual(Segment((1, 2), (3, 4)), Segment((3, 4), (1, 6))) is different from Segment((1, 2), (3, 4)) == Segment((3, 4), (1, 6)) as the latter compares just the lengths See also AreCollinear, AreConcyclic, AreConcurrent, AreCongruent, ArePerpendicular, AreParallel, IsTangent commands.
search_text: AreEqual | [algebra] | AreEqual( <Object>, <Object> ): Decides if the objects are equal. Normally this command computes the result numerically. This behavior can be changed by using the Prove command. | Examples: AreEqual(Circle((0, 0),1),x^2+y^2=1)

description: Decides if the lines are parallel. Normally this command computes the result numerically. This behavior can be changed by using the Prove command.
notes: See also AreCollinear, AreConcurrent, AreCongruent, AreConcyclic, AreEqual, ArePerpendicular, IsTangent commands.
search_text: AreParallel | [geometry] | AreParallel( <Line>, <Line> ): Decides if the lines are parallel. Normally this command computes the result numerically. This behavior can be changed by using the Prove command. | Examples: AreParallel(Line[(1, 2), (3, 4)), Line((5, 6),(7,8)))

description: Decides if the lines are perpendicular. Normally this command computes the result numerically. This behavior can be changed by using the Prove command.
notes: See also AreCollinear, AreConcurrent, AreConcyclic, AreCongruent, AreEqual, AreParallel, IsTangent commands.
search_text: ArePerpendicular | [geometry] | ArePerpendicular( <Line>, <Line> ): Decides if the lines are perpendicular. Normally this command computes the result numerically. This behavior can be changed by using the Prove command. | Examples: ArePerpendicular(Line((-1, 0), (0, -1)), Line((0, 0),(2,2)))

description: Calculates the area of a conic section (circle or ellipse).
notes: for Polygons, the absolute value of the Algebraic Area is calculated (which gives unexpected answers for self-intersecting polygons) In order to calculate the area between two function graphs, you need to use the command IntegralBetween. * See also the Area tool.
search_text: Area | [geometry] | Area( <Conic> ): Calculates the area of a conic section (circle or ellipse). | Examples: Area(x^2 + y^2 = 2)

description: Calculates the area of the polygon defined by the given points.
notes: for Polygons, the absolute value of the Algebraic Area is calculated (which gives unexpected answers for self-intersecting polygons) In order to calculate the area between two function graphs, you need to use the command IntegralBetween. * See also the Area tool.
search_text: Area | [geometry] | Area( <Point>, ..., <Point> ): Calculates the area of the polygon defined by the given points. | Examples: Area((0, 0), (3, 0), (3, 2), (0, 2))

description: Calculates the area of the polygon.
notes: for Polygons, the absolute value of the Algebraic Area is calculated (which gives unexpected answers for self-intersecting polygons) In order to calculate the area between two function graphs, you need to use the command IntegralBetween. * See also the Area tool.
search_text: Area | [geometry] | Area( <Polygon> ): Calculates the area of the polygon.

description: Evaluates the expression according to the condition
notes: See also Solve Command.
search_text: Assume | [algebra] | Assume( <Condition>, <Expression> ): Evaluates the expression according to the condition | Examples: Assume(a > 0, Integral(exp(-a x), 0, infinity)); 1 / a; Assume(x>0 && n>0, Solve(log(n^2*(x/n)^lg(x))=log(x^2), x))

description: Yields both asymptotes of the conic.
search_text: Asymptote | [functions_and_calculus] | Asymptote( <Conic> ): Yields both asymptotes of the conic. | Examples: Asymptote(x^2 - y^2 /4 = 1)

description: GeoGebra will attempt to find the asymptotes of the function and return them in a list. It may not find them all, for example vertical asymptotes of non-rational functions such as ln(x). This syntax is not available in the Graphing and Geometry Apps
search_text: Asymptote | [functions_and_calculus] | Asymptote( <Function> ): GeoGebra will attempt to find the asymptotes of the function and return them in a list. It may not find them all, for example vertical asymptotes of non-rational functions such as ln(x). This syntax is not available in the Graphing and Geometry Apps | Examples: Asymptote((x^3 - 2x^2 - x + 4) / (2x^2 - 2))

description: Yields a list containing all the asymptotes of the Implicit Curve.
search_text: Asymptote | [functions_and_calculus] | Asymptote( <Implicit Curve> ): Yields a list containing all the asymptotes of the Implicit Curve. | Examples: Asymptote(x^3 + y^3 + y^2 - 3 x = 0)

description: If View = 0, a copy of given object is created. For View = 1 or View = 2 this command creates a dependent copy of given object whose size in given Graphics View is constant.
notes: Once the copy is created, four more arguments are added to the command definition.
All points of the object are copied, even if they lie outside the view.
search_text: AttachCopyToView | [other] | AttachCopyToView( <Object>, <View 0|1|2> ): If View = 0, a copy of given object is created. For View = 1 or View = 2 this command creates a dependent copy of given object whose size in given Graphics View is constant. | Examples: poly = Polygon((0, 0), (1, 0), (1, 1), (0, 1)); AttachCopyToView(poly, 1)

description: If View = 0, a copy of given object is created. For View = 1 or View = 2 this command creates a dependent copy of given object whose size in given Graphics View is transformed using the affine transform that maps Point 1 to a point whose screen coordinates (in pixels) are equal to Screen Point 1, and Point 2 to a point with screen coordinates equal to Screen Point 2.
notes: Once the copy is created, four more arguments are added to the command definition.
All points of the object are copied, even if they lie outside the view.
search_text: AttachCopyToView | [other] | AttachCopyToView( <Object>, <View 0|1|2>, <Point 1>, <Point 2>, <Screen Point 1>, <Screen Point 2> ): If View = 0, a copy of given object is created. For View = 1 or View = 2 this command creates a dependent copy of given object whose size in given Graphics View is transformed using the affine transform that maps Point 1 to a point whose screen coordinates (in pixels) are equal to Screen Point 1, and Point 2 to a point with screen coordinates equal to Screen Point 2. | Examples: poly = Polygon((0, 0), (1, 0), (1, 1), (0, 1)); AttachCopyToView(poly, 1, (0, 0), (1, 1), (0, 0), (100, 100))

description: Returns the equations of the major and minor axes of a conic section.
notes: See also MajorAxis and MinorAxis commands.

Specifically: if the given quadric is a cylinder, the command yields the two axes of the bottom circle and the rotation axis. if the given quadric is a sphere, the command yields the three axes parallel to the coordinate system axes.
search_text: Axes | [3d] | Axes( <Conic> ): Returns the equations of the major and minor axes of a conic section.

description: Creates the 3 axes of the given quadric.
notes: See also MajorAxis and MinorAxis commands.

Specifically: if the given quadric is a cylinder, the command yields the two axes of the bottom circle and the rotation axis. if the given quadric is a sphere, the command yields the three axes parallel to the coordinate system axes.
search_text: Axes | [3d] | Axes( <Quadric> ): Creates the 3 axes of the given quadric. | Examples: Axes(x^2 + y^2 + z^2= 3)

description: Returns the current step width for the x‐axis.
notes: See also AxisStepY command. Together with the Corner and Sequence commands, the AxisStepX and AxisStepY commands allow you to create custom axes (also see section Customizing Coordinate Axes and Grid).
search_text: AxisStepX | [geogebra_general] | AxisStepX( ): Returns the current step width for the x‐axis.

description: Returns the current step width for the y‐axis.
notes: See also AxisStepX command. Together with the Corner and Sequence commands, the AxisStepX and AxisStepY commands allow you to create custom axes (also see section Customizing Coordinate Axes and Grid).
search_text: AxisStepY | [geogebra_general] | AxisStepY( ): Returns the current step width for the y‐axis.

syntax: BarChart( <List of Data> , <List of Frequencies>, <Width of Bars> )
description: Creates a bar chart using the list of data and corresponding frequencies. Bars' width is given.
notes: The numbers in the list of raw data need to be arranged in increasing order.
It is possible to specify a different color/filling for each bar in the Object Properties.
search_text: BarChart | [chart] | BarChart( <List of Data> , <List of Frequencies>, <Width of Bars> ): Creates a bar chart using the list of data and corresponding frequencies. Bars' width is given. | Examples: BarChart({10, 11, 12, 13, 14}, {5, 8, 12, 0, 1}, 0.5); BarChart({10, 11, 12, 13, 14}, {5, 8, 12, 0, 1}, 0)

description: Creates a bar chart using the list of data with corresponding frequencies.
notes: The numbers in the list of raw data need to be arranged in increasing order.
It is possible to specify a different color/filling for each bar in the Object Properties.
search_text: BarChart | [chart] | BarChart( <List of Data>, <List of Frequencies> ): Creates a bar chart using the list of data with corresponding frequencies. | Examples: BarChart({10, 11, 12, 13, 14}, {5, 8, 12, 0, 1}); BarChart({5, 6, 7, 8, 9}, {1, 0, 12, 43, 3}); BarChart({0.3, 0.4, 0.5, 0.6}, {12, 33, 13, 4})

syntax: BarChart( <List of Raw Data>, <Width of Bars>, <Vertical Scale Factor (optional)> )
description: Creates a bar chart using the given raw data; the bars have the given width and the height of the bars depends on the vertical scale factor.
notes: The numbers in the list of raw data need to be arranged in increasing order.
It is possible to specify a different color/filling for each bar in the Object Properties.
search_text: BarChart | [chart] | BarChart( <List of Raw Data>, <Width of Bars>, <Vertical Scale Factor (optional)> ): Creates a bar chart using the given raw data; the bars have the given width and the height of the bars depends on the vertical scale factor. | Examples: BarChart({1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 5, 5, 5, 5}, 1); BarChart({1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 5, 5, 5, 5}, 1, 2)

description: Creates a bar chart over the interval [Start Value, End Value], that calculates the bars’ heights using the expression of the given Variable in the interval [From number, To number].
examples: BarChart(-0.5, n + 0.5, BinomialCoefficient(n,k) * p^k * q^(n-k), k, 0, n)
notes: The numbers in the list of raw data need to be arranged in increasing order.
It is possible to specify a different color/filling for each bar in the Object Properties.
search_text: BarChart | [chart] | BarChart( <Start Value>, <End Value> , <Expression>, <Variable>, <From Number>, <To Number> ): Creates a bar chart over the interval [Start Value, End Value], that calculates the bars’ heights using the expression of the given Variable in the interval [From number, To number]. | Examples: BarChart(-0.5, n + 0.5, BinomialCoefficient(n,k) * p^k * q^(n-k), k, 0, n)

syntax: BarChart( <Start Value>, <End Value>, <Expression>, <Variable>, <From Number>, <To Number>, <Step Width> )
description: Creates a bar chart over the interval [Start Value, End Value], that calculates the bars’ heights using the expression of the given Variable in the interval [From number, To number] with given Step width.
notes: The numbers in the list of raw data need to be arranged in increasing order.
It is possible to specify a different color/filling for each bar in the Object Properties.
search_text: BarChart | [chart] | BarChart( <Start Value>, <End Value>, <Expression>, <Variable>, <From Number>, <To Number>, <Step Width> ): Creates a bar chart over the interval [Start Value, End Value], that calculates the bars’ heights using the expression of the given Variable in the interval [From number, To number] with given Step width.

description: Creates a bar chart over the given interval: the number of bars is determined by the length of the list, whose elements are the heights of the bars.
notes: The numbers in the list of raw data need to be arranged in increasing order.
It is possible to specify a different color/filling for each bar in the Object Properties.
search_text: BarChart | [chart] | BarChart( <Start Value>, <End Value>, <List of Heights> ): Creates a bar chart over the given interval: the number of bars is determined by the length of the list, whose elements are the heights of the bars. | Examples: BarChart(10, 20, {1, 2, 3, 4, 5})

description: Set the center of a system of points in the list, defined as the average of their positions, weighted by their value, using the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Center_of_mass[proper formula].
notes: This command differs among variants of English: Barycenter (US) Barycentre (UK + Aus)
search_text: Barycenter | [geometry] | Barycenter( <List of Points>, <List of Weights> ): Set the center of a system of points in the list, defined as the average of their positions, weighted by their value, using the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Center_of_mass[proper formula]. | Examples: Barycenter({(2, 0), (0, 2), (-2, 0), (0, -2)}, {1, 1, 1, 1}); Barycenter({(2, 0), (0, 2), (-2, 0), (0, -2)}, {2, 1, 1, 1})

description: For Cumulative = false returns the bar graph of https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bernoulli_distribution[Bernoulli distribution] where probability of success is equal to p. For Cumulative = true returns the bar graph of cumulative Bernoulli distribution.
search_text: Bernoulli | [probability] | Bernoulli( <Probability p>, <Boolean Cumulative> ): For Cumulative = false returns the bar graph of https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bernoulli_distribution[Bernoulli distribution] where probability of success is equal to p. For Cumulative = true returns the bar graph of cumulative Bernoulli distribution.

description: Calculates the value of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beta_distribution[Beta distribution] with parameters α, β at the variable value v, that is the probability P(X≤v) where X is a random variable with a beta distribution with parameters α and β
notes: See also InverseBeta command.
search_text: BetaDist | [probability] | BetaDist( <Number α>, <Number β>, <Variable value> ): Calculates the value of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beta_distribution[Beta distribution] with parameters α, β at the variable value v, that is the probability P(X≤v) where X is a random variable with a beta distribution with parameters α and β

description: If Cumulative is true, calculates the value of the cumulative distribution function of a Beta distribution with parameters α and β at the variable value v. If Cumulative is false, it calculates the value of the probability density function (pdf) of the corresponding beta distribution at v.
notes: See also InverseBeta command.
search_text: BetaDist | [probability] | BetaDist( <Number α>, <Number β>, <Variable value>, <Boolean Cumulative> ): If Cumulative is true, calculates the value of the cumulative distribution function of a Beta distribution with parameters α and β at the variable value v. If Cumulative is false, it calculates the value of the probability density function (pdf) of the corresponding beta distribution at v.

description: If Cumulative is true, creates the cumulative distribution function of a beta distribution with parameters α and β, otherwise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the corresponding Beta distribution.
notes: See also InverseBeta command.
search_text: BetaDist | [probability] | BetaDist(<Number α>, <Number β>, x, <Boolean Cumulative>): If Cumulative is true, creates the cumulative distribution function of a beta distribution with parameters α and β, otherwise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the corresponding Beta distribution.

description: Returns a histogram of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binomial_distribution[Binomial distribution]. The parameter Number of Trials specifies the number of independent Bernoulli trials and the parameter Probability of Success specifies the probability of success in one trial.
notes: The syntaxes BinomialDist(10, 0.2, {1,2,3}) and BinomialDist(10, 0.2, 1..3) are equivalent.
search_text: BinomialDist | [probability] | BinomialDist( <Number of Trials>, <Probability of Success> ): Returns a histogram of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binomial_distribution[Binomial distribution]. The parameter Number of Trials specifies the number of independent Bernoulli trials and the parameter Probability of Success specifies the probability of success in one trial.

syntax: BinomialDist( <Number of Trials>, <Probability of Success>, <Boolean Cumulative> )
description: Returns a histogram of a Binomial distribution when Cumulative = false. Returns a graph of a cumulative Binomial distribution when Cumulative = true. First two parameters are same as above.
notes: The syntaxes BinomialDist(10, 0.2, {1,2,3}) and BinomialDist(10, 0.2, 1..3) are equivalent.
search_text: BinomialDist | [probability] | BinomialDist( <Number of Trials>, <Probability of Success>, <Boolean Cumulative> ): Returns a histogram of a Binomial distribution when Cumulative = false. Returns a graph of a cumulative Binomial distribution when Cumulative = true. First two parameters are same as above.

syntax: BinomialDist( <Number of Trials>, <Probability of Success>, <List of values>)
description: Calculates P(u ≤ X ≤ v) by applying the previous syntax (with Cumulative = false) and adding the values obtained when the elements of the List of values are used as variable values.
notes: The syntaxes BinomialDist(10, 0.2, {1,2,3}) and BinomialDist(10, 0.2, 1..3) are equivalent.
search_text: BinomialDist | [probability] | BinomialDist( <Number of Trials>, <Probability of Success>, <List of values>): Calculates P(u ≤ X ≤ v) by applying the previous syntax (with Cumulative = false) and adding the values obtained when the elements of the List of values are used as variable values. | Examples: BinomialDist(10, 0.2, {1,2,3}); BinomialDist(10, 0.2, 1, false); BinomialDist(10, 0.2, 2, false)

syntax: BinomialDist( <Number of Trials>, <Probability of Success>, <List of values>)
description: Calculates P(u ≤ X ≤ v) by applying the previous syntax (with Cumulative = false) and adding the values obtained when the elements of the List of values are used as variable values.
notes: The syntaxes BinomialDist(10, 0.2, {1,2,3}) and BinomialDist(10, 0.2, 1..3) are equivalent.
search_text: BinomialDist | [probability] | BinomialDist( <Number of Trials>, <Probability of Success>, <List of values>): Calculates P(u ≤ X ≤ v) by applying the previous syntax (with Cumulative = false) and adding the values obtained when the elements of the List of values are used as variable values. | Examples: BinomialDist(10, 0.2, {1,2,3}); BinomialDist(10, 0.2, 1, false); BinomialDist(10, 0.2, 2, false)

syntax: BinomialDist( <Number of Trials>, <Probability of Success>, <Variable Value>, <Boolean Cumulative> )
description: Let X be a Binomial random variable and let v be the variable value. Returns P( X = v) when Cumulative = false. Returns P( X ≤ v) when Cumulative = true. First two parameters are same as above.
notes: The syntaxes BinomialDist(10, 0.2, {1,2,3}) and BinomialDist(10, 0.2, 1..3) are equivalent.
search_text: BinomialDist | [probability] | BinomialDist( <Number of Trials>, <Probability of Success>, <Variable Value>, <Boolean Cumulative> ): Let X be a Binomial random variable and let v be the variable value. Returns P( X = v) when Cumulative = false. Returns P( X ≤ v) when Cumulative = true. First two parameters are same as above.

syntax: BinomialDist( <Number of Trials>, <Probability of Success>, <Variable Value>, <Boolean Cumulative> )
description: Let X be a Binomial random variable and let v be the variable value. Returns P( X = v) when Cumulative = false. Returns P( X ≤ v) when Cumulative = true.
notes: The syntaxes BinomialDist(10, 0.2, {1,2,3}) and BinomialDist(10, 0.2, 1..3) are equivalent.
search_text: BinomialDist | [probability] | BinomialDist( <Number of Trials>, <Probability of Success>, <Variable Value>, <Boolean Cumulative> ): Let X be a Binomial random variable and let v be the variable value. Returns P( X = v) when Cumulative = false. Returns P( X ≤ v) when Cumulative = true. | Examples: f(x):=BinomialDist(100,x,36,true)-BinomialDist(100,x,23,true); BinomialDist(3, 0.9, 0, false); BinomialDist(3, 0.9, 1, false)

description: Creates the bottom of the limited quadric.
notes: See also Top Command, Ends Command and Side Command.
search_text: Bottom | [3d] | Bottom( <Quadric> ): Creates the bottom of the limited quadric. | Examples: Bottom(cylinder)

syntax: BoxPlot( <yOffset>, <yScale>, <List of Data>, <List of Frequencies>, <Boolean Outliers> )
description: This allows data from a frequency table to be easily plotted as a boxplot.
search_text: BoxPlot | [chart] | BoxPlot( <yOffset>, <yScale>, <List of Data>, <List of Frequencies>, <Boolean Outliers> ): This allows data from a frequency table to be easily plotted as a boxplot.

description: This allows outliers to be plotted as "X"s rather than included in the boxplot. For this command, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Outlier[outliers] are data lying below Q1 - 1.5 (Q3 - Q1) or above Q3 + 1.5 (Q3 - Q1) (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IQR[see IQR]).
search_text: BoxPlot | [chart] | BoxPlot( <yOffset>, <yScale>, <List of Raw Data>, <Boolean Outliers> ): This allows outliers to be plotted as "X"s rather than included in the boxplot. For this command, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Outlier[outliers] are data lying below Q1 - 1.5 (Q3 - Q1) or above Q3 + 1.5 (Q3 - Q1) (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IQR[see IQR]).

syntax: BoxPlot( yOffset, yScale, List of Raw Data )
description: Creates a box plot using the given raw data and whose vertical position in the coordinate system is controlled by variable yOffset and whose height is influenced by factor yScale.
search_text: BoxPlot | [chart] | BoxPlot( yOffset, yScale, List of Raw Data ): Creates a box plot using the given raw data and whose vertical position in the coordinate system is controlled by variable yOffset and whose height is influenced by factor yScale. | Examples: BoxPlot(0, 1, {2, 2, 3, 4, 5, 5, 6, 7, 7, 8, 8, 8, 9})

syntax: BoxPlot( yOffset, yScale, Start Value, Q1, Median, Q3, End Value )
description: Creates a box plot for the given statistical data in interval (Start Value, End Value).
search_text: BoxPlot | [chart] | BoxPlot( yOffset, yScale, Start Value, Q1, Median, Q3, End Value ): Creates a box plot for the given statistical data in interval (Start Value, End Value).

description: Creates a new button.
search_text: Button | [other] | Button( ): Creates a new button.

description: Creates a new button with given caption.
search_text: Button | [other] | Button( <Caption> ): Creates a new button with given caption. | Examples: Button("Ok")

description: Returns a boolean value: `true` if CAS commands were already loaded, `false` otherwise. The value is dynamic (changes to `true` when CAS commands are loaded)
notes: This commands is useful in the web version of GeoGebra where CAS commands are loaded with a delay after the app is started. You can use this command together with Conditional Visibility to hide some construction elements while CAS is loading.
search_text: CASLoaded | [geogebra_general] | CASLoaded(): Returns a boolean value: `true` if CAS commands were already loaded, `false` otherwise. The value is dynamic (changes to `true` when CAS commands are loaded)

description: Factorizes a given expression, allowing for complex factors.
notes: This command differs among variants of English: CFactor (US) CFactorise (UK + Aus)
This command factors expressions over the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaussian_rational[Complex Rational Numbers]. To factor over rational numbers, see the Factor Command.
search_text: CFactor | [algebra] | CFactor( <Expression> ): Factorizes a given expression, allowing for complex factors. | Examples: CFactor(x^2 + 4)

description: Factorizes an expression with respect to a given variable, allowing for complex factors.
notes: This command differs among variants of English: CFactor (US) CFactorise (UK + Aus)
This command factors expressions over the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaussian_rational[Complex Rational Numbers]. To factor over rational numbers, see the Factor Command.
search_text: CFactor | [algebra] | CFactor( <Expression>, <Variable> ): Factorizes an expression with respect to a given variable, allowing for complex factors. | Examples: CFactor(a^2 + x^2, a); CFactor(a^2 + x^2, x)

description: Factors over the complex irrationals.
notes: See also IFactor command.
search_text: CIFactor | [algebra] | CIFactor( <Expression> ): Factors over the complex irrationals. | Examples: CIFactor(x^2 + x + 1)

description: Factors over the complex irrationals with respect to a given variable.
notes: See also IFactor command.
search_text: CIFactor | [algebra] | CIFactor( <Expression>, <Variable> ): Factors over the complex irrationals with respect to a given variable. | Examples: CIFactor(a^2 + a + 1, a)

description: Solves a given equation for the main variable and returns a list of all solutions, allowing for complex solutions.
notes: The complex ί is obtained by pressing [.kcode]#ALT# + [.kcode]#i#. See also CSolve Command and Solutions Command.
search_text: CSolutions | [algebra] | CSolutions( <Equation> ): Solves a given equation for the main variable and returns a list of all solutions, allowing for complex solutions. | Examples: CSolutions(x^2 = -1)

description: Solves an equation for a given unknown variable and returns a list of all solutions, allowing for complex solutions.
notes: The complex ί is obtained by pressing [.kcode]#ALT# + [.kcode]#i#. See also CSolve Command and Solutions Command.
search_text: CSolutions | [algebra] | CSolutions( <Equation>, <Variable> ): Solves an equation for a given unknown variable and returns a list of all solutions, allowing for complex solutions. | Examples: CSolutions(a^2 = -1, a)

description: Solves a set of equations for a given set of unknown variables and returns a list of all solutions, allowing for complex solutions.
notes: The complex ί is obtained by pressing [.kcode]#ALT# + [.kcode]#i#. See also CSolve Command and Solutions Command.
search_text: CSolutions | [algebra] | CSolutions( <List of Equations>, <List of Variables> ): Solves a set of equations for a given set of unknown variables and returns a list of all solutions, allowing for complex solutions. | Examples: CSolutions({y^2 = x - 1, x = 2 * y - 1}, {x, y})

description: Solves a given equation for the main variable and returns a list of all solutions, allowing for complex solutions.
notes: The complex ί is obtained by pressing [.kcode]#ALT# + [.kcode]#i#. See also CSolutions Command and Solve Command.
search_text: CSolve | [algebra] | CSolve( <Equation> ): Solves a given equation for the main variable and returns a list of all solutions, allowing for complex solutions. | Examples: CSolve(x^2 = -1)

description: Solves an equation for a given unknown variable and returns a list of all solutions, allowing for complex solutions.
notes: The complex ί is obtained by pressing [.kcode]#ALT# + [.kcode]#i#. See also CSolutions Command and Solve Command.
search_text: CSolve | [algebra] | CSolve( <Equation>, <Variable> ): Solves an equation for a given unknown variable and returns a list of all solutions, allowing for complex solutions. | Examples: CSolve(a^2 = -1, a)

description: Solves a set of equations for a given set of unknown variables and returns a list of all solutions, allowing for complex solutions.
notes: The complex ί is obtained by pressing [.kcode]#ALT# + [.kcode]#i#. See also CSolutions Command and Solve Command.
search_text: CSolve | [algebra] | CSolve( <List of Equations>, <List of Variables> ): Solves a set of equations for a given set of unknown variables and returns a list of all solutions, allowing for complex solutions. | Examples: CSolve({y^2 = x - 1, x = 2 * y - 1}, {x, y})

description: Calculates the value of the cumulative density function (cdf) at the given variable value v of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cauchy_distribution[Cauchy distribution], that is the probability P(X≤v) where X is a random variable of a Cauchy distribution of given parameters median and scale.
notes: This syntax returns the probability for a given value, that is the area under the Cauchy distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: Cauchy | [probability] | Cauchy( <Median>, <Scale>, <Variable value> ): Calculates the value of the cumulative density function (cdf) at the given variable value v of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cauchy_distribution[Cauchy distribution], that is the probability P(X≤v) where X is a random variable of a Cauchy distribution of given parameters median and scale. | Examples: Cauchy(1, 2, 3)

description: If Cumulative is true, calculates the value of a cumulative distribution function of a Cauchy distribution at variable value, otherwise it calculates the value of the probability density function (pdf) at variable value of the given Cauchy distribution of parameters median and scale.
notes: This syntax returns the probability for a given value, that is the area under the Cauchy distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: Cauchy | [probability] | Cauchy( <Median>, <Scale>, <Variable value>, <Boolean Cumulative>): If Cumulative is true, calculates the value of a cumulative distribution function of a Cauchy distribution at variable value, otherwise it calculates the value of the probability density function (pdf) at variable value of the given Cauchy distribution of parameters median and scale.

description: If Cumulative is true, creates the cumulative distribution function of a Cauchy distribution, otherwise creates the probability density function of the given Cauchy distribution of parameters median and scale.
notes: This syntax returns the probability for a given value, that is the area under the Cauchy distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: Cauchy | [probability] | Cauchy( <Median>, <Scale>, x, <Boolean Cumulative>): If Cumulative is true, creates the cumulative distribution function of a Cauchy distribution, otherwise creates the probability density function of the given Cauchy distribution of parameters median and scale.

description: Returns copy of spreadsheet cell in given column and row.
notes: By default the cells in spreadsheet cells are auxiliary and in such case this command returns auxiliary object as well. You must make sure that the cells you refer to are earlier in the Construction Protocol than this command.
search_text: Cell | [spreadsheet] | Cell( <Column>, <Row> ): Returns copy of spreadsheet cell in given column and row. | Examples: Cell(2, 1)

description: Creates a list containing the cell values in this cell range.
notes: A1:A3 is a shorter syntax.
search_text: CellRange | [spreadsheet] | CellRange( <Start Cell>, <End Cell> ): Creates a list containing the cell values in this cell range. | Examples: A1 = 1; A2 = 4; A3 = 9

description: Returns the center of a circle, ellipse, or hyperbola.
notes: See also Midpoint or Center (, : Midpoint or Centre) tool .
search_text: Center | [3d] | Center( <Conic> ): Returns the center of a circle, ellipse, or hyperbola. | Examples: Center(x^2 + 4 y^2 + 2x - 8y + 1 = 0); Centre(x^2 + 4 y^2 + 2x - 8y + 1 = 0)

description: Creates the center of a quadric (e.g. sphere, cone, etc.).
notes: See also Midpoint or Center (, : Midpoint or Centre) tool .
search_text: Center | [3d] | Center( <Quadric> ): Creates the center of a quadric (e.g. sphere, cone, etc.). | Examples: Center(x^2 + (y-1)^2 + (z-2)^2 = 1)

description: Translates the Graphics View so that the specified point is in the center.
notes: This command differs among variants of English: CenterView (US) CentreView (UK + Aus)
If multiple Graphics Views are open, the command applies to the active View.
search_text: CenterView | [other] | CenterView( <Center Point> ): Translates the Graphics View so that the specified point is in the center. | Examples: CenterView((0, 0))

description: Returns the centroid of the polygon.
search_text: Centroid | [geometry] | Centroid( <Polygon> ): Returns the centroid of the polygon. | Examples: A = (1, 4); B = (1, 1); C = (5, 1)

description: Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Characteristic_polynomial[characteristic polynomial] of the given matrix.
search_text: CharacteristicPolynomial | [vector_and_matrix] | CharacteristicPolynomial( <Matrix> ): Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Characteristic_polynomial[characteristic polynomial] of the given matrix. | Examples: CharacteristicPolynomial({{1,2},{3,4}})

description: Creates a checkbox.
search_text: Checkbox | [other] | Checkbox( ): Creates a checkbox.

description: Creates a checkbox with given caption.
search_text: Checkbox | [other] | Checkbox( <Caption> ): Creates a checkbox with given caption.

description: Creates checkbox with given caption which, when unchecked, hides listed objects.
search_text: Checkbox | [other] | Checkbox( <Caption>, <List> ): Creates checkbox with given caption which, when unchecked, hides listed objects.

description: Creates a checkbox which, when unchecked, hides listed objects.
search_text: Checkbox | [other] | Checkbox( <List> ): Creates a checkbox which, when unchecked, hides listed objects. | Examples: c = Checkbox({A,B})

description: Calculates the value of the cumulative distribution function of a Chi squared distribution at variable value v, i.e. the probability P(X ≤ v) where X is a random variable with a Chi squared distribution with the given degrees of freedom.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given x-coordinate's value, that is the area under the Chi squared distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: ChiSquared | [probability] | ChiSquared( <Degrees of Freedom>, <Variable Value> ): Calculates the value of the cumulative distribution function of a Chi squared distribution at variable value v, i.e. the probability P(X ≤ v) where X is a random variable with a Chi squared distribution with the given degrees of freedom. | Examples: ChiSquared(4, 3)

description: If Cumulative = true, calculates the value of the cumulative distribution function of a Chi squared distribution with given degrees of freedom at the given variable value, otherwise it calculates the probability density function of the distribution at variable value.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given x-coordinate's value, that is the area under the Chi squared distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: ChiSquared | [probability] | ChiSquared( <Degrees of Freedom>, <Variable Value>, <Boolean Cumulative> ): If Cumulative = true, calculates the value of the cumulative distribution function of a Chi squared distribution with given degrees of freedom at the given variable value, otherwise it calculates the probability density function of the distribution at variable value.

description: If Cumulative = true, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chi-square_distribution[Chi squared distribution] with the given degrees of freedom, othewise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given x-coordinate's value, that is the area under the Chi squared distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: ChiSquared | [probability] | ChiSquared( <Degrees of Freedom>, x, <Boolean Cumulative> ): If Cumulative = true, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chi-square_distribution[Chi squared distribution] with the given degrees of freedom, othewise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.

description: Performs a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goodness_of_fit[Goodness of Fit test] that compares the given list of observed counts against the given list of expected counts, using a specific number of degrees of freedom. Results are returned in list form as {Probability value, chi-squared test statistic}. For lists of different sizes the result is undefined.
search_text: ChiSquaredTest | [probability] | ChiSquaredTest( <List>, <List>, <Degrees of Freedom> ): Performs a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goodness_of_fit[Goodness of Fit test] that compares the given list of observed counts against the given list of expected counts, using a specific number of degrees of freedom. Results are returned in list form as {Probability value, chi-squared test statistic}. For lists of different sizes the result is undefined. | Examples: ChiSquaredTest({1, 2, 3, 4}, {3, 2, 4, 2}, 2)

description: Performs a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chi-squared_test[chi-squared test] that compares the given matrix of observed counts against the matrix of expected counts determined by the hypothesis of independence. The matrix of expected counts is calculated internally. Each expected count is found from the row and column totals of the given matrix of observed counts using the rule: (formula) Results are returned in list form as {Probability value, chi-squared test statistic}.
search_text: ChiSquaredTest | [probability] | ChiSquaredTest( <Matrix> ): Performs a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chi-squared_test[chi-squared test] that compares the given matrix of observed counts against the matrix of expected counts determined by the hypothesis of independence. The matrix of expected counts is calculated internally. Each expected count is found from the row and column totals of the given matrix of observed counts using the rule: (formula) Results are returned in list form as {Probability value, chi-squared test statistic}. | Examples: ChiSquaredTest({{1, 2, 1}, {3, 2, 3}})

description: Performs a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goodness_of_fit[Goodness of Fit test] that compares the given list of observed counts against the given list of expected counts. To compute the probability, n-1 degrees of freedom are assumed, where n is the number of elements in each list. For lists of different sizes the result is undefined. Results are returned in list form as {Probability value, chi-squared test statistic}.
search_text: ChiSquaredTest | [probability] | ChiSquaredTest( <Observed List>, <Expected List> ): Performs a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goodness_of_fit[Goodness of Fit test] that compares the given list of observed counts against the given list of expected counts. To compute the probability, n-1 degrees of freedom are assumed, where n is the number of elements in each list. For lists of different sizes the result is undefined. Results are returned in list form as {Probability value, chi-squared test statistic}. | Examples: ChiSquaredTest({1, 2, 3, 4}, {3, 2, 4, 2})

description: Performs a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chi-squared_test[chi-squared test] that compares the given matrix of observed counts against the given matrix of expected counts. To compute the probability, (formula) degrees of freedom are assumed, where r and c are the number of rows and columns in each matrix. For matrices of different size the result is undefined. Results are returned in list form as {Probability value, chi-squared test statistic}.
search_text: ChiSquaredTest | [probability] | ChiSquaredTest( <Observed Matrix>, <Expected Matrix> ): Performs a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chi-squared_test[chi-squared test] that compares the given matrix of observed counts against the given matrix of expected counts. To compute the probability, (formula) degrees of freedom are assumed, where r and c are the number of rows and columns in each matrix. For matrices of different size the result is undefined. Results are returned in list form as {Probability value, chi-squared test statistic}. | Examples: ChiSquaredTest({{1, 2, 1}, {3, 2, 3}}, {{2, 3, 2}, {4, 2, 3}})

description: Creates a circle with line as axis and through the point.
notes: See also Compass, Circle with Center through Point, Circle with Center and Radius, and Circle through 3 Points tools.

In order to avoid the ambiguity line/plane of notations in 2D and 3D, don't use equations like x = 0 or y = 0 for the Direction. For example, you want the Direction to be the plane x = 0, use an expression like x + 0y + 0z = 0 instead.
See also Circle with Axis through Point and Circle with Center, Radius and Direction tools.
search_text: Circle | [3d] | Circle( <Line>, <Point> ): Creates a circle with line as axis and through the point.

description: Yields a circle with given center through a given point.
notes: See also Compass, Circle with Center through Point, Circle with Center and Radius, and Circle through 3 Points tools.

In order to avoid the ambiguity line/plane of notations in 2D and 3D, don't use equations like x = 0 or y = 0 for the Direction. For example, you want the Direction to be the plane x = 0, use an expression like x + 0y + 0z = 0 instead.
See also Circle with Axis through Point and Circle with Center, Radius and Direction tools.
search_text: Circle | [3d] | Circle( <Point>, <Point> ): Yields a circle with given center through a given point.

description: Creates a circle with center, through a point, and axis parallel to direction.
notes: See also Compass, Circle with Center through Point, Circle with Center and Radius, and Circle through 3 Points tools.

In order to avoid the ambiguity line/plane of notations in 2D and 3D, don't use equations like x = 0 or y = 0 for the Direction. For example, you want the Direction to be the plane x = 0, use an expression like x + 0y + 0z = 0 instead.
See also Circle with Axis through Point and Circle with Center, Radius and Direction tools.
search_text: Circle | [3d] | Circle( <Point>, <Point>, <Direction> ): Creates a circle with center, through a point, and axis parallel to direction.

description: Yields a circle through the three given points (if they do not lie on the same line).
notes: See also Compass, Circle with Center through Point, Circle with Center and Radius, and Circle through 3 Points tools.

In order to avoid the ambiguity line/plane of notations in 2D and 3D, don't use equations like x = 0 or y = 0 for the Direction. For example, you want the Direction to be the plane x = 0, use an expression like x + 0y + 0z = 0 instead.
See also Circle with Axis through Point and Circle with Center, Radius and Direction tools.
search_text: Circle | [3d] | Circle( <Point>, <Point>, <Point> ): Yields a circle through the three given points (if they do not lie on the same line).

description: Yields a circle with given center and radius.
notes: See also Compass, Circle with Center through Point, Circle with Center and Radius, and Circle through 3 Points tools.

In order to avoid the ambiguity line/plane of notations in 2D and 3D, don't use equations like x = 0 or y = 0 for the Direction. For example, you want the Direction to be the plane x = 0, use an expression like x + 0y + 0z = 0 instead.
See also Circle with Axis through Point and Circle with Center, Radius and Direction tools.
search_text: Circle | [3d] | Circle( <Point>, <Radius Number> ): Yields a circle with given center and radius.

description: Creates a circle with center, radius, and axis parallel to direction, which can be a line, vector or plane.
notes: See also Compass, Circle with Center through Point, Circle with Center and Radius, and Circle through 3 Points tools.

In order to avoid the ambiguity line/plane of notations in 2D and 3D, don't use equations like x = 0 or y = 0 for the Direction. For example, you want the Direction to be the plane x = 0, use an expression like x + 0y + 0z = 0 instead.
See also Circle with Axis through Point and Circle with Center, Radius and Direction tools.
search_text: Circle | [3d] | Circle( <Point>, <Radius>, <Direction> ): Creates a circle with center, radius, and axis parallel to direction, which can be a line, vector or plane.

description: Yields a circle with given center and radius equal to the length of the given segment.
notes: See also Compass, Circle with Center through Point, Circle with Center and Radius, and Circle through 3 Points tools.

In order to avoid the ambiguity line/plane of notations in 2D and 3D, don't use equations like x = 0 or y = 0 for the Direction. For example, you want the Direction to be the plane x = 0, use an expression like x + 0y + 0z = 0 instead.
See also Circle with Axis through Point and Circle with Center, Radius and Direction tools.
search_text: Circle | [3d] | Circle( <Point>, <Segment> ): Yields a circle with given center and radius equal to the length of the given segment.

description: Creates a circular arc with midpoint between the two points.
notes: The arc length is displayed in Algebra View. Point B does not have to lie on the arc. * See also Circular Arc tool.
search_text: CircularArc | [3d] | CircularArc( <Midpoint>, <Point A>, <Point B> ): Creates a circular arc with midpoint between the two points.

description: Creates a circular sector with midpoint between the two points.
notes: The sector area is displayed in Algebra View Point B does not have to lie on the arc of the sector. * See also Circular Sector tool.
search_text: CircularSector | [3d] | CircularSector( <Midpoint>, <Point A>, <Point B> ): Creates a circular sector with midpoint between the two points.

description: Creates a circular arc through three points, where the first point is the starting point and the third point is the endpoint of the circumcircular arc.
notes: See also Circumcircular Arc tool.
search_text: CircumcircularArc | [3d] | CircumcircularArc( <Point>, <Point>, <Point> ): Creates a circular arc through three points, where the first point is the starting point and the third point is the endpoint of the circumcircular arc.

description: Creates a circular sector whose arc runs through the three points, where the first point is the starting point and the third point is the endpoint of the arc.
notes: See also Circumcircular Sector through Three Points tool.
search_text: CircumcircularSector | [3d] | CircumcircularSector( <Point>, <Point>, <Point> ): Creates a circular sector whose arc runs through the three points, where the first point is the starting point and the third point is the endpoint of the arc.

description: If the given conic is a circle or ellipse, this command returns its circumference. Otherwise the result is undefined.
notes: See also Perimeter command.
search_text: Circumference | [3d] | Circumference(Conic): If the given conic is a circle or ellipse, this command returns its circumference. Otherwise the result is undefined. | Examples: Circumference(x^2 + 2y^2 = 1)

description: Gives a list of class boundaries. The first boundary (min) is equal to the minimum of the List, the last boundary (max) will be the maximum of the List and the boundaries will be equally spaced between min and max.
notes: By convention this uses the a ≤ x < b rule for each class except for the last class which is a ≤ x ≤ b
search_text: Classes | [list] | Classes( <List of Data>, <Number of Classes> ): Gives a list of class boundaries. The first boundary (min) is equal to the minimum of the List, the last boundary (max) will be the maximum of the List and the boundaries will be equally spaced between min and max. | Examples: Classes({1, 3, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10}, 3)

description: Gives a list of class boundaries. The first boundary (min) is equal to Start, the last boundary (max) will be at least the maximum of the List and the boundaries will be equally spaced between min and max.
notes: By convention this uses the a ≤ x < b rule for each class except for the last class which is a ≤ x ≤ b
search_text: Classes | [list] | Classes( <List of Data>, <Start>, <Width of Classes> ): Gives a list of class boundaries. The first boundary (min) is equal to Start, the last boundary (max) will be at least the maximum of the List and the boundaries will be equally spaced between min and max. | Examples: Classes({0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 1.1}, 0, 1)

description: Returns a new point on the first line which is the closest to the second line.
notes: For Functions, this command now uses closest point (rather than vertical point). This works best for polynomials; for other functions the numerical algorithm is less stable.
search_text: ClosestPoint | [geometry] | ClosestPoint( <Line>, <Line> ): Returns a new point on the first line which is the closest to the second line.

description: Returns a new point on a path which is the closest to a selected point.
notes: For Functions, this command now uses closest point (rather than vertical point). This works best for polynomials; for other functions the numerical algorithm is less stable.
search_text: ClosestPoint | [geometry] | ClosestPoint( <Path>, <Point> ): Returns a new point on a path which is the closest to a selected point.

description: Returns a new point on the region which is the closest to a selected point.
search_text: ClosestPointRegion | [geometry] | ClosestPointRegion( <Region>, <Point> ): Returns a new point on the region which is the closest to a selected point.

description: Returns the list of the coefficients a, b, c, d, e, f of a conic in standard form: (formula)
notes: For non-polynomial curves obtained using one the fitting commands e.g. f(x) = FitExp(l1), the command Coefficients(f) will return the list of the calculated parameters.
For a line in implicit form l: ax + by + c = 0 it is possible to obtain the coefficients using the syntax x(l), y(l), z(l).
search_text: Coefficients | [algebra] | Coefficients( <Conic> ): Returns the list of the coefficients a, b, c, d, e, f of a conic in standard form: (formula) | Examples: l: 3x + 2y - 2 = 0; x(l); y(l)

description: Yields the list of all coefficients (formula) of the polynomial (formula).
notes: For non-polynomial curves obtained using one the fitting commands e.g. f(x) = FitExp(l1), the command Coefficients(f) will return the list of the calculated parameters.
For a line in implicit form l: ax + by + c = 0 it is possible to obtain the coefficients using the syntax x(l), y(l), z(l).
search_text: Coefficients | [algebra] | Coefficients( <Polynomial> ): Yields the list of all coefficients (formula) of the polynomial (formula). | Examples: Coefficients(x^3 - 3 x^2 + 3 x)

description: Yields the list of all coefficients of the polynomial in the main variable.
notes: For non-polynomial curves obtained using one the fitting commands e.g. f(x) = FitExp(l1), the command Coefficients(f) will return the list of the calculated parameters.
For a line in implicit form l: ax + by + c = 0 it is possible to obtain the coefficients using the syntax x(l), y(l), z(l).
search_text: Coefficients | [algebra] | Coefficients( <Polynomial> ): Yields the list of all coefficients of the polynomial in the main variable. | Examples: Coefficients(x^3 - 3 x^2 + 3 x)

description: Yields the list of all coefficients of the polynomial in the given variable.
notes: For non-polynomial curves obtained using one the fitting commands e.g. f(x) = FitExp(l1), the command Coefficients(f) will return the list of the calculated parameters.
For a line in implicit form l: ax + by + c = 0 it is possible to obtain the coefficients using the syntax x(l), y(l), z(l).
search_text: Coefficients | [algebra] | Coefficients( <Polynomial>, <Variable> ): Yields the list of all coefficients of the polynomial in the given variable. | Examples: Coefficients(a^3 - 3 a^2 + 3 a, a); Coefficients(a^3 - 3 a^2 + 3 a, x)

description: Returns the column of the cell as a number (starting at 1).

notes: Use CSolve Command instead.

description: Creates a cone with given base and height.
notes: This command yields undefined if angle ≥ (formula).
See also InfiniteCone command, the Cone tool and the Extrude to Pyramid or Cone tool, that operates by dragging or selecting a circle and entering altitude to create a right circular cone.
search_text: Cone | [3d] | Cone( <Circle>, <Height> ): Creates a cone with given base and height.

notes: This command yields undefined if angle ≥ (formula).

description: Creates an infinite cone with given point as vertex, axis of symmetry parallel to the given vector and apex angle 2α.
notes: This command yields undefined if angle ≥ (formula).

search_text: Cone | [3d] | Cone( <Point>, <Vector>, <Angle α> ): Creates an infinite cone with given point as vertex, axis of symmetry parallel to the given vector and apex angle 2α.

See also Conic through 5 Points tool and Coefficients command.
search_text: Conic | [conic] | Conic( <List> ): Returns a conic section (formula). | Examples: Conic({2, 3, -1, 4, 2, -3})

description: Returns a conic section (formula).
notes: If four of the points lie on one line, then the conic section is not defined.
See also Conic through 5 Points tool and Coefficients command.

syntax: ContingencyTable( <List of Row Values>, <List of Column Values> <Frequency Table>, <Options> )
description: Draws a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contingency_table[Contingency Table] using the given list of row values, column values and corresponding frequency table. The text Options controls the display of optional calculations within the table as described above.
notes: Possible values for Options are "|", "", "+", "e", "k", "=". "|" = show column percentages "" = show row percentages "+" = show total percentages "e" = show expected counts "k" = show Chi Squared contributions "=" = show results of a Chi Squared test
search_text: ContingencyTable | [chart] | ContingencyTable( <List of Row Values>, <List of Column Values> <Frequency Table>, <Options> ): Draws a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contingency_table[Contingency Table] using the given list of row values, column values and corresponding frequency table. The text Options controls the display of optional calculations within the table as described above. | Examples: ContingencyTable({"Males","Females"},{"Right-handed", "Left-handed"},{{43,9},{44,4}},"_")

syntax: ContingencyTable( <List of Row Values>, <List of Column Values>, <Frequency Table> )
description: Draws a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contingency_table[Contingency Table] using the given list of row values, column values and corresponding frequency table.
notes: Possible values for Options are "|", "", "+", "e", "k", "=". "|" = show column percentages "" = show row percentages "+" = show total percentages "e" = show expected counts "k" = show Chi Squared contributions "=" = show results of a Chi Squared test
search_text: ContingencyTable | [chart] | ContingencyTable( <List of Row Values>, <List of Column Values>, <Frequency Table> ): Draws a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contingency_table[Contingency Table] using the given list of row values, column values and corresponding frequency table. | Examples: ContingencyTable({"Males","Females"},{"Right-handed", "Left-handed"},{{43,9},{44,4}})

description: Draws a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contingency_table[Contingency Table] created from the two given lists. Unique values from the first list are used as row values in the table. Unique values from the second list are used as column values in the table.
notes: Possible values for Options are "|", "", "+", "e", "k", "=". "|" = show column percentages "" = show row percentages "+" = show total percentages "e" = show expected counts "k" = show Chi Squared contributions "=" = show results of a Chi Squared test
search_text: ContingencyTable | [chart] | ContingencyTable( <List of Text>, <List of Text> ): Draws a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contingency_table[Contingency Table] created from the two given lists. Unique values from the first list are used as row values in the table. Unique values from the second list are used as column values in the table.

description: Draws a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contingency_table[Contingency Table] created from the two given lists as described above. The text Options controls the display of optional calculations within the table.
notes: Possible values for Options are "|", "", "+", "e", "k", "=". "|" = show column percentages "" = show row percentages "+" = show total percentages "e" = show expected counts "k" = show Chi Squared contributions "=" = show results of a Chi Squared test
search_text: ContingencyTable | [chart] | ContingencyTable( <List of Text>, <List of Text>, <Options> ): Draws a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contingency_table[Contingency Table] created from the two given lists as described above. The text Options controls the display of optional calculations within the table.

description: Creates the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Continued_fraction[continued fraction] approximating a given number. The result is a LaTeX text object. The fraction is computed numerically within precision 10^-8^.
search_text: ContinuedFraction | [algebra] | ContinuedFraction( <Number> ): Creates the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Continued_fraction[continued fraction] approximating a given number. The result is a LaTeX text object. The fraction is computed numerically within precision 10^-8^. | Examples: ContinuedFraction(5.45)

description: Creates the continued fraction approximating the given number. If the parameter Level is specified, the number of quotients is less than or equal to Level, but never exceeding the number of quotients needed to achieve the numerical precision of 10^-8^. When Shorthand is true, the LaTeX text uses a shorter syntax, and contains a list of the integer parts of the continued fraction.
search_text: ContinuedFraction | [algebra] | ContinuedFraction( <Number>, <Level> (optional), <Boolean Shorthand> ): Creates the continued fraction approximating the given number. If the parameter Level is specified, the number of quotients is less than or equal to Level, but never exceeding the number of quotients needed to achieve the numerical precision of 10^-8^. When Shorthand is true, the LaTeX text uses a shorter syntax, and contains a list of the integer parts of the continued fraction. | Examples: ContinuedFraction(5.45, true); ContinuedFraction(5.45, 3, true)

description: Creates the continued fraction approximating the given number. The number of quotients is less than or equal to Level, but never exceeding the number of quotients needed to achieve the numerical precision of 10^-8^.
search_text: ContinuedFraction | [algebra] | ContinuedFraction( <Number>, <Level> ): Creates the continued fraction approximating the given number. The number of quotients is less than or equal to Level, but never exceeding the number of quotients needed to achieve the numerical precision of 10^-8^. | Examples: ContinuedFraction(5.45, 3)

description: Creates https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/convex_hull[convex hull] of given set of points. Returned object is a locus, so it is auxiliary.
search_text: ConvexHull | [discrete_math] | ConvexHull( <List of Points> ): Creates https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/convex_hull[convex hull] of given set of points. Returned object is a locus, so it is auxiliary.

description: Creates a free copy of the object. Preserves all basic Object Properties and copy of Auxiliary Object is auxiliary as well.
search_text: CopyFreeObject | [other] | CopyFreeObject( <Object> ): Creates a free copy of the object. Preserves all basic Object Properties and copy of Auxiliary Object is auxiliary as well.

description: Creates a point at the corner of Graphics View (1, 2) which is never visible in that view. Supported values of number n are 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 as above.
notes: Corner( <Number of Corner> ) won't work inside other commands. Instead create eg C_1 = Corner(1) and use that.
Corner( <Graphics View>, <Number of Corner> ) won't work inside other commands. Instead create eg C_1 = Corner(1, 1) and use that.

Use -1 for the 3D Graphics View's corners (available values for Number: from 1 to 8). Moreover: Corner(-1,9) returns point (w, h, 0), where w and h are width and height of the 3D Graphics View in pixels Corner(-1,10) returns point (w, h, 0), where w and h are width and height of the main window in pixels Corner(-1,11) returns view direction (for parallel projections) or eye position (for e.g. perspective projection) Corner(-1,12) returns screen left-to-right direction * Corner(-1,13) returns scales for x, y and z axes.

Corner( <Text>, <Number of Corner> ) won't work inside the Sequence or Zip commands. Also the Absolute Position on Screen property must be unchecked The numbering of the corners is counter‐clockwise and starts at the lower left corner.
search_text: Corner | [geogebra_general] | Corner( <Graphics View>, <Number of Corner> ): Creates a point at the corner of Graphics View (1, 2) which is never visible in that view. Supported values of number n are 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 as above.

description: Counts the number of elements in the list satisfying the condition.
notes: For list of numbers arbitrary condition may be used. For list of other objects one can use only conditions of the form x==constant or x!=constant.

description: As above, using a more flexible syntax.
notes: For list of numbers arbitrary condition may be used. For list of other objects one can use only conditions of the form x==constant or x!=constant.
search_text: CountIf | [list] | CountIf( <Condition>, <Variable>, <List> ): As above, using a more flexible syntax. | Examples: CountIf(x(A) < 3, A, {P, Q, R}); CountIf(x(A) < 3, A, {(0, 1), (4, 2), (2, 2)})
