description: Calculates the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross_product[cross product] of u and v. Instead of vectors you can also use lists. For 2D vectors or points the result is the z-coordinate of the actual cross product.
notes: If a vector in the CAS View contains undefined variables, the command yields a formula for the cross product, e.g. Cross((a, b, c), (d, e, f)) yields (b f - c e, -a f + c d, a e - b d).
You can also use the operator u ⊗ v + See also Dot Command.
search_text: Cross | [vector_and_matrix] | Cross( <Vector u> , <Vector v> ): Calculates the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross_product[cross product] of u and v. Instead of vectors you can also use lists. For 2D vectors or points the result is the z-coordinate of the actual cross product. | Examples: Cross((1, 3, 2), (0, 3, -2)); Cross({1, 1, 1}, {-1, -1, -1}); Cross((1,2),(4,5))

description: Calculates the cross ratio λ of four collinear points A, B, C and D, where: λ = AffineRatio[B, C, D] / AffineRatio[A, C, D].
search_text: CrossRatio | [geometry] | CrossRatio( <Point A>, <Point B>, <Point C>, <Point D> ): Calculates the cross ratio λ of four collinear points A, B, C and D, where: λ = AffineRatio[B, C, D] / AffineRatio[A, C, D]. | Examples: CrossRatio((-1, 1), (1, 1), (3, 1), (4, 1))

description: Creates a cube with two (adjacent) points of the first face, and the third point automatically created on a circle, so that the cube can rotate around its first edge.
notes: `Cube(A, B)` is a shortcut for `Cube(A, B, C)` with `C = Point(Circle(B, Distance(A, B), Segment(A, B)))`.
See also Tetrahedron, Octahedron, Icosahedron, Dodecahedron commands.
search_text: Cube | [3d] | Cube( <Point>, <Point>): Creates a cube with two (adjacent) points of the first face, and the third point automatically created on a circle, so that the cube can rotate around its first edge.

description: Creates a cube having the segment between the two points as an edge. The other vertices are uniquely determined by the given direction, that should be one of: a vector, a segment, a line, a ray orthogonal to the segment, or a polygon, a plane parallel to the segment. + The created cube will have: a face with the segment as an edge in a plane orthogonal to the given vector/segment/line/ray, or a face with the segment as an edge in a plane parallel to the polygon/plane.
notes: `Cube(A, B)` is a shortcut for `Cube(A, B, C)` with `C = Point(Circle(B, Distance(A, B), Segment(A, B)))`.
See also Tetrahedron, Octahedron, Icosahedron, Dodecahedron commands.
search_text: Cube | [3d] | Cube( <Point>, <Point>, <Direction> ): Creates a cube having the segment between the two points as an edge. The other vertices are uniquely determined by the given direction, that should be one of: a vector, a segment, a line, a ray orthogonal to the segment, or a polygon, a plane parallel to the segment. + The created cube will have: a face with the segment as an edge in a plane orthogonal to the given vector/segment/line/ray, or a face with the segment as an edge in a plane parallel to the polygon/plane.

description: Creates a cube with three (adjacent) points of the first face. The points have to start a square for the cube to be defined.
notes: `Cube(A, B)` is a shortcut for `Cube(A, B, C)` with `C = Point(Circle(B, Distance(A, B), Segment(A, B)))`.
See also Tetrahedron, Octahedron, Icosahedron, Dodecahedron commands.
search_text: Cube | [3d] | Cube( <Point>, <Point>, <Point>): Creates a cube with three (adjacent) points of the first face. The points have to start a square for the cube to be defined.

description: Creates a cube having as base the given square.
notes: `Cube(A, B)` is a shortcut for `Cube(A, B, C)` with `C = Point(Circle(B, Distance(A, B), Segment(A, B)))`.
See also Tetrahedron, Octahedron, Icosahedron, Dodecahedron commands.
search_text: Cube | [3d] | Cube( <Square> ): Creates a cube having as base the given square.

description: Gives n-th https://bernard-gibert.pagesperso-orange.fr/ctc.html[triangle cubic] of the given triangle ABC.
notes: This command is in development, set of supported index n is changing.
search_text: Cubic | [functions_and_calculus] | Cubic( <Point>, <Point>, <Point>, <Number> ): Gives n-th https://bernard-gibert.pagesperso-orange.fr/ctc.html[triangle cubic] of the given triangle ABC. | Examples: Cubic(A, B, C, 2)

description: Yields the curvature of the object (function, curve, conic) at the given point.
search_text: Curvature | [conic] | Curvature( <Point>, <Object> ): Yields the curvature of the object (function, curve, conic) at the given point. | Examples: Curvature((0 ,0), x^2); Curvature((0, 0), Curve(cos(t), sin(2t), t, 0, π)); Curvature((-1, 0), Conic({1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3}))

description: Yields the curvature vector of the object (function, curve, conic) in the given point.
search_text: CurvatureVector | [functions_and_calculus] | CurvatureVector( <Point>, <Object> ): Yields the curvature vector of the object (function, curve, conic) in the given point. | Examples: CurvatureVector((0, 0), x^2); CurvatureVector((0, 0), Curve(cos(t), sin(2t), t, 0, π)); CurvatureVector((-1, 0), Conic({1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3}))

description: Yields the 3D Cartesian parametric curve for the given x-expression (first <Expression>), y-expression (second <Expression>) and z-expression (third <Expression>) (using parameter variable) within the given interval [Start Value, End Value].
notes: End Value must be greater than or equal to Start Value and both must be finite. x, y and z are not allowed as parameter variables. * See Curves for details, also see the Derivative Command and the Parametric Derivative Command.
search_text: Curve | [3d] | Curve( <Expression> , <Expression> , <Expression> , <Parameter Variable> , <Start Value> , <End Value> ): Yields the 3D Cartesian parametric curve for the given x-expression (first <Expression>), y-expression (second <Expression>) and z-expression (third <Expression>) (using parameter variable) within the given interval [Start Value, End Value]. | Examples: Curve(cos(t), sin(t), t, t, 0, 10π)

description: Yields the Cartesian parametric curve for the given x-expression (first <Expression>) and y-expression (second <Expression>) (using parameter variable) within the given interval [Start Value, End Value].
notes: End Value must be greater than or equal to Start Value and both must be finite. x, y and z are not allowed as parameter variables. * See Curves for details, also see the Derivative Command and the Parametric Derivative Command.
search_text: Curve | [3d] | Curve( <Expression>, <Expression>, <Parameter Variable>, <Start Value>, <End Value> ): Yields the Cartesian parametric curve for the given x-expression (first <Expression>) and y-expression (second <Expression>) (using parameter variable) within the given interval [Start Value, End Value]. | Examples: Curve(2 cos(t), 2 sin(t), t, 0, 2π)

description: Creates a cylinder with given base and given height.
notes: See also the InfiniteCylinder command and the Cylinder and Extrude to Prism or Cylinder tools.
search_text: Cylinder | [3d] | Cylinder( <Circle>, <Height> ): Creates a cylinder with given base and given height.

description: Creates a cylinder with given radius and with given points as the centers of the top and bottom.
notes: See also the InfiniteCylinder command and the Cylinder and Extrude to Prism or Cylinder tools.
search_text: Cylinder | [3d] | Cylinder( <Point>, <Point>, <Radius> ): Creates a cylinder with given radius and with given points as the centers of the top and bottom.

description: Yields a function that connects points (x~1~, y~1~), (x~2~, y~2~),...,(x~n~, y~n~) where {x~1~, ..., x~n~}, {y~1~, ..., y~n~} are the input lists. In between these points linear interpolation is used. This command is used by Sensors.
search_text: DataFunction | [functions_and_calculus] | DataFunction( <List of Numbers>, <List of Numbers> ): Yields a function that connects points (x~1~, y~1~), (x~2~, y~2~),...,(x~n~, y~n~) where {x~1~, ..., x~n~}, {y~1~, ..., y~n~} are the input lists. In between these points linear interpolation is used. This command is used by Sensors. | Examples: DataFunction({0, 1, 2, 4}, {0, 1, 4, 16})

description: Gives the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/degree_of_a_polynomial[degree of a polynomial] (in the main variable).

description: Gives the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/degree_of_a_polynomial[degree of a polynomial] (in the main variable or https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monomial[monomial]).
search_text: Degree | [algebra] | Degree( <Polynomial> ): Gives the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/degree_of_a_polynomial[degree of a polynomial] (in the main variable or https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monomial[monomial]). | Examples: Degree(x^4 + 2 x^2); Degree(x^6 y^3 + 2 x^2 y^3)

description: Gives the degree of a polynomial in the given variable.
search_text: Degree | [algebra] | Degree( <Polynomial>, <Variable> ): Gives the degree of a polynomial in the given variable. | Examples: Degree(x^4 y^3 + 2 x^2 y^3, x); Degree(x^4 y^3 + 2 x^2 y^3, y)

description: Creates a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delaunay_Triangulation[Delaunay Triangulation] of the list of points. Returned object is a locus, so it is auxiliary.
search_text: DelaunayTriangulation | [discrete_math] | DelaunayTriangulation( <List of Points> ): Creates a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delaunay_Triangulation[Delaunay Triangulation] of the list of points. Returned object is a locus, so it is auxiliary.

description: Deletes the object and all its dependent objects.
notes: See also Delete tool.
search_text: Delete | [cas] | Delete( <Object> ): Deletes the object and all its dependent objects. | Examples: Delete(sli)

description: Deletes the object and all its dependent objects in GeoGebra and removes any value assigned to the object in the CAS View.
notes: See also Delete tool.
search_text: Delete | [cas] | Delete( <Object> ): Deletes the object and all its dependent objects in GeoGebra and removes any value assigned to the object in the CAS View. | Examples: Delete(sli)

description: Returns the denominator of a rational number or expression.
notes: See also Numerator Command and FractionText Command.
search_text: Denominator | [algebra] | Denominator( <Expression> ): Returns the denominator of a rational number or expression. | Examples: Denominator(2 / 3 + 1 / 15)

description: Returns the denominator of a function.
notes: See also Numerator Command and FractionText Command.
search_text: Denominator | [algebra] | Denominator( <Function> ): Returns the denominator of a function. | Examples: Denominator(5 / (x^2 + 2))

description: For a rational number returns its (simplified) denominator. It uses a numerical method, which limits this command to numbers with small denominator. For irrational input the denominator of its continued fraction is returned.
notes: See also Numerator Command and FractionText Command.
search_text: Denominator | [algebra] | Denominator( <Number> ): For a rational number returns its (simplified) denominator. It uses a numerical method, which limits this command to numbers with small denominator. For irrational input the denominator of its continued fraction is returned. | Examples: Denominator(5 / 3); Denominator(10 / 6); Denominator(15 / 3)

description: Returns the derivative of the curve.
notes: This only works for parametric curves.
This only works for parametric curves.
You can use f'(x) instead of Derivative(f), or f''(x) instead of Derivative(f, 2), and so on.

description: Returns derivative of an expression with respect to the main variable.

notes: This only works for parametric curves.

description: Gives the determinant of the matrix.
search_text: Determinant | [vector_and_matrix] | Determinant( <Matrix> ): Gives the determinant of the matrix. | Examples: Determinant({{1, 2}, {3, 4}})

description: Gives the determinant of the matrix. If the matrix contains undefined variables, it yields a formula for the determinant.
search_text: Determinant | [vector_and_matrix] | Determinant( <Matrix> ): Gives the determinant of the matrix. If the matrix contains undefined variables, it yields a formula for the determinant. | Examples: Determinant({{1, a}, {b, 4}})

description: Finds the difference of the two polygons.
notes: Works only for where the polygons are not self-intersecting, and where the resulting pieces don't have holes.
search_text: Difference | [geometry] | Difference( <Polygon>, <Polygon> ): Finds the difference of the two polygons.

description: Dilates the object from a point of origin using the given factor.
notes: This command differs among variants of English: Dilate (US) Enlarge (UK + Aus)
When dilating polygons, GeoGebra creates also all the transformed vertices and segments.
See also Dilate from Point by Factor tool.
search_text: Dilate | [transformation] | Dilate( <Object>, <Dilation Factor> ): Dilates the object from a point of origin using the given factor.

description: Dilates the object from a point, which is the dilation center point, using the given factor.
notes: This command differs among variants of English: Dilate (US) Enlarge (UK + Aus)
When dilating polygons, GeoGebra creates also all the transformed vertices and segments.
See also Dilate from Point by Factor tool.
search_text: Dilate | [transformation] | Dilate( <Object>, <Dilation Factor>, <Dilation Center Point> ): Dilates the object from a point, which is the dilation center point, using the given factor.

description: Gives the dimension of a vector or a matrix.
search_text: Dimension | [vector_and_matrix] | Dimension( <Object> ): Gives the dimension of a vector or a matrix. | Examples: Dimension({1, 2, 0, -4, 3}); Dimension({{1, 2}, {3, 4}, {5, 6}})

description: Gives the dimension of a vector or matrix.
search_text: Dimension | [vector_and_matrix] | Dimension( <Object> ): Gives the dimension of a vector or matrix. | Examples: Dimension({1, 2, 0, -4, 3}); Dimension({{a, b}, {c, d}, {e, f}})

description: Yields the direction vector of the line.
notes: A line with equation ax + by = c has the direction vector (b, - a).
search_text: Direction | [geometry] | Direction( <Line> ): Yields the direction vector of the line. | Examples: Direction(-2x + 3y + 1 = 0)

description: Yields the directrix of the conic.
notes: See also the Focus command.
search_text: Directrix | [conic] | Directrix( <Conic> ): Yields the directrix of the conic. | Examples: Directrix(x^2 - 3x + 3y = 9)

description: Yields the distance between two lines.
notes: The command works for points, segments, lines, conics, functions, and implicit curves. For functions, it uses a numerical algorithm which works better for polynomials.
The distance between intersecting lines is 0. Thus, this command is only interesting for parallel lines.
The distance between intersecting planes is 0. Thus, this command is only meaningful for parallel planes.
See also Distance or Length tool .
search_text: Distance | [3d] | Distance( <Line>, <Line> ): Yields the distance between two lines. | Examples: Distance(y = x + 3, y = x + 1); Distance(y = 3x + 1, y = x + 1); Distance(a, b)

description: Yields the distance between the two planes.
notes: The command works for points, segments, lines, conics, functions, and implicit curves. For functions, it uses a numerical algorithm which works better for polynomials.
The distance between intersecting lines is 0. Thus, this command is only interesting for parallel lines.
The distance between intersecting planes is 0. Thus, this command is only meaningful for parallel planes.
See also Distance or Length tool .
search_text: Distance | [3d] | Distance( <Plane>, <Plane> ): Yields the distance between the two planes. | Examples: Distance(eq1, eq2)

description: Yields the shortest distance between a point and an object.
notes: The command works for points, segments, lines, conics, functions, and implicit curves. For functions, it uses a numerical algorithm which works better for polynomials.
The distance between intersecting lines is 0. Thus, this command is only interesting for parallel lines.
The distance between intersecting planes is 0. Thus, this command is only meaningful for parallel planes.
See also Distance or Length tool .
search_text: Distance | [3d] | Distance( <Point>, <Object> ): Yields the shortest distance between a point and an object. | Examples: Distance((2, 1), x^2 + (y - 1)^2 = 1); Distance((2, 1, 2), (1, 3, 0)); Distance(A, f)

description: Returns the quotient (integer part of the result) of the two numbers.
search_text: Div | [algebra] | Div( <Dividend Number>, <Divisor Number> ): Returns the quotient (integer part of the result) of the two numbers. | Examples: Div(16, 3)

description: Returns the quotient of the two polynomials.
search_text: Div | [algebra] | Div( <Dividend Polynomial>, <Divisor Polynomial> ): Returns the quotient of the two polynomials. | Examples: Div(x^2 + 3 x + 1, x - 1)

description: Gives the quotient (integer part of the result) and the remainder of the division of the two numbers.
notes: In the Algebra View only one variable can be used and it will always be renamed to x. In the CAS View multivariable division is also supported.
search_text: Division | [algebra] | Division( <Dividend Number>, <Divisor Number> ): Gives the quotient (integer part of the result) and the remainder of the division of the two numbers. | Examples: Division(16, 3)

description: Gives the quotient and the remainder of the division of the two polynomials.
notes: In the Algebra View only one variable can be used and it will always be renamed to x. In the CAS View multivariable division is also supported.
search_text: Division | [algebra] | Division( <Dividend Polynomial>, <Divisor Polynomial> ): Gives the quotient and the remainder of the division of the two polynomials. | Examples: Division(x^2 + 3 x + 1, x - 1); Division(x^2+y^2, x+y); Division(x^2+y^2, y+x)

description: Calculates the number of all the positive divisors, including the number itself.

description: Calculates the sum of all the positive divisors, including the number itself.
notes: See also Divisors Command and DivisorsList Command.
search_text: DivisorsSum | [algebra] | DivisorsSum( <Number> ): Calculates the sum of all the positive divisors, including the number itself. | Examples: DivisorsSum(15)

description: Creates a dodecahedron with two (adjacent) points of the first face, and the third point automatically created on a circle, so that the dodecahedron can rotate around its first edge.

See also Cube, Tetrahedron, Icosahedron, Octahedron commands.
search_text: Dodecahedron | [3d] | Dodecahedron( <Point>, <Point>): Creates a dodecahedron with two (adjacent) points of the first face, and the third point automatically created on a circle, so that the dodecahedron can rotate around its first edge.

description: Creates a dodecahedron having the segment between two points as an edge. The other vertices are univocally determined by the given direction, that needs to be: a vector, a segment, a line, a ray orthogonal to the segment, or a polygon, a plane parallel to the segment. + The created dodecahedron will have: a face with the segment as an edge in a plane orthogonal to the given vector/segment/line/ray, or a face with the segment as an edge in a plane parallel to the polygon/plane.
notes: `Dodecahedron(A, B)` is a shortcut for `Dodecahedron(A, B, C)` with C = Point(Circle(((1 - sqrt(5)) A + (3 + sqrt(5)) B) / 4, Distance(A, B) sqrt(10 + 2sqrt(5)) / 4, Segment(A, B))).
See also Cube, Tetrahedron, Icosahedron, Octahedron commands.
search_text: Dodecahedron | [3d] | Dodecahedron( <Point>, <Point>, <Direction> ): Creates a dodecahedron having the segment between two points as an edge. The other vertices are univocally determined by the given direction, that needs to be: a vector, a segment, a line, a ray orthogonal to the segment, or a polygon, a plane parallel to the segment. + The created dodecahedron will have: a face with the segment as an edge in a plane orthogonal to the given vector/segment/line/ray, or a face with the segment as an edge in a plane parallel to the polygon/plane.

description: Creates a dodecahedron with three (adjacent) points of the first face. The points have to start a regular pentagon for the dodecahedron to be defined.
notes: `Dodecahedron(A, B)` is a shortcut for `Dodecahedron(A, B, C)` with C = Point(Circle(((1 - sqrt(5)) A + (3 + sqrt(5)) B) / 4, Distance(A, B) sqrt(10 + 2sqrt(5)) / 4, Segment(A, B))).

notes: `Dodecahedron(A, B)` is a shortcut for `Dodecahedron(A, B, C)` with C = Point(Circle(((1 - sqrt(5)) A + (3 + sqrt(5)) B) / 4, Distance(A, B) sqrt(10 + 2sqrt(5)) / 4, Segment(A, B))).

description: Returns the dot product (scalar product) of the two vectors.
notes: See also Cross Command.
search_text: Dot | [vector_and_matrix] | Dot( <Vector>, <Vector> ): Returns the dot product (scalar product) of the two vectors. | Examples: Dot((1, 3, 2), (0, 3, -2))

description: Returns a dot plot for the given list of numbers, as well as the list of the dot plot points. If a number n appears in the list of raw data k times, the returned list contains points (n, 1), (n, 2), ..., (n, k).
notes: If you use a list of text the DotPlot command will put the result in alphabetical order. (e.g. Blue appears two times, Red three times and B comes before R in the alphabet, so you get (1, 1), (1, 2) for Blue and (2, 1), (2, 2), (2, 3) for Red.)
search_text: DotPlot | [chart] | DotPlot( <List of Raw Data> ): Returns a dot plot for the given list of numbers, as well as the list of the dot plot points. If a number n appears in the list of raw data k times, the returned list contains points (n, 1), (n, 2), ..., (n, k). | Examples: DotPlot({2, 5, 3, 4, 3, 5, 3})

syntax: DotPlot( <List of Raw Data>, <Stack Adjacent Dots (optional)>, <Scale Factor (optional)> )
description: Returns a dot plot for the given list of data, as well as the list of the dot plot points. If a data n appears in the list of raw data k times, the returned list contains points (n, 1), (n, 2),..., (n, k). If you choose a Scale Factor s, the returned list contains points (n, 1s), (n, 2s), ..., (n, ks). Stack Adjacent Dots means a Boolean Value (true or false): If you choose true, points (which are close to each other) are stacked. If you choose false, the result will be the same as without <Stack Adjacent Dots (optional)>. The command DotPlot will also work with a list of text.
notes: If you use a list of text the DotPlot command will put the result in alphabetical order. (e.g. Blue appears two times, Red three times and B comes before R in the alphabet, so you get (1, 1), (1, 2) for Blue and (2, 1), (2, 2), (2, 3) for Red.)
search_text: DotPlot | [chart] | DotPlot( <List of Raw Data>, <Stack Adjacent Dots (optional)>, <Scale Factor (optional)> ): Returns a dot plot for the given list of data, as well as the list of the dot plot points. If a data n appears in the list of raw data k times, the returned list contains points (n, 1), (n, 2),..., (n, k). If you choose a Scale Factor s, the returned list contains points (n, 1s), (n, 2s), ..., (n, ks). Stack Adjacent Dots means a Boolean Value (true or false): If you choose true, points (which are close to each other) are stacked. If you choose false, the result will be the same as without <Stack Adjacent Dots (optional)>. The command DotPlot will also work with a list of text. | Examples: DotPlot({"Red", "Red", "Red", "Blue", "Blue"})

description: Creates a new point with given coordinates: this point is dependent, but can be moved. Whenever you try to move the new point to coordinates (x, y), the given point is moved there and coordinates for the new point are calculated. Works best if the given point is not visible and dragging is done with the mouse. At least one of the given coordinates should depend on the given point. PointIn(y < sin(x)) is the easier solution in this case. ==== The following examples show other ways to restrain the positions of a point C: Let A = Point(xAxis) and B = Point(xAxis). Now type in the Input Bar: DynamicCoordinates(B, Min(x(B), x(A)), 0) and press [.kcode]#Enter# SetVisibleInView(B, 1, false) and press [.kcode]#Enter# SetLayer(C, 1) and press [.kcode]#Enter# Now, C cannot be moved to the right of A. Define A=(1, 2). Now, type in the Input Bar: SetVisibleInView(A, 1, false) and press [.kcode]#Enter# B = DynamicCoordinates(A, If(x(A) > 3, 3, If(x(A) < -3, -3, If(x(A) < 0, round(x(A)), x(A)))), If(x(A) < 0, 0.5, If(y(A) > 2, 2, If(y(A) < 0, 0, y(A))))) and press [.kcode]#Enter# * This example makes A a sticky point when a point C is dragged near it. Define A = (1, 2) and B = (2, 3). Now, type in the Input Bar: SetVisibleInView(B, 1, false) and press [.kcode]#Enter# C = DynamicCoordinates(B, If(Distance(A, B) < 1, x(A), x(B)), If(Distance(A, B) < 1, y(A), y(B))). ====
search_text: DynamicCoordinates | [geogebra_general] | DynamicCoordinates( <Point>, <x-Coordinate>, <y-Coordinate> ): Creates a new point with given coordinates: this point is dependent, but can be moved. Whenever you try to move the new point to coordinates (x, y), the given point is moved there and coordinates for the new point are calculated. Works best if the given point is not visible and dragging is done with the mouse. At least one of the given coordinates should depend on the given point. PointIn(y < sin(x)) is the easier solution in this case. ==== The following examples show other ways to restrain the positions of a point C: Let A = Point(xAxis) and B = Point(xAxis). Now type in the Input Bar: DynamicCoordinates(B, Min(x(B), x(A)), 0) and press [.kcode]#Enter# SetVisibleInView(B, 1, false) and press [.kcode]#Enter# SetLayer(C, 1) and press [.kcode]#Enter# Now, C cannot be moved to the right of A. Define A=(1, 2). Now, type in the Input Bar: SetVisibleInView(A, 1, false) and press [.kcode]#Enter# B = DynamicCoordinates(A, If(x(A) > 3, 3, If(x(A) < -3, -3, If(x(A) < 0, round(x(A)), x(A)))), If(x(A) < 0, 0.5, If(y(A) > 2, 2, If(y(A) < 0, 0, y(A))))) and press [.kcode]#Enter# * This example makes A a sticky point when a point C is dragged near it. Define A = (1, 2) and B = (2, 3). Now, type in the Input Bar: SetVisibleInView(B, 1, false) and press [.kcode]#Enter# C = DynamicCoordinates(B, If(Distance(A, B) < 1, x(A), x(B)), If(Distance(A, B) < 1, y(A), y(B))). ==== | Examples: B = DynamicCoordinates(A, round(x(A)), round(y(A))); B = DynamicCoordinates(A, x(A), min(y(A), sin(x(A))))

description: Creates an ellipse with two focal points passing through a given point.

description: Creates the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Envelope_(mathematics)[envelope] equation of a set of output paths while the moving point is bound to another object. An envelope is a curve that is tangent to each member of the family of the output paths at some point.
notes: See also Locus, LocusEquation commands and https://github.com/kovzol/gg-art-doc/tree/master/pdf/english.pdf[GeoGebra Automated Reasoning Tools: A Tutorial].

An envelope is a curve that is tangent to each member of the family of the output paths at some point.

description: Calculates the value of cumulative distribution function of an https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Erlang_distribution[Erlang distribution] at variable value v, i.e. the probability P(X ≤ v) where X is a random variable with an Erlang distribution defined by the parameters shape and rate.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given value, that is the area under the Erlang distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: Erlang | [probability] | Erlang( <Shape>, <Rate>, <Variable Value> ): Calculates the value of cumulative distribution function of an https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Erlang_distribution[Erlang distribution] at variable value v, i.e. the probability P(X ≤ v) where X is a random variable with an Erlang distribution defined by the parameters shape and rate.

description: If Cumulative = true, calculates the value of the cumulative distribution function of an Erlang distribution with given shape and rate at the given variable value, otherwise it calculates the probability density function of the distribution at variable value.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given value, that is the area under the Erlang distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: Erlang | [probability] | Erlang( <Shape>, <Rate>, <Variable Value>, <Boolean Cumulative> ): If Cumulative = true, calculates the value of the cumulative distribution function of an Erlang distribution with given shape and rate at the given variable value, otherwise it calculates the probability density function of the distribution at variable value.

description: If Cumulative = true, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of an Erlang distribution with given shape and rate, othewise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given value, that is the area under the Erlang distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: Erlang | [probability] | Erlang( <Shape>, <Rate>, x, <Boolean Cumulative> ): If Cumulative = true, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of an Erlang distribution with given shape and rate, othewise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.

description: Executes a list of commands entered as texts.
notes: The Execute command works only if the commands in the list are written in English (US), regardless the language chosen for your GeoGebra interface.
If the quote symbol (") is not available in your keyboard, use the virtual keyboard of GeoGebra or the command UnicodeToLetter(34)
search_text: Execute | [other] | Execute( <List of Texts> ): Executes a list of commands entered as texts. | Examples: Execute({"A=(1,1)","B=(3,3)","C = Midpoint(A, B)"}); Execute(Join({"f_{1} = 1", "f_{2} = 1"}, Sequence("f_{"+(i + 2) + "} = f_{" + (i+1) + "} + f_{"+ i +"}", i, 1, 10)))

description: Use the placeholders %1, %2 and so on as arguments of the commands in the list. The placeholders will be respectively replaced with the parameters used in the Execute command. Up to 9 parameters can be specified. After the replacement, the resulting scripts will be executed.
notes: The Execute command works only if the commands in the list are written in English (US), regardless the language chosen for your GeoGebra interface.
If the quote symbol (") is not available in your keyboard, use the virtual keyboard of GeoGebra or the command UnicodeToLetter(34)
search_text: Execute | [other] | Execute( <List of Texts>, <Parameter>, ... , <Parameter> ): Use the placeholders %1, %2 and so on as arguments of the commands in the list. The placeholders will be respectively replaced with the parameters used in the Execute command. Up to 9 parameters can be specified. After the replacement, the resulting scripts will be executed. | Examples: Execute({"Segment(%1,%2)","Midpoint(%1,%2)"}, A, B)

description: Exports an image of the currently active view (or the view specified by the "view" parameter)
notes: The Properties used in the command syntax, listed in the following examples, need to be entered in English. The values related to the View that will be saved as image are: 1 for Graphics View 2 for Graphics View 2 * -1 for 3__D View__

In GeoGebra Classic 5 if there is no filename parameter the image will be copied to the Clipboard. In the Chrome browser you can specify "clipboard", true To crop an export, make the Points Export_1 and Export_2 to define the rectangle to crop (These also crop the exports from the menus eg File -> Export Image) Some syntaxes aren't supported in GeoGebra 5 Classic In Chrome, you can try the experimental syntax "type", "webm" to get a faster & smaller export than with animated GIFs For the 2D Graphics Views you can try this syntax to get a monochrome export "grayscale", true (PNG format only) For the 3D Graphics View, only bitmaps (eg png, gif) are supported. In GeoGebra Classic 5 you can try this to get a high-resolution output ExportImage("filename", "c:\Users\<username>\AppData\image.png", "view", -1, "width", 2000). Change <username> to your Windows username
search_text: ExportImage | [other] | ExportImage( <Property>, <Value>, <Property>, <Value>, ... ): Exports an image of the currently active view (or the view specified by the "view" parameter) | Examples: ExportImage("scale", 5); ExportImage("filename", "image.png"); ExportImage("filename", "image.png", "view", 2)

description: Returns a list containing the integer coefficients (formula) of Bézout's identity (formula) and the greatest common divisor of the given integers (formula) and (formula). Results are calculated by applying the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Extended_Euclidean_algorithm[Extended Euclidean algorithm].
notes: The GCD of two polynomials is not unique (it's unique up to a scalar multiple). See also GCD Command.
search_text: ExtendedGCD | [algebra] | ExtendedGCD( <Integer>,<Integer> ): Returns a list containing the integer coefficients (formula) of Bézout's identity (formula) and the greatest common divisor of the given integers (formula) and (formula). Results are calculated by applying the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Extended_Euclidean_algorithm[Extended Euclidean algorithm]. | Examples: ExtendedGCD(240,46)

description: Returns a list containing the polynomial coefficients (formula) of Bézout's identity for polynomials (formula) and the greatest common divisor of the given polynomials (formula) and (formula). Results are calculated by applying the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Extended_Euclidean_algorithm[Extended Euclidean algorithm].
notes: The GCD of two polynomials is not unique (it's unique up to a scalar multiple). See also GCD Command.
search_text: ExtendedGCD | [algebra] | ExtendedGCD( <Polynomial>, <Polynomial> ): Returns a list containing the polynomial coefficients (formula) of Bézout's identity for polynomials (formula) and the greatest common divisor of the given polynomials (formula) and (formula). Results are calculated by applying the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Extended_Euclidean_algorithm[Extended Euclidean algorithm]. | Examples: ExtendedGCD(x^2-1,x+4)

description: Will attempt to return all local extrema of the function (which should be continuous and differentiable)

description: Factors an expression with respect to a given variable.
notes: This command differs among variants of English: Factor (US) Factorise (UK + Aus)
This command needs to load the Computer Algebra System, so can be slow on some computers.

This command factors expressions over the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rational_number[Rational Numbers]. To factor over irrational real numbers, see the IFactor Command. To factor over complex numbers, see the CFactor Command and CIFactor Command.
search_text: Factor | [algebra] | Factor( <Expression>, <Variable> ): Factors an expression with respect to a given variable. | Examples: Factor(x^2 - y^2, x); Factor(x^2 - y^2, y)

notes: This command differs among variants of English: Factor (US) Factorise (UK + Aus)
This command needs to load the Computer Algebra System, so can be slow on some computers.

This command factors expressions over the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rational_number[Rational Numbers]. To factor over irrational real numbers, see the IFactor Command. To factor over complex numbers, see the CFactor Command and CIFactor Command.
search_text: Factor | [algebra] | Factor( <Number> ): Expresses a number in its prime factorization | Examples: Factor(360)

description: Factors the polynomial.
notes: This command differs among variants of English: Factor (US) Factorise (UK + Aus)
This command needs to load the Computer Algebra System, so can be slow on some computers.

This command factors expressions over the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rational_number[Rational Numbers]. To factor over irrational real numbers, see the IFactor Command. To factor over complex numbers, see the CFactor Command and CIFactor Command.

description: Gives matrix of the type (formula) such that the product of all these primes raised to the power of the corresponding exponents equals the given number. The primes are sorted in ascending order.
notes: Not all of the factors are irreducible over the reals.
See also PrimeFactors Command and Factor Command.
In the CAS View undefined variables can be used as input and the results are returned as proper matrices.
search_text: Factors | [algebra] | Factors( <Number> ): Gives matrix of the type (formula) such that the product of all these primes raised to the power of the corresponding exponents equals the given number. The primes are sorted in ascending order. | Examples: Factors(1024); Factors(42); Factors(a^8 - 1)

description: Gives a list of lists of the type {factor, exponent} such that the product of all these factors raised to the power of the corresponding exponents equals the given polynomial. The factors are sorted by degree in ascending order.
notes: Not all of the factors are irreducible over the reals.
See also PrimeFactors Command and Factor Command.
In the CAS View undefined variables can be used as input and the results are returned as proper matrices.
search_text: Factors | [algebra] | Factors( <Polynomial> ): Gives a list of lists of the type {factor, exponent} such that the product of all these factors raised to the power of the corresponding exponents equals the given polynomial. The factors are sorted by degree in ascending order. | Examples: Factors(x^8 - 1)

description: Copies values from the list to the first cells on the right of the given cell. Resulting cells are ([Free, Dependent and Auxiliary Objects|free objects)], i.e. independent of the list.
notes: CellRange has to be entered like this: e.g.: B2:D5. Object can be anything, e.g.: 3, RandomBetween(0, 10), Circle(A, B). * Cells are labelled by column and row, e.g.: B2.
See also FillRow and FillColumn commands.
You can use FillCell(cell, Transpose({list})) to fill vertically. The extra braces convert the list into a matrix thus {list}
search_text: FillCells | [spreadsheet] | FillCells( <Cell>, <List> ): Copies values from the list to the first cells on the right of the given cell. Resulting cells are ([Free, Dependent and Auxiliary Objects|free objects)], i.e. independent of the list.

description: Copies values from the matrix into the spreadsheet. The upper left corner of the matrix is matched to the given cell. Resulting cells are free objects, i.e. independent of the matrix.
notes: CellRange has to be entered like this: e.g.: B2:D5. Object can be anything, e.g.: 3, RandomBetween(0, 10), Circle(A, B). * Cells are labelled by column and row, e.g.: B2.
See also FillRow and FillColumn commands.
You can use FillCell(cell, Transpose({list})) to fill vertically. The extra braces convert the list into a matrix thus {list}
search_text: FillCells | [spreadsheet] | FillCells( <Cell>, <Matrix> ): Copies values from the matrix into the spreadsheet. The upper left corner of the matrix is matched to the given cell. Resulting cells are free objects, i.e. independent of the matrix.

description: Copies the value/equation etc. of the object to the given cellrange. Resulting cells are free objects, i.e. independent of object.
notes: CellRange has to be entered like this: e.g.: B2:D5. Object can be anything, e.g.: 3, RandomBetween(0, 10), Circle(A, B). * Cells are labelled by column and row, e.g.: B2.
See also FillRow and FillColumn commands.
You can use FillCell(cell, Transpose({list})) to fill vertically. The extra braces convert the list into a matrix thus {list}
search_text: FillCells | [spreadsheet] | FillCells( <CellRange>, <Object> ): Copies the value/equation etc. of the object to the given cellrange. Resulting cells are free objects, i.e. independent of object.

description: Copies values from the list to the first cells of the column given by number (1 for A, 2 for B, etc.). Resulting cells are free objects, i.e. independent of the list.
notes: See also the FillRow and FillCells commands.
search_text: FillColumn | [spreadsheet] | FillColumn( <Column>, <List> ): Copies values from the list to the first cells of the column given by number (1 for A, 2 for B, etc.). Resulting cells are free objects, i.e. independent of the list.

description: Copies values from the list to the first cells of the row given by number. Resulting cells are free objects, i.e. independent of the list.
notes: See also the FillColumn and FillCells commands.
search_text: FillRow | [spreadsheet] | FillRow( <Row>, <List> ): Copies values from the list to the first cells of the row given by number. Resulting cells are free objects, i.e. independent of the list.

description: Gives a new list that contains the first element of the given list.
notes: To get the first element use Element({1, 4, 3}, 1).
See also Last Command.
search_text: First | [list] | First( <List> ): Gives a new list that contains the first element of the given list. | Examples: First({1, 4, 3})

description: Gives a new list that contains just the first n elements of the given list.
notes: To get the first element use Element({1, 4, 3}, 1).
See also Last Command.
search_text: First | [list] | First( <List>, <Number n of elements> ): Gives a new list that contains just the first n elements of the given list. | Examples: First({1, 4, 3}, 2)

description: This command is useful for loci generated by NSolveODE Command - It returns list points that were created in the first n steps of the numeric ODE-solving algorithm. loci generated using ShortestDistance Command, TravelingSalesman Command, Voronoi Command, MinimumSpanningTree Command and ConvexHull Command Commands - It returns vertices of the graph.
notes: To get the first element use Element({1, 4, 3}, 1).
See also Last Command.
search_text: First | [list] | First( <Locus>, <Number n of elements> ): This command is useful for loci generated by NSolveODE Command - It returns list points that were created in the first n steps of the numeric ODE-solving algorithm. loci generated using ShortestDistance Command, TravelingSalesman Command, Voronoi Command, MinimumSpanningTree Command and ConvexHull Command Commands - It returns vertices of the graph.

description: Gives first character of the text.
notes: To get the first element use Element({1, 4, 3}, 1).
See also Last Command.
search_text: First | [list] | First( <Text> ): Gives first character of the text. | Examples: First("Hello")

description: Gives the first n characters of the text.
notes: To get the first element use Element({1, 4, 3}, 1).
See also Last Command.
search_text: First | [list] | First( <Text> , <Number n of elements> ): Gives the first n characters of the text. | Examples: First("Hello",2)

description: Returns a linear combination of the functions that best fit the points in the list.
notes: See also FitExp, FitGrowth, FitLine, FitLineX, FitLog, FitLogistic, FitPoly, FitPow and FitSin If you work with big/small numbers, you should consider normalizing them for a more accurate result, see Normalize Command.
search_text: Fit | [statistics] | Fit( <List of Points>, <List of Functions> ): Returns a linear combination of the functions that best fit the points in the list. | Examples: Fit({(-2, 3), (0, 1), (2, 1), (2, 3)}, {x^2, x}); Fit(L, F)

description: Returns a function that fits the points in the list with minimum squared error with respect to the specified model. The given model function must depend on one or more sliders, that are taken as start values of parameters to be optimized. The non-linear iteration might not converge, but adjusting the sliders to a better starting point might help.
notes: See also FitExp, FitGrowth, FitLine, FitLineX, FitLog, FitLogistic, FitPoly, FitPow and FitSin If you work with big/small numbers, you should consider normalizing them for a more accurate result, see Normalize Command.
search_text: Fit | [statistics] | Fit( <List of points>, <Function> ): Returns a function that fits the points in the list with minimum squared error with respect to the specified model. The given model function must depend on one or more sliders, that are taken as start values of parameters to be optimized. The non-linear iteration might not converge, but adjusting the sliders to a better starting point might help. | Examples: Fit({(-2, 3), (0, 1), (2, 1), (2, 3)}, a + x^2)

description: Calculates the exponential regression curve in the form aℯ^bx^.
notes: If you want the answer in the form (formula) then use the FitGrowth Command. You can do a direct least-squares fitting with Fit(list, aexp(bx)) Euler's number ℯ can be obtained by pressing [.kcode]#ALT# + [.kcode]#e#. See also Fit, FitGrowth, FitLine, FitLineX, FitLog, FitLogistic, FitPoly, FitPow and FitSin.
search_text: FitExp | [statistics] | FitExp( <List of Points> ): Calculates the exponential regression curve in the form aℯ^bx^. | Examples: FitExp({(0, 1), (2, 3), (4, 3), (6, 4)})

description: Calculates a function of the form (formula) to the points in the list. (Very similar to FitExp[ <List of Points> ], just in a slightly different form).
notes: You can do a direct least-squares fitting with Fit(list, ab^x) * See also Fit, FitExp, FitLine, FitLineX, FitLog, FitLogistic, FitPoly, FitPow and FitSin
search_text: FitGrowth | [statistics] | FitGrowth( <List of Points> ): Calculates a function of the form (formula) to the points in the list. (Very similar to FitExp[ <List of Points> ], just in a slightly different form). | Examples: FitGrowth({(0, 1), (2, 3), (4, 3), (6, 4)})

description: Attempts to find a best-fit implicit curve of order n ≥ 2 through the points. You need at least (formula) points.
notes: See also the ImplicitCurve, FitExp, FitGrowth, FitLine, FitLineX, FitLog, FitLogistic, FitPoly, FitPow and FitSin commands.
search_text: FitImplicit | [statistics] | FitImplicit( <List of Points>, <Order> ): Attempts to find a best-fit implicit curve of order n ≥ 2 through the points. You need at least (formula) points.

description: Calculates the y on x regression line of the points.
notes: See also Best Fit Line tool and FitLineX Command If you work with big/small numbers, you should consider normalizing them for a more accurate result, see Normalize Command.
search_text: FitLine | [statistics] | FitLine( <List of Points> ): Calculates the y on x regression line of the points. | Examples: FitLine({(-2, 1), (1, 2), (2, 4), (4, 3), (5, 4)})

description: Calculates the y on x regression line of the points.
notes: See also Best Fit Line tool and FitLineX Command If you work with big/small numbers, you should consider normalizing them for a more accurate result, see Normalize Command.
search_text: FitLine | [statistics] | FitLine( <List of Points> ): Calculates the y on x regression line of the points. | Examples: FitLine({(-2, 1), (1, 2), (2, 4), (4, 3), (5, 4)})

description: Calculates the x on y regression line of the points.
notes: See also Best Fit Line tool and FitLine Command If you work with big/small numbers, you should consider normalizing them for a more accurate result, see Normalize Command.
search_text: FitLineX | [statistics] | FitLineX( <List of Points> ): Calculates the x on y regression line of the points. | Examples: FitLineX({(-1, 3), (2, 1), (3, 4), (5, 3), (6, 5)})

description: Calculates the x on y regression line of the points.
notes: See also Best Fit Line tool and FitLine Command If you work with big/small numbers, you should consider normalizing them for a more accurate result, see Normalize Command.
search_text: FitLineX | [statistics] | FitLineX( <List of Points> ): Calculates the x on y regression line of the points. | Examples: FitLineX({(-1, 3), (2, 1), (3, 4), (5, 3), (6, 5)})

description: Calculates the logarithmic regression curve.
notes: Euler's number ℯ can be obtained by pressing [.kcode]#ALT# + [.kcode]#e#. See also FitExp Command, FitPoly Command, FitPow Command and FitSin Command. * If you work with big/small numbers, you should consider normalizing them for a more accurate result, see Normalize Command.
search_text: FitLog | [statistics] | FitLog( <List of Points> ): Calculates the logarithmic regression curve. | Examples: FitLog({(ℯ, 1), (ℯ^2, 4)})

description: Calculates the logarithmic regression curve.
notes: Euler's number ℯ can be obtained by pressing [.kcode]#ALT# + [.kcode]#e#. See also FitExp Command, FitPoly Command, FitPow Command and FitSin Command. * If you work with big/small numbers, you should consider normalizing them for a more accurate result, see Normalize Command.
search_text: FitLog | [statistics] | FitLog( <List of Points> ): Calculates the logarithmic regression curve. | Examples: FitLog({(ℯ, 1), (ℯ^2, 4)})

description: Calculates the regression curve in the form (formula).
notes: The first and last data points should be fairly close to the curve. The list should have at least 3 points, preferably more. All points should have positive y-coordinates See also Fit, FitExp, FitGrowth, FitLine, FitLineX, FitLog, FitPoly, FitPow and FitSin. * If you work with big/small numbers, you should consider normalizing them for a more accurate result, see Normalize Command.
search_text: FitLogistic | [statistics] | FitLogistic( <List of Points> ): Calculates the regression curve in the form (formula). | Examples: FitLogistic({(-6, 2), (0, 2), (3, 4), (3.4, 8)})

description: Calculates the polynomial regression model of given degree that fits a function drawn using the Freehand Shape Tool.
notes: To obtain a polynomial of degree n the list must contain at least n + 1 points. See also FitExp Command, FitLog Command, FitPow Command and FitSin Command. * When working with big/small numbers, consider normalizing them for a more accurate result. See Normalize Command.
search_text: FitPoly | [statistics] | FitPoly( <Freehand Function>, <Degree of Polynomial> ): Calculates the polynomial regression model of given degree that fits a function drawn using the Freehand Shape Tool.

description: Calculates the polynomial regression model of given degree that fits the specified points.
notes: To obtain a polynomial of degree n the list must contain at least n + 1 points. See also FitExp Command, FitLog Command, FitPow Command and FitSin Command. * When working with big/small numbers, consider normalizing them for a more accurate result. See Normalize Command.
search_text: FitPoly | [statistics] | FitPoly( <List of Points>, <Degree of Polynomial> ): Calculates the polynomial regression model of given degree that fits the specified points. | Examples: FitPoly({(-1, -1), (0, 1), (1, 1), (2, 5)}, 3)

description: Calculates the polynomial regression model of given degree that fits the specified points.
notes: To obtain a polynomial of degree n the list must contain at least n + 1 points. See also FitExp Command, FitLog Command, FitPow Command and FitSin Command. * When working with big/small numbers, consider normalizing them for a more accurate result. See Normalize Command.
search_text: FitPoly | [statistics] | FitPoly( <List of Points>, <Degree of Polynomial> ): Calculates the polynomial regression model of given degree that fits the specified points. | Examples: FitPoly({(-1, -1), (0, 1), (1, 1), (2, 5)}, 3)

description: Calculates the regression curve in the form a x^b^.
notes: All points used need to be in the first quadrant of the coordinate system. See also FitExp Command, FitLog Command, FitPoly Command, and FitSin Command. * If you work with big/small numbers, you should consider normalizing them for a more accurate result, see Normalize Command.
search_text: FitPow | [statistics] | FitPow( <List of Points> ): Calculates the regression curve in the form a x^b^. | Examples: FitPow({(1, 1), (3, 2), (7, 4)})

description: Calculates the regression curve in the form a x^b^.
notes: All points used need to be in the first quadrant of the coordinate system. See also FitExp Command, FitLog Command, FitPoly Command, and FitSin Command. * If you work with big/small numbers, you should consider normalizing them for a more accurate result, see Normalize Command.
search_text: FitPow | [statistics] | FitPow( <List of Points> ): Calculates the regression curve in the form a x^b^. | Examples: FitPow({(1, 1), (3, 2), (7, 4)})

description: Calculates the regression curve in the form a + b sin (c x + d).
notes: The list should have at least four points, preferably more. The list should cover at least two extremal points. The first two local extremal points should not be too different from the absolute extremal points of the curve. See also FitExp Command, FitLog Command, FitPoly Command and FitPow Command. * If you work with big/small numbers, you should consider normalizing them for a more accurate result, see Normalize Command.
search_text: FitSin | [statistics] | FitSin( <List of Points> ): Calculates the regression curve in the form a + b sin (c x + d). | Examples: FitSin({(1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 1), (4, 0), (5, 1), (6, 2)})

description: Flattens lists to one list.
search_text: Flatten | [list] | Flatten( <List> ): Flattens lists to one list. | Examples: Flatten({2, 3, {5, 1}, {{2, 1, {3}}}})

description: Yields (all) foci of the conic section.
notes: See also the Directrix command.
search_text: Focus | [conic] | Focus( <Conic> ): Yields (all) foci of the conic section. | Examples: Focus(4x^2 - y^2 + 16x + 20 = 0)

description: Returns the formula that defines the object as a LaTeX text. Values are substituted for variables by default.
notes: In GeoGebra Classic v.5, the command FormulaText($1) returns the content of cell 1 of the CAS View as a LaTeX text.
search_text: FormulaText | [text] | FormulaText( <Object> ): Returns the formula that defines the object as a LaTeX text. Values are substituted for variables by default. | Examples: FormulaText(f)

description: Returns the formula that defines the object as a LaTeX text. The Boolean variable determines if values are substituted for variables (true) or if variable names are shown in the text (false).
notes: In GeoGebra Classic v.5, the command FormulaText($1) returns the content of cell 1 of the CAS View as a LaTeX text.
search_text: FormulaText | [text] | FormulaText( <Object>, <Boolean for Substitution of Variables> ): Returns the formula that defines the object as a LaTeX text. The Boolean variable determines if values are substituted for variables (true) or if variable names are shown in the text (false). | Examples: FormulaText(f, true); FormulaText(f, false)

syntax: FormulaText( <Object>, <Boolean for Substitution of Variables>, <Boolean Show Name> )
description: Returns the formula that defines the object as a LaTeX text. The first Boolean variable determines if values are substituted for variables (true) or if variable names are shown in the text (false), the second Boolean variable determines if the object name is shown in the text (true) or not (false).
notes: In GeoGebra Classic v.5, the command FormulaText($1) returns the content of cell 1 of the CAS View as a LaTeX text.
search_text: FormulaText | [text] | FormulaText( <Object>, <Boolean for Substitution of Variables>, <Boolean Show Name> ): Returns the formula that defines the object as a LaTeX text. The first Boolean variable determines if values are substituted for variables (true) or if variable names are shown in the text (false), the second Boolean variable determines if the object name is shown in the text (true) or not (false). | Examples: FormulaText(f, true, true); FormulaText(f, false, false)

description: Creates and shows in the Graphics View a LaTeX text containing the fraction form of the given number.
notes: See also SurdText command.
search_text: FractionText | [text] | FractionText( <Number> ): Creates and shows in the Graphics View a LaTeX text containing the fraction form of the given number. | Examples: FractionText(Slope(a))

description: Creates and shows in the Graphics View a LaTeX text containing the fraction form of the given number. The boolean sets the position of a possible negative sign of the fraction. If true, the minus sign will be displayed in the numerator, if false the minus sign will be displayed in front of the fraction.
notes: See also SurdText command.
search_text: FractionText | [text] | FractionText( <Number>, <Boolean Single fraction>): Creates and shows in the Graphics View a LaTeX text containing the fraction form of the given number. The boolean sets the position of a possible negative sign of the fraction. If true, the minus sign will be displayed in the numerator, if false the minus sign will be displayed in front of the fraction. | Examples: FractionText(n, true); FractionText(n, false)

description: Creates and shows in the Graphics View a LaTeX text containing the fraction form of the coordinates of the given point.
notes: See also SurdText command.
search_text: FractionText | [text] | FractionText( <Point> ): Creates and shows in the Graphics View a LaTeX text containing the fraction form of the coordinates of the given point. | Examples: FractionText(A)

syntax: Frequency( <Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Use Density> , <Density Scale Factor>(optional) )
description: Returns a list of frequencies for the corresponding Histogram Command.
notes: See also the ContingencyTable command.
search_text: Frequency | [list] | Frequency( <Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Use Density> , <Density Scale Factor>(optional) ): Returns a list of frequencies for the corresponding Histogram Command.

syntax: Frequency( <Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Class Boundaries>,<List of Raw Data> )
description: If Cumulative = false, returns the same list as Frequency( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data> ) If Cumulative = true, returns a list of cumulative frequencies for Frequency( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data> )
notes: See also the ContingencyTable command.
search_text: Frequency | [list] | Frequency( <Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Class Boundaries>,<List of Raw Data> ): If Cumulative = false, returns the same list as Frequency( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data> ) If Cumulative = true, returns a list of cumulative frequencies for Frequency( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data> )

description: If Cumulative = false, returns the same list as Frequency( <List of Raw Data> ) If Cumulative = true, returns a list of cumulative frequencies for Frequency( <List of Raw Data> ) .
notes: See also the ContingencyTable command.
search_text: Frequency | [list] | Frequency( <Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Raw Data> ): If Cumulative = false, returns the same list as Frequency( <List of Raw Data> ) If Cumulative = true, returns a list of cumulative frequencies for Frequency( <List of Raw Data> ) . | Examples: list1 = { 0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 2 }; Frequency(true, list1); Frequency(false, list1)

syntax: Frequency( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data> )
description: Returns a list of the counts of values from the given data list that lie within intervals of the form [a, b), where a and b are all the couples of consecutive numbers in the given class boundaries list. The highest interval has the form [a, b].
notes: See also the ContingencyTable command.
search_text: Frequency | [list] | Frequency( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data> ): Returns a list of the counts of values from the given data list that lie within intervals of the form [a, b), where a and b are all the couples of consecutive numbers in the given class boundaries list. The highest interval has the form [a, b]. | Examples: Frequency({1, 2, 3},  {1, 1, 2, 3})

syntax: Frequency( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Use Density> , <Density Scale Factor> (optional) )
description: Returns a list of frequencies for the corresponding Histogram Command. If Use density = false, returns the same list as Frequency( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data> ) If Use density = true, returns the list of frequencies of each class.
notes: See also the ContingencyTable command.
search_text: Frequency | [list] | Frequency( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Use Density> , <Density Scale Factor> (optional) ): Returns a list of frequencies for the corresponding Histogram Command. If Use density = false, returns the same list as Frequency( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data> ) If Use density = true, returns the list of frequencies of each class. | Examples: data = {1, 2, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 4}; classes={0, 2, 5}; Frequency(classes, data, false)

description: Returns a list with a count of the occurrences of each unique value in the given list of data. This input list can be numbers or text. The list is sorted in ascending order of the unique values. To get a list of the corresponding unique values use the Unique Command.
notes: See also the ContingencyTable command.
search_text: Frequency | [list] | Frequency( <List of Raw Data> ): Returns a list with a count of the occurrences of each unique value in the given list of data. This input list can be numbers or text. The list is sorted in ascending order of the unique values. To get a list of the corresponding unique values use the Unique Command. | Examples: list1 = { "a", "a", "x", "x", "x", "b" }; Frequency(list1); Unique(list1)

description: Returns a contingency matrix containing counts of paired values from the two lists. The rows of the matrix correspond to the unique values in the first list, and the columns correspond to the unique values in the second list. To get a list of the unique values for each list use the command Unique Command.
notes: See also the ContingencyTable command.
search_text: Frequency | [list] | Frequency( <List of Text>, <List of Text> ): Returns a contingency matrix containing counts of paired values from the two lists. The rows of the matrix correspond to the unique values in the first list, and the columns correspond to the unique values in the second list. To get a list of the unique values for each list use the command Unique Command. | Examples: list1 = {"a", "b", "b", "c", "c", "c", "c"}; list2 =  {"a", "b", "a", "a", "c", "c", "d"}; Frequency(list1, list2)

syntax: FrequencyPolygon( <Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Boolean Use Density> , <Density Scale Factor (optional)> )
description: If Cumulative is true this creates a frequency polygon where each vertex y-coordinate equals the frequency of the class plus the sum of all previous frequencies.
notes: Frequency polygon is a line graph drawn by joining all the midpoints of the top of the bars of a histogram. Therefore usage of this command is the same as usage of Histogram Command.
For further examples see Histogram Command.
search_text: FrequencyPolygon | [chart] | FrequencyPolygon( <Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Boolean Use Density> , <Density Scale Factor (optional)> ): If Cumulative is true this creates a frequency polygon where each vertex y-coordinate equals the frequency of the class plus the sum of all previous frequencies.

syntax: FrequencyPolygon( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Heights> )
description: Creates a frequency polygon with vertices in given heights. The class boundaries determine the x-coordinate of each vertex.
notes: Frequency polygon is a line graph drawn by joining all the midpoints of the top of the bars of a histogram. Therefore usage of this command is the same as usage of Histogram Command.
For further examples see Histogram Command.
search_text: FrequencyPolygon | [chart] | FrequencyPolygon( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Heights> ): Creates a frequency polygon with vertices in given heights. The class boundaries determine the x-coordinate of each vertex. | Examples: FrequencyPolygon({0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, {2, 6, 8, 3, 1})

syntax: FrequencyPolygon( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Boolean Use Density>, <Density Scale Factor (optional)> )
description: Creates a frequency polygon using the raw data. The class boundaries determine the x-coordinates of vertices and are used to determine how many data elements lie in each class. The y-coordinate of a vertex is determined as follows If Use Density = true, height = (Density Scale Factor) (class frequency) / (class width) * If Use Density = false, height = class frequency By default, Use Density = true and Density Scale Factor = 1.
notes: Frequency polygon is a line graph drawn by joining all the midpoints of the top of the bars of a histogram. Therefore usage of this command is the same as usage of Histogram Command.
For further examples see Histogram Command.
search_text: FrequencyPolygon | [chart] | FrequencyPolygon( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Boolean Use Density>, <Density Scale Factor (optional)> ): Creates a frequency polygon using the raw data. The class boundaries determine the x-coordinates of vertices and are used to determine how many data elements lie in each class. The y-coordinate of a vertex is determined as follows If Use Density = true, height = (Density Scale Factor) (class frequency) / (class width) * If Use Density = false, height = class frequency By default, Use Density = true and Density Scale Factor = 1.

syntax: FrequencyTable( <Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data> )
description: If Cumulative = false, returns the same table as FrequencyTable( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data> ) If Cumulative = true, returns a table whose first column is the same as in FrequencyTable( <List of Raw Data> ) and the second contains cumulative frequencies of values in the first column.
notes: In the list there appears 1 three-times, so the count of the occurences of 1 (=3) has to be multiplied by the scale factor 2 to get entry 6 in the second column.
This command is similar to Frequency Command and Histogram Command. Articles about these commands contain some related examples.
search_text: FrequencyTable | [chart] | FrequencyTable( <Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data> ): If Cumulative = false, returns the same table as FrequencyTable( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data> ) If Cumulative = true, returns a table whose first column is the same as in FrequencyTable( <List of Raw Data> ) and the second contains cumulative frequencies of values in the first column.

syntax: FrequencyTable( <Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Use Density> , <Density Scale Factor (optional)> )
description: Returns a table (as text) whose first column contains intervals (classes) and second contains frequencies for the corresponding Histogram Command.
notes: In the list there appears 1 three-times, so the count of the occurences of 1 (=3) has to be multiplied by the scale factor 2 to get entry 6 in the second column.
This command is similar to Frequency Command and Histogram Command. Articles about these commands contain some related examples.
search_text: FrequencyTable | [chart] | FrequencyTable( <Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Use Density> , <Density Scale Factor (optional)> ): Returns a table (as text) whose first column contains intervals (classes) and second contains frequencies for the corresponding Histogram Command.

description: If Cumulative = false, returns the same table as Frequency( <List of Raw Data> ) If Cumulative = true, returns a table whose first column is the same as in FrequencyTable( <List of Raw Data> ) and the second contains cumulative frequencies of values in the first column.
notes: In the list there appears 1 three-times, so the count of the occurences of 1 (=3) has to be multiplied by the scale factor 2 to get entry 6 in the second column.
This command is similar to Frequency Command and Histogram Command. Articles about these commands contain some related examples.
search_text: FrequencyTable | [chart] | FrequencyTable( <Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Raw Data> ): If Cumulative = false, returns the same table as Frequency( <List of Raw Data> ) If Cumulative = true, returns a table whose first column is the same as in FrequencyTable( <List of Raw Data> ) and the second contains cumulative frequencies of values in the first column.

syntax: FrequencyTable( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data> )
description: Returns a table (as text) whose first column contains intervals (classes) and second column contains the count of numbers in List of Raw Data, which belong to the interval in the first column. All intervals except the highest interval are of the form [a, b). The highest interval has the form [a, b].
notes: In the list there appears 1 three-times, so the count of the occurences of 1 (=3) has to be multiplied by the scale factor 2 to get entry 6 in the second column.
This command is similar to Frequency Command and Histogram Command. Articles about these commands contain some related examples.
search_text: FrequencyTable | [chart] | FrequencyTable( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data> ): Returns a table (as text) whose first column contains intervals (classes) and second column contains the count of numbers in List of Raw Data, which belong to the interval in the first column. All intervals except the highest interval are of the form [a, b). The highest interval has the form [a, b].

syntax: FrequencyTable( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Use Density> , <Density Scale Factor (optional)> )
description: Returns a table (as text) whose first column contains intervals (classes) and second contains frequencies for the corresponding Histogram Command.
notes: In the list there appears 1 three-times, so the count of the occurences of 1 (=3) has to be multiplied by the scale factor 2 to get entry 6 in the second column.
This command is similar to Frequency Command and Histogram Command. Articles about these commands contain some related examples.
search_text: FrequencyTable | [chart] | FrequencyTable( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Use Density> , <Density Scale Factor (optional)> ): Returns a table (as text) whose first column contains intervals (classes) and second contains frequencies for the corresponding Histogram Command.

description: Returns a table (as text) whose first column contains sorted list of unique elements of list L and second column contains the count of the occurrences of value in the first column. List L can be numbers or text.
notes: In the list there appears 1 three-times, so the count of the occurences of 1 (=3) has to be multiplied by the scale factor 2 to get entry 6 in the second column.
This command is similar to Frequency Command and Histogram Command. Articles about these commands contain some related examples.
search_text: FrequencyTable | [chart] | FrequencyTable( <List of Raw Data> ): Returns a table (as text) whose first column contains sorted list of unique elements of list L and second column contains the count of the occurrences of value in the first column. List L can be numbers or text.

description: Returns a table (as text) whose first column Value contains a sorted list of unique elements of the <List of Raw Data> and second column Frequency contains the count of the occurrences of value in the first column multiplied by the <Scale Factor>. The list can be numbers or text.
notes: In the list there appears 1 three-times, so the count of the occurences of 1 (=3) has to be multiplied by the scale factor 2 to get entry 6 in the second column.
This command is similar to Frequency Command and Histogram Command. Articles about these commands contain some related examples.
search_text: FrequencyTable | [chart] | FrequencyTable( <List of Raw Data>,<Scale Factor (optional)> ): Returns a table (as text) whose first column Value contains a sorted list of unique elements of the <List of Raw Data> and second column Frequency contains the count of the occurrences of value in the first column multiplied by the <Scale Factor>. The list can be numbers or text. | Examples: FrequencyTable({"red", "red", "green", "green", "blue"}, 5); FrequencyTable({1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, 5}, 2)

description: Converts given number from given https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix[base] into decimal base. The base must be between 2 and 36. The number must be an integer.
notes: See also ToBase command
search_text: FromBase | [algebra] | FromBase( "<Number as Text>", <Base> ): Converts given number from given https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix[base] into decimal base. The base must be between 2 and 36. The number must be an integer. | Examples: FromBase("FF", 16); FromBase("100000000", 2)

syntax: Function( <Expression>, <Parameter Variable 1>, <Start Value>, <End Value>, <Parameter Variable 2>, <Start Value>, <End Value> )
description: Restricts the visualization of the representative surface of a function of two variables in 3D space.
search_text: Function | [3d] | Function( <Expression>, <Parameter Variable 1>, <Start Value>, <End Value>, <Parameter Variable 2>, <Start Value>, <End Value> ): Restricts the visualization of the representative surface of a function of two variables in 3D space. | Examples: a(x, y) = x + 0y; Function[u, u, 0, 3, v, 0, 2]

description: Yields the following function: The first two numbers determine the start x-value and the end x-value. The rest of the numbers are the y-values of the function in between in equal distances.
search_text: Function | [3d] | Function( <List of Numbers> ): Yields the following function: The first two numbers determine the start x-value and the end x-value. The rest of the numbers are the y-values of the function in between in equal distances. | Examples: Function[{2, 4, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0}]; Function[{-3, 3, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5}]

search_text: Function | [3d] | Function(Function, Start x-value, End x-value): Restricts the visualization of the given function to the interval [Start x-value, End x-value]. | Examples: Function(x + 2, 1, 2)

description: Returns the future value of an investment based on periodic, constant payments and a constant interest rate. <Rate> Interest rate per period. <Number of Periods> Total number of payment periods in an annuity. <Payment> The amount paid in each period. <Present Value (optional)> Total amount that a series of future payments is worth now. If you do not enter a value, it is assumed to be 0. <Type (optional)>* Indicates when payments are due. If you do not enter a value or you enter 0 the payment is due at the end of the period. If you enter 1 it is due at the beginning of the period.
notes: Make sure that you are consistent about the units you use for <Rate> and <Number of Periods>. If you make monthly payments on a four-year loan at an annual interest rate of 10 percent, use 10%/12 for rate and 4*12 for number of payments.
For all arguments, cash paid out is represented by negative numbers and cash received by positive numbers.
See also Payment, Rate, Present Value and Periods commands.
search_text: FutureValue | [financial] | FutureValue( <Rate>, <Number of Periods>, <Payment>, <Present Value (optional)>, <Type (optional)> ): Returns the future value of an investment based on periodic, constant payments and a constant interest rate. <Rate> Interest rate per period. <Number of Periods> Total number of payment periods in an annuity. <Payment> The amount paid in each period. <Present Value (optional)> Total amount that a series of future payments is worth now. If you do not enter a value, it is assumed to be 0. <Type (optional)>* Indicates when payments are due. If you do not enter a value or you enter 0 the payment is due at the end of the period. If you enter 1 it is due at the beginning of the period. | Examples: FutureValue(10%/12, 15, -200, 0, 1)

description: Calculates the greatest common divisor of the list of numbers.
notes: This command differs among variants of English: GCD (US) HCF (UK + Aus)
See also LCM Command and ExtendedGCD Command.
search_text: GCD | [algebra] | GCD( <List of Numbers> ): Calculates the greatest common divisor of the list of numbers. | Examples: GCD({12, 30, 18})

notes: This command differs among variants of English: GCD (US) HCF (UK + Aus)
See also LCM Command and ExtendedGCD Command.
search_text: GCD | [algebra] | GCD( <List of Polynomials> ): Calculates the greatest common divisor of the list of polynomials. | Examples: GCD({x^2 + 4 x + 4, x^2 - x - 6, x^3 - 4 x^2 - 3 x + 18})

description: Calculates the greatest common divisor of the two numbers .
notes: This command differs among variants of English: GCD (US) HCF (UK + Aus)
See also LCM Command and ExtendedGCD Command.
search_text: GCD | [algebra] | GCD( <Number>, <Number> ): Calculates the greatest common divisor of the two numbers . | Examples: GCD(12, 15)

description: Calculates the greatest common divisor of the two polynomials.
notes: This command differs among variants of English: GCD (US) HCF (UK + Aus)
See also LCM Command and ExtendedGCD Command.
search_text: GCD | [algebra] | GCD( <Polynomial>, <Polynomial> ): Calculates the greatest common divisor of the two polynomials. | Examples: GCD(x^2 + 4 x + 4, x^2 - x - 6)

description: Calculates the value of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gamma_distribution[Gamma distribution] at variable value v, i.e. the probability P(X ≤ v) where X is a random variable with a Gamma distribution defined by the parameters alpha and beta.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given value, that is the area under the Gamma distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: Gamma | [probability] | Gamma( <Alpha>, <Beta>, <Variable Value> ): Calculates the value of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gamma_distribution[Gamma distribution] at variable value v, i.e. the probability P(X ≤ v) where X is a random variable with a Gamma distribution defined by the parameters alpha and beta.

description: If Cumulative = true, calculates the value of the cumulative distribution function of a Gamma distribution with given alpha and beta at the given variable value, otherwise it calculates the probability density function of the distribution at variable value.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given value, that is the area under the Gamma distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: Gamma | [probability] | Gamma( <Alpha>, <Beta>, <Variable Value>, <Boolean Cumulative> ): If Cumulative = true, calculates the value of the cumulative distribution function of a Gamma distribution with given alpha and beta at the given variable value, otherwise it calculates the probability density function of the distribution at variable value.

description: If Cumulative = true, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of a Gamma distribution with given alpha and beta, othewise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given value, that is the area under the Gamma distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: Gamma | [probability] | Gamma( <Alpha>, <Beta>, x, <Boolean Cumulative> ): If Cumulative = true, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of a Gamma distribution with given alpha and beta, othewise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.

description: Creates a text using Format as a template replacing any of the following characters when prefixed by a backslash (\): d, D, j, l, N, S, w, z, W, F, m, M, n, t, L, Y, y, a, A, g, G, h, H, i, s, U - the explanation to these characters are here http://php.net/manual/en/function.date.php .
