description: Computes the Gröbner basis of the list of the polynomials with respect to graded reverse lexicographical ordering of the variables (also known as total degree reverse lexicographic ordering, degrevlex or grevlex ordering).

description: Computes the Gröbner basis of the list of the polynomials with respect to graded reverse lexicographical ordering of the given variables (also known as total degree reverse lexicographic ordering, degrevlex or grevlex ordering).

description: Makes all objects in given layer invisible. Does not override Conditional Visibility.
search_text: HideLayer | [other] | HideLayer( <Number> ): Makes all objects in given layer invisible. Does not override Conditional Visibility.

syntax: Histogram( <Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Boolean Use Density> , <Density Scale Factor> (optional) )
description: If Cumulative is true this creates a histogram where each bar height equals the frequency of the class plus the sum of all previous frequencies.
notes: All elements of Raw Data must be within the interval of the class boundaries, otherwise “undefined” will be returned.
By convention this uses the a ≤ x < b rule for each class except for the last class which is a ≤ x ≤ b
search_text: Histogram | [chart] | Histogram( <Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Boolean Use Density> , <Density Scale Factor> (optional) ): If Cumulative is true this creates a histogram where each bar height equals the frequency of the class plus the sum of all previous frequencies. | Examples: Histogram(true, {10, 20, 30, 40}, {10, 11, 11, 12, 18, 20, 25, 40}, true)

syntax: Histogram( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Heights> )
description: Creates a histogram with bars of the given heights. The class boundaries determine the width and position of each bar of the histogram.
notes: All elements of Raw Data must be within the interval of the class boundaries, otherwise “undefined” will be returned.
By convention this uses the a ≤ x < b rule for each class except for the last class which is a ≤ x ≤ b
search_text: Histogram | [chart] | Histogram( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Heights> ): Creates a histogram with bars of the given heights. The class boundaries determine the width and position of each bar of the histogram. | Examples: Histogram({0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, {2, 6, 8, 3, 1})

syntax: Histogram( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Boolean Use Density>, <Density Scale Factor>(optional) )
description: Creates a histogram using the raw data. The class boundaries determine the width and position of each bar of the histogram and are used to determine how many data elements lie in each class. Bar height is determined as follows If Use Density = true, height = (Density Scale Factor) (class frequency) / (class width) * If Use Density = false, height = class frequency By default, Use Density = true and Density Scale Factor = 1. This creates a histogram with total area = n, the number of data values.
notes: All elements of Raw Data must be within the interval of the class boundaries, otherwise “undefined” will be returned.
By convention this uses the a ≤ x < b rule for each class except for the last class which is a ≤ x ≤ b
search_text: Histogram | [chart] | Histogram( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Boolean Use Density>, <Density Scale Factor>(optional) ): Creates a histogram using the raw data. The class boundaries determine the width and position of each bar of the histogram and are used to determine how many data elements lie in each class. Bar height is determined as follows If Use Density = true, height = (Density Scale Factor) (class frequency) / (class width) * If Use Density = false, height = class frequency By default, Use Density = true and Density Scale Factor = 1. This creates a histogram with total area = n, the number of data values. | Examples: Histogram({10, 20, 30, 40}, {10, 11, 11, 12, 18, 20, 25, 40}, true); Histogram({10, 20, 30, 40}, {10, 11, 11, 12, 18, 20, 25, 40}, false); Histogram({10, 20, 30, 40}, {10, 11, 11, 12, 18, 20, 25, 40}, true, 10/ 8)

syntax: HistogramRight( <Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Boolean Use Density> , <Density Scale Factor> (optional) )
description: Same as Histogram(<Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Boolean Use Density>, <Density Scale Factor>), except that if a datum is equal to the right border of a class, it is counted in this class and not in the next one.
notes: By convention this uses the a < x ≤ b rule for each class except for the first class which is a ≤ x ≤ b
search_text: HistogramRight | [chart] | HistogramRight( <Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Boolean Use Density> , <Density Scale Factor> (optional) ): Same as Histogram(<Boolean Cumulative>, <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Boolean Use Density>, <Density Scale Factor>), except that if a datum is equal to the right border of a class, it is counted in this class and not in the next one.

syntax: HistogramRight( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Heights> )
description: Same as Histogram(<List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Heights>) (see Histogram Command).
notes: By convention this uses the a < x ≤ b rule for each class except for the first class which is a ≤ x ≤ b
search_text: HistogramRight | [chart] | HistogramRight( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Heights> ): Same as Histogram(<List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Heights>) (see Histogram Command).

syntax: HistogramRight( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Boolean Use Density> , <Density Scale Factor> (optional) )
description: Same as Histogram(<List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Boolean Use Density>, <Density Scale Factor>), except that if a datum is equal to the right border of a class, it is counted in this class and not in the next one.
notes: By convention this uses the a < x ≤ b rule for each class except for the first class which is a ≤ x ≤ b
search_text: HistogramRight | [chart] | HistogramRight( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Boolean Use Density> , <Density Scale Factor> (optional) ): Same as Histogram(<List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data>, <Boolean Use Density>, <Density Scale Factor>), except that if a datum is equal to the right border of a class, it is counted in this class and not in the next one.

description: Returns a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hypergeometric_distribution[Hypergeometric distribution] bar graph.
search_text: HyperGeometric | [probability] | HyperGeometric( <Population Size>, <Number of Successes>, <Sample Size>): Returns a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hypergeometric_distribution[Hypergeometric distribution] bar graph. | Examples: HyperGeometric(100, 50, 5)

syntax: HyperGeometric( <Population Size>, <Number of Successes>, <Sample Size>, <Boolean Cumulative> )
description: When Cumulative = false returns a bar graph of a Hypergeometric distribution, otherwise it returns the graph of a cumulative Hypergeometric distribution function. First three parameters are same as above.
search_text: HyperGeometric | [probability] | HyperGeometric( <Population Size>, <Number of Successes>, <Sample Size>, <Boolean Cumulative> ): When Cumulative = false returns a bar graph of a Hypergeometric distribution, otherwise it returns the graph of a cumulative Hypergeometric distribution function. First three parameters are same as above.

syntax: HyperGeometric( <Population Size>, <Number of Successes>, <Sample Size>, <Variable Value>, <Boolean Cumulative> )
description: Let X be a Hypergeometric random variable and v the variable value. The first three parameters are same as above. When Cumulative = false it returns P( X = v) When Cumulative = true it returns P( X ≤ v)
search_text: HyperGeometric | [probability] | HyperGeometric( <Population Size>, <Number of Successes>, <Sample Size>, <Variable Value>, <Boolean Cumulative> ): Let X be a Hypergeometric random variable and v the variable value. The first three parameters are same as above. When Cumulative = false it returns P( X = v) When Cumulative = true it returns P( X ≤ v) | Examples: HyperGeometric(10, 2, 2, 0, false); HyperGeometric(10, 2, 2, 1, false); HyperGeometric(10, 2, 2, 2, false)

syntax: HyperGeometric( <Population Size>, <Number of Successes>, <Sample Size>, <Variable Value>, <Boolean Cumulative> )
description: Let X be a Hypergeometric random variable and v the variable value. The first three parameters are the same as above. When Cumulative = false it returns P( X = v) When Cumulative = true it returns P( X ≤ v)
search_text: HyperGeometric | [probability] | HyperGeometric( <Population Size>, <Number of Successes>, <Sample Size>, <Variable Value>, <Boolean Cumulative> ): Let X be a Hypergeometric random variable and v the variable value. The first three parameters are the same as above. When Cumulative = false it returns P( X = v) When Cumulative = true it returns P( X ≤ v)

description: Creates a hyperbola with given focus points passing through a given point.
notes: If the condition: 0 < 2*semimajor axis length < Distance between the focus points isn't met, you will get an ellipse.
See also Hyperbola tool .
search_text: Hyperbola | [conic] | Hyperbola( <Focus>, <Focus>, <Point> ): Creates a hyperbola with given focus points passing through a given point. | Examples: Hyperbola((1, 1), (2, 1), (-2,-4))

description: Creates a hyperbola with given focus points where the length of the semimajor axis equals the length of the segment.
notes: If the condition: 0 < 2*semimajor axis length < Distance between the focus points isn't met, you will get an ellipse.
See also Hyperbola tool .
search_text: Hyperbola | [conic] | Hyperbola( <Focus>, <Focus>, <Segment> ): Creates a hyperbola with given focus points where the length of the semimajor axis equals the length of the segment. | Examples: a = Segment((0,1), (2,1)); Hyperbola((4, 1), (-2, 1), a)

description: Creates a hyperbola with given focus points and semimajor axis length.
notes: If the condition: 0 < 2*semimajor axis length < Distance between the focus points isn't met, you will get an ellipse.
See also Hyperbola tool .
search_text: Hyperbola | [conic] | Hyperbola( <Focus>, <Focus>, <Semimajor Axis Length> ): Creates a hyperbola with given focus points and semimajor axis length. | Examples: Hyperbola((0, -4), (2, 4), 1)

description: Creates an icosahedron having as base the given equilateral triangle.
notes: `Icosahedron(A, B)` is a shortcut for `Icosahedron(A, B, C)` with `C = Point(Circle(Midpoint(A, B), Distance(A, B) sqrt(3) / 2, Segment(A, B)))`.
See also Cube, Tetrahedron, Octahedron, Dodecahedron commands.
search_text: Icosahedron | [3d] | Icosahedron( <Equilateral Triangle> ): Creates an icosahedron having as base the given equilateral triangle.

description: Creates an icosahedron with the two points of the first face, and the third point automatically created on a circle, so that the icosahedron can rotate around its first edge.
notes: `Icosahedron(A, B)` is a shortcut for `Icosahedron(A, B, C)` with `C = Point(Circle(Midpoint(A, B), Distance(A, B) sqrt(3) / 2, Segment(A, B)))`.
See also Cube, Tetrahedron, Octahedron, Dodecahedron commands.
search_text: Icosahedron | [3d] | Icosahedron( <Point>, <Point>): Creates an icosahedron with the two points of the first face, and the third point automatically created on a circle, so that the icosahedron can rotate around its first edge.

description: Creates an icosahedron having the segment between the two points as an edge. The other vertices are univocally determined by the given direction, that needs to be: a vector, a segment, a line, a ray orthogonal to the segment, or a polygon, a plane parallel to the segment. + The created icosahedron will have: a face with the segment as an edge in a plane orthogonal to the given vector/segment/line/ray, or a face with the segment as an edge in a plane parallel to the polygon/plane.
notes: `Icosahedron(A, B)` is a shortcut for `Icosahedron(A, B, C)` with `C = Point(Circle(Midpoint(A, B), Distance(A, B) sqrt(3) / 2, Segment(A, B)))`.
See also Cube, Tetrahedron, Octahedron, Dodecahedron commands.
search_text: Icosahedron | [3d] | Icosahedron( <Point>, <Point>, <Direction> ): Creates an icosahedron having the segment between the two points as an edge. The other vertices are univocally determined by the given direction, that needs to be: a vector, a segment, a line, a ray orthogonal to the segment, or a polygon, a plane parallel to the segment. + The created icosahedron will have: a face with the segment as an edge in a plane orthogonal to the given vector/segment/line/ray, or a face with the segment as an edge in a plane parallel to the polygon/plane.

description: Creates an icosahedron with the three points of the first face. The points have to draw an equilateral triangle for the icosahedron to be defined.
notes: `Icosahedron(A, B)` is a shortcut for `Icosahedron(A, B, C)` with `C = Point(Circle(Midpoint(A, B), Distance(A, B) sqrt(3) / 2, Segment(A, B)))`.
See also Cube, Tetrahedron, Octahedron, Dodecahedron commands.
search_text: Icosahedron | [3d] | Icosahedron( <Point>, <Point>, <Point>): Creates an icosahedron with the three points of the first face. The points have to draw an equilateral triangle for the icosahedron to be defined.

description: Gives the identity matrix of the given order.
notes: If A is a square matrix of order n, A^0 yields the same as Identity(n).
search_text: Identity | [vector_and_matrix] | Identity( <Number> ): Gives the identity matrix of the given order. | Examples: Identity(3)

description: Yields a copy of "Then 1" when first condition is satisfied, "Then 2" if second condition is satisfied etc. If none of the conditions are satisfied and Else is given, this command yields a copy of Else. Otherwise undefined is returned.
examples: f(x,y,a,b,c) = If(sliderVal==1, x + 0y +a + 0b + c, sliderVal==2, 0x+ y^2 + 0a +2b +0c, x + y + 0a + b +0c)
notes: Derivative of If(condition, f(x), g(x)) gives If(condition, f'(x), g'(x)). It does not do any evaluation of limits at the critical points. See section: Boolean values for the symbols used in conditional statements.
Arguments of If must be Objects or Scripting Commands, not assignments. Syntax b = If(a > 1, 2, 3) is correct, but b = 2 or b = 3 would not be accepted as parameters of If.
search_text: If | [logic] | If( <Condition 1>, <Then 1>, <Condition 2>, <Then 2>, ... , <Else (optional)> ): Yields a copy of "Then 1" when first condition is satisfied, "Then 2" if second condition is satisfied etc. If none of the conditions are satisfied and Else is given, this command yields a copy of Else. Otherwise undefined is returned. | Examples: If(a ≟ 1, "Matthew", a ≟ 2,"Larry", a ≟ 3, "Vivian", "Alex"); f(x) = If(x < 3, sin(x), x^2); f(x) = If(0 <= x <= 3, sin(x))

description: Yields a copy of the object Then if the condition evaluates to true, and an undefined object if it evaluates to false.
notes: Derivative of If(condition, f(x), g(x)) gives If(condition, f'(x), g'(x)). It does not do any evaluation of limits at the critical points. See section: Boolean values for the symbols used in conditional statements.
Arguments of If must be Objects or Scripting Commands, not assignments. Syntax b = If(a > 1, 2, 3) is correct, but b = 2 or b = 3 would not be accepted as parameters of If.
search_text: If | [logic] | If( <Condition>, <Then> ): Yields a copy of the object Then if the condition evaluates to true, and an undefined object if it evaluates to false. | Examples: If(n==3, x + y = 4); If(n==3, x + y = 4)

description: Yields a copy of object Then if the condition evaluates to true, and a copy of object Else if it evaluates to false. Both objects must be of the same type.
notes: Derivative of If(condition, f(x), g(x)) gives If(condition, f'(x), g'(x)). It does not do any evaluation of limits at the critical points. See section: Boolean values for the symbols used in conditional statements.
Arguments of If must be Objects or Scripting Commands, not assignments. Syntax b = If(a > 1, 2, 3) is correct, but b = 2 or b = 3 would not be accepted as parameters of If.
search_text: If | [logic] | If( <Condition>, <Then>, <Else> ): Yields a copy of object Then if the condition evaluates to true, and a copy of object Else if it evaluates to false. Both objects must be of the same type. | Examples: If(n==3, x + y = 4, x - y = 4)

description: Returns position of first occurrence of Object in List.
notes: When the object is not found, result is undefined.
search_text: IndexOf | [list] | IndexOf( <Object>, <List> ): Returns position of first occurrence of Object in List. | Examples: IndexOf(5, {1, 3, 5, 2, 5, 4})

description: Same as above, but the search starts at given index.
notes: When the object is not found, result is undefined.
search_text: IndexOf | [list] | IndexOf( <Object>, <List>, <Start Index> ): Same as above, but the search starts at given index. | Examples: IndexOf(5, {1, 3, 5, 2, 5, 4}, 3); IndexOf(5, {1, 3, 5, 2, 5, 4}, 4); IndexOf(5, {1, 3, 5, 2, 5, 4}, 6)

description: Specifies the position at which the short text appears for the first time in the whole text.

description: Same as above, but the search starts at given index.
notes: When the object is not found, result is undefined.
search_text: IndexOf | [list] | IndexOf( <Text>, <Text>, <Start Index> ): Same as above, but the search starts at given index. | Examples: IndexOf("Ge", "GeoGebra",2)

description: Creates an infinite cone with given point as vertex, axis of symmetry parallel to given line and apex angle 2α.

search_text: InfiniteCone | [3d] | InfiniteCone( <Point>, <Vector>, <Angle α> ): Creates an infinite cone with given point as vertex, axis of symmetry parallel to the given vector and apex angle 2α.

description: Inserts all elements of the first list in the second list at the given position.

If the position is a negative number, then the position is counted from the right.

notes: If the position is a negative number, then the position is counted from the right.

description: Gives the indefinite integral with respect to the main variable.
notes: This command also shades the area between the function graph of f and the x-axis.

The answer isn't guaranteed to be continuous, eg Integral(floor(x)), that is the integral of the function ⌊x⌋ - in that case you can define your own function to use eg F(x)=(floor(x)² - floor(x))/2 + x floor(x) - floor(x)², i.e. the function (formula) in some versions of GeoGebra, a numerical algorithm is used so integrating up to an asypmtote or similar eg Integral(ln(x), 0, 1) won't work. In this case try Integral(ln(x), 0, 1, false)
search_text: Integral | [functions_and_calculus] | Integral( <Function> ): Gives the indefinite integral with respect to the main variable. | Examples: Integral(x³)

description: Gives the definite integral over the interval [Start x-Value , End x-Value] with respect to the main variable.
notes: This command also shades the area between the function graph of f and the x-axis.

The answer isn't guaranteed to be continuous, eg Integral(floor(x)), that is the integral of the function ⌊x⌋ - in that case you can define your own function to use eg F(x)=(floor(x)² - floor(x))/2 + x floor(x) - floor(x)², i.e. the function (formula) in some versions of GeoGebra, a numerical algorithm is used so integrating up to an asypmtote or similar eg Integral(ln(x), 0, 1) won't work. In this case try Integral(ln(x), 0, 1, false)
search_text: Integral | [functions_and_calculus] | Integral( <Function>, <Start x-Value>, <End x-Value> ): Gives the definite integral over the interval [Start x-Value , End x-Value] with respect to the main variable. | Examples: Integral(x³, 1, 2)

description: Gives the definite integral of the function over the interval [Start x-Value , End x-Value] with respect to the main variable and shades the related area if Evaluate is true. In case Evaluate is false the related area is shaded but the integral value is not calculated.
notes: This command also shades the area between the function graph of f and the x-axis.

The answer isn't guaranteed to be continuous, eg Integral(floor(x)), that is the integral of the function ⌊x⌋ - in that case you can define your own function to use eg F(x)=(floor(x)² - floor(x))/2 + x floor(x) - floor(x)², i.e. the function (formula) in some versions of GeoGebra, a numerical algorithm is used so integrating up to an asypmtote or similar eg Integral(ln(x), 0, 1) won't work. In this case try Integral(ln(x), 0, 1, false)
search_text: Integral | [functions_and_calculus] | Integral( <Function>, <Start x-Value>, <End x-Value>, <Boolean Evaluate> ): Gives the definite integral of the function over the interval [Start x-Value , End x-Value] with respect to the main variable and shades the related area if Evaluate is true. In case Evaluate is false the related area is shaded but the integral value is not calculated. | Examples: Integral(cos(a t), t)

description: Gives the partial integral with respect to the given variable.
notes: This command also shades the area between the function graph of f and the x-axis.

The answer isn't guaranteed to be continuous, eg Integral(floor(x)), that is the integral of the function ⌊x⌋ - in that case you can define your own function to use eg F(x)=(floor(x)² - floor(x))/2 + x floor(x) - floor(x)², i.e. the function (formula) in some versions of GeoGebra, a numerical algorithm is used so integrating up to an asypmtote or similar eg Integral(ln(x), 0, 1) won't work. In this case try Integral(ln(x), 0, 1, false)
search_text: Integral | [functions_and_calculus] | Integral( <Function>, <Variable> ): Gives the partial integral with respect to the given variable. | Examples: Integral(x³+3x y, x)

description: Gives the definite integral over the interval [Start Value , End Value] of the given variable.
notes: This command also shades the area between the function graph of f and the x-axis.

The answer isn't guaranteed to be continuous, eg Integral(floor(x)), that is the integral of the function ⌊x⌋ - in that case you can define your own function to use eg F(x)=(floor(x)² - floor(x))/2 + x floor(x) - floor(x)², i.e. the function (formula) in some versions of GeoGebra, a numerical algorithm is used so integrating up to an asypmtote or similar eg Integral(ln(x), 0, 1) won't work. In this case try Integral(ln(x), 0, 1, false)
search_text: Integral | [functions_and_calculus] | Integral( <Function>, <Variable>, <Start Value>, <End Value> ): Gives the definite integral over the interval [Start Value , End Value] of the given variable. | Examples: Integral(cos(t), t, a, b)

description: Gives the definite integral of the difference f(x) ‐ g(x) of two function f and g over the interval [a, b], where a is the first number and b the second, with respect to the main variable.
notes: This command also shades the area between the function graphs of f and g.
search_text: IntegralBetween | [functions_and_calculus] | IntegralBetween( <Function>, <Function>, <Number>, <Number> ): Gives the definite integral of the difference f(x) ‐ g(x) of two function f and g over the interval [a, b], where a is the first number and b the second, with respect to the main variable. | Examples: IntegralBetween(sin(x), cos(x), 0, pi)

description: Gives the definite integral of the difference f(x) ‐ g(x) of two function f and g over the interval [a, b], where a is the first number and b the second, with respect to the main variable.
notes: This command also shades the area between the function graphs of f and g.
search_text: IntegralBetween | [functions_and_calculus] | IntegralBetween( <Function>, <Function>, <Number>, <Number> ): Gives the definite integral of the difference f(x) ‐ g(x) of two function f and g over the interval [a, b], where a is the first number and b the second, with respect to the main variable. | Examples: IntegralBetween(sin(x), cos(x), pi / 4, pi * 5 / 4)

description: Gives the definite integral of the difference f(x) ‐ g(x) of two function f and g over the interval [a, b], where a is the first number and b the second, with respect to the main variable and shadows the related area if Evaluate is true. In case Evaluate is false the related area is shaded but the integral value is not calculated.
notes: This command also shades the area between the function graphs of f and g.
search_text: IntegralBetween | [functions_and_calculus] | IntegralBetween( <Function>, <Function>, <Number>, <Number>, <Boolean Evaluate> ): Gives the definite integral of the difference f(x) ‐ g(x) of two function f and g over the interval [a, b], where a is the first number and b the second, with respect to the main variable and shadows the related area if Evaluate is true. In case Evaluate is false the related area is shaded but the integral value is not calculated.

description: Gives the definite integral of a variable of the difference f(x) ‐ g(x) of two function f and g over the interval [a, b], where a is the first number and b the second, with respect to the given variable.
examples: IntegralBetween(a * sin(t), a * cos(t), t, pi / 4, pi * 5 / 4)
notes: This command also shades the area between the function graphs of f and g.
search_text: IntegralBetween | [functions_and_calculus] | IntegralBetween( <Function>, <Function>, <Variable>, <Number>, <Number> ): Gives the definite integral of a variable of the difference f(x) ‐ g(x) of two function f and g over the interval [a, b], where a is the first number and b the second, with respect to the given variable. | Examples: IntegralBetween(a * sin(t), a * cos(t), t, pi / 4, pi * 5 / 4)

description: Creates all the interior angles of the given polygon.

search_text: InteriorAngles | [3d] | InteriorAngles( <Polygon> ): Creates all the interior angles of the given polygon.

description: Finds one intersection point using a numerical, iterative method starting at the given parameters.
notes: to get all the intersection points in a list you can use eg {Intersect(a,b)} See also IntersectConic and IntersectPath commands. * See also Intersect tool.
search_text: Intersect | [3d] | Intersect( <Curve 1>, <Curve 2>, <Parameter 1>, <Parameter 2> ): Finds one intersection point using a numerical, iterative method starting at the given parameters. | Examples: a = Curve(cos(t), sin(t), t, 0, π); b = Curve(cos(t) + 1, sin(t), t, 0, π); Intersect(a, b, 0, 2)

description: Yields a list containing the intersection points of two objects.
notes: to get all the intersection points in a list you can use eg {Intersect(a,b)} See also IntersectConic and IntersectPath commands. * See also Intersect tool.
search_text: Intersect | [3d] | Intersect( <Function>, <Function> ): Yields a list containing the intersection points of two objects. | Examples: f(x):= x^3 + x^2 - x; g(x):= x; Intersect(f(x), g(x))

description: Yields the intersection points numerically for the two functions in the given interval.
notes: to get all the intersection points in a list you can use eg {Intersect(a,b)} See also IntersectConic and IntersectPath commands. * See also Intersect tool.
search_text: Intersect | [3d] | Intersect( <Function>, <Function>, <Start x-Value>, <End x-Value> ): Yields the intersection points numerically for the two functions in the given interval. | Examples: f(x) = x^3 + x^2 - x; g(x) = 4 / 5 + 3 / 5 x; Intersect(f, g, -1, 2)

description: Yields the intersection points of two objects.
notes: to get all the intersection points in a list you can use eg {Intersect(a,b)} See also IntersectConic and IntersectPath commands. * See also Intersect tool.
search_text: Intersect | [3d] | Intersect( <Object>, <Object> ): Yields the intersection points of two objects. | Examples: a: -3x + 7y = -10; c: x^2 + 2y^2 = 8; Intersect(a, c)

notes: to get all the intersection points in a list you can use eg {Intersect(a,b)} See also IntersectConic and IntersectPath commands. * See also Intersect tool.
search_text: Intersect | [3d] | Intersect( <Object>, <Object> ):  | Examples: Intersect( <Line> , <Object> ); Intersect( <Plane> , <Object> ); Intersect( <Conic>, <Conic> )

description: Yields the n^th^ intersection point of two objects. Each object must be a line, conic, polynomial function or implicit curve.
notes: to get all the intersection points in a list you can use eg {Intersect(a,b)} See also IntersectConic and IntersectPath commands. * See also Intersect tool.
search_text: Intersect | [3d] | Intersect( <Object>, <Object>, <Index of Intersection Point> ): Yields the n^th^ intersection point of two objects. Each object must be a line, conic, polynomial function or implicit curve. | Examples: a(x) = x^3 + x^2 - x; b: -3x + 5y = 4; Intersect(a, b, 2)

description: Yields an intersection point of two objects by using a numerical, iterative method with initial point.
notes: to get all the intersection points in a list you can use eg {Intersect(a,b)} See also IntersectConic and IntersectPath commands. * See also Intersect tool.
search_text: Intersect | [3d] | Intersect( <Object>, <Object>, <Initial Point> ): Yields an intersection point of two objects by using a numerical, iterative method with initial point. | Examples: a(x) = x^3 + x^2 - x; b: -3x + 5y = 4; Intersect(a, b, C)

description: Computes the inverse of the Beta cumulative distribution with parameters α and β for a given probability p. In other words, the command finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is a random variable with a Beta distribution. Probability p is any value in the interval [0,1].
notes: See also BetaDist command.
search_text: InverseBeta | [probability] | InverseBeta( <Number α>, <Number β>, <Probability> ): Computes the inverse of the Beta cumulative distribution with parameters α and β for a given probability p. In other words, the command finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is a random variable with a Beta distribution. Probability p is any value in the interval [0,1].

syntax: InverseBinomial( <Number of Trials>, <Probability of Success>, <Cumulative Probability> )
description: Returns least integer n such that P(X ≤ n) ≥ p, where p is the probability and X is https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binomial_distribution[binomial random variable] given by Number of Trials and Probability of Success.
notes: See also BinomialDist Command.
search_text: InverseBinomial | [probability] | InverseBinomial( <Number of Trials>, <Probability of Success>, <Cumulative Probability> ): Returns least integer n such that P(X ≤ n) ≥ p, where p is the probability and X is https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binomial_distribution[binomial random variable] given by Number of Trials and Probability of Success.

syntax: InverseBinomialMinimumTrials(Cumulative Probability, Probability of Success, Number of Successes)
description: Returns the minimum number n of trials to obtain the given number of successes.
search_text: InverseBinomialMinimumTrials | [probability] | InverseBinomialMinimumTrials(Cumulative Probability, Probability of Success, Number of Successes): Returns the minimum number n of trials to obtain the given number of successes.

description: Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cauchy_distribution[Cauchy distribution] at probability p, where the Cauchy distribution is defined by parameters median and scale. In other words, finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is a Cauchy random variable. Probability p must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].
search_text: InverseCauchy | [probability] | InverseCauchy( <Median>, <Scale>, <Probability> ): Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cauchy_distribution[Cauchy distribution] at probability p, where the Cauchy distribution is defined by parameters median and scale. In other words, finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is a Cauchy random variable. Probability p must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].

description: Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chi-square_distribution[Chi squared distribution] at probability p, where the Chi squared distribution is defined by the given degrees of freedom. In other words, it finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is a Chi squared random variable. Probability p must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].
search_text: InverseChiSquared | [probability] | InverseChiSquared( <Degrees of Freedom>, <Probability> ): Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chi-square_distribution[Chi squared distribution] at probability p, where the Chi squared distribution is defined by the given degrees of freedom. In other words, it finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is a Chi squared random variable. Probability p must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].

description: Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of an https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exponential_distribution[Exponential distribution] at probability p, where the Exponential distribution is defined by the given parameter lambda. In other words, finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is an Exponential random variable. Probability p must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].
search_text: InverseExponential | [probability] | InverseExponential( <Lambda>, <Probability> ): Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of an https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exponential_distribution[Exponential distribution] at probability p, where the Exponential distribution is defined by the given parameter lambda. In other words, finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is an Exponential random variable. Probability p must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].

syntax: InverseFDistribution( <Numerator Degrees of Freedom>, <Denominator Degrees of Freedom>, <Probability> )
description: Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of an https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/F-distribution[F-distribution] at probability p, where the F-distribution is defined by the given degrees of freedom. In other words, it finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is a random variable with an F-distribution. Probability p must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].
search_text: InverseFDistribution | [probability] | InverseFDistribution( <Numerator Degrees of Freedom>, <Denominator Degrees of Freedom>, <Probability> ): Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of an https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/F-distribution[F-distribution] at probability p, where the F-distribution is defined by the given degrees of freedom. In other words, it finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is a random variable with an F-distribution. Probability p must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].

description: Computes the inverse of the Gamma cumulative distribution with parameters α and β for a given probability p. In other words, the command finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is a random variable with a Gamma distribution. Probability p is any value in the interval [0,1].
search_text: InverseGamma | [probability] | InverseGamma( <Alpha>, <Beta>, <Probability> ): Computes the inverse of the Gamma cumulative distribution with parameters α and β for a given probability p. In other words, the command finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is a random variable with a Gamma distribution. Probability p is any value in the interval [0,1].

description: Returns least integer n such that P(X ≤ n) ≥ p, where p is the probability and X is https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hypergeometric_distribution[hypergeometric random variable] given by Population Size, Number of Successes and Sample Size.
notes: See also HyperGeometric Command.
search_text: InverseHyperGeometric | [probability] | InverseHyperGeometric( <Population Size>, <Number of Successes>, <Sample Size>, <Probability> ): Returns least integer n such that P(X ≤ n) ≥ p, where p is the probability and X is https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hypergeometric_distribution[hypergeometric random variable] given by Population Size, Number of Successes and Sample Size.

description: Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inverse_Laplace_transform[inverse Laplace transform] of the given function.
notes: See also Laplace command.
search_text: InverseLaplace | [functions_and_calculus] | InverseLaplace( <Function> ): Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inverse_Laplace_transform[inverse Laplace transform] of the given function. | Examples: InverseLaplace(1/(1+t^2))

description: Returns the inverse Laplace transform of the function, with respect to the given variable.
notes: See also Laplace command.
search_text: InverseLaplace | [functions_and_calculus] | InverseLaplace( <Function>, <Variable> ): Returns the inverse Laplace transform of the function, with respect to the given variable. | Examples: InverseLaplace( exp(- a*b),a); InverseLaplace( exp(- a*b),b)

description: Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Log-normal_distribution[log-normal distribution] at probability p, where the log-normal distribution is defined by the given parameters mean and standard deviation. In other words, it finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is a log-normal random variable. Probability p must be a value in the closed interval [0, 1].
search_text: InverseLogNormal | [probability] | InverseLogNormal( <Mean>, <Standard Deviation>, <Probability> ): Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Log-normal_distribution[log-normal distribution] at probability p, where the log-normal distribution is defined by the given parameters mean and standard deviation. In other words, it finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is a log-normal random variable. Probability p must be a value in the closed interval [0, 1]. | Examples: InverseLogNormal(10, 20, 1/3); InverseLogNormal(1000, 2, 1)

description: Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Logistic_distribution[Logistic distribution] at probability p, where the Logistic distribution is defined by the given parameters mean and scale. In other words, it finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is a Logistic random variable. Probability p must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].
search_text: InverseLogistic | [probability] | InverseLogistic( <Mean>, <Scale>, <Probability> ): Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Logistic_distribution[Logistic distribution] at probability p, where the Logistic distribution is defined by the given parameters mean and scale. In other words, it finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is a Logistic random variable. Probability p must be a value in the closed interval [0,1]. | Examples: InverseLogistic(100, 2, 1)

description: Evaluates the expression (formula) at given probability P, where (formula) is the inverse of the cumulative distribution function Φ for N(0,1), defined by the given parameters mean and standard deviation.
notes: Returns the x-coordinate of the point with the given probability (area) to the left, under the normal distribution curve.
search_text: InverseNormal | [probability] | InverseNormal( <Mean>, <Standard Deviation>, <Probability> ): Evaluates the expression (formula) at given probability P, where (formula) is the inverse of the cumulative distribution function Φ for N(0,1), defined by the given parameters mean and standard deviation. | Examples: InverseNormal(50, 2, 0.9)

description: Returns least integer n such that P(X≤n) ≥ p, where p is the probability and X is https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Negative_binomial_distribution[Pascal random variable] given by n and p.
notes: See also Pascal Command.
search_text:
InversePascal | [probability] | InversePascal( ,

, ): Returns least integer n such that P(X≤n) ≥ p, where p is the probability and X is https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Negative_binomial_distribution[Pascal random variable] given by n and p.

description: Returns the least integer n such that P(X≤n) ≥ p, where p is the given probability and X is a Poisson random variable with given mean.
notes: See also Poisson Command.
search_text: InversePoisson | [probability] | InversePoisson( <Mean>, <Probability> ): Returns the least integer n such that P(X≤n) ≥ p, where p is the given probability and X is a Poisson random variable with given mean.

description: Evaluates at p the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/T-distribution[t-distribution] with the given number of degrees of freedom. In other words, it finds r such that P(X≤r)=p, where X is a random variable with a t-distribution. Probability p must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].
search_text: InverseTDistribution | [probability] | InverseTDistribution( <Degrees of Freedom>, <Probability> ): Evaluates at p the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/T-distribution[t-distribution] with the given number of degrees of freedom. In other words, it finds r such that P(X≤r)=p, where X is a random variable with a t-distribution. Probability p must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].

description: Evaluates the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weibull_distribution[Weibull distribution] at given p, where the Weibull distribution is defined by the given parameters shape and scale. In other words, it finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is a random variable with Weibull distribution. Probability p must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].
search_text: InverseWeibull | [probability] | InverseWeibull( <Shape>, <Scale>, <Probability> ): Evaluates the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weibull_distribution[Weibull distribution] at given p, where the Weibull distribution is defined by the given parameters shape and scale. In other words, it finds t such that P(X ≤ t) = p, where X is a random variable with Weibull distribution. Probability p must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].

description: Returns the least integer n such that P(X≤n) ≥ p, where X is a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zipf_distribution[Zipf random variable] defined by the given number of elements and exponent, and p is the probability.
notes: See also Zipf Command.
search_text: InverseZipf | [probability] | InverseZipf( <Number of Elements>, <Exponent>, <Probability> ): Returns the least integer n such that P(X≤n) ≥ p, where X is a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zipf_distribution[Zipf random variable] defined by the given number of elements and exponent, and p is the probability.

description: Gives the inverse of the function.
notes: * In the CAS View undefined variables are allowed too.

No account is taken of domain or range, for example for f(x) = x^2^ or f(x) = sin(x). The command works faster for functions that only contain one x. To make your construction more efficient you may want to rearrange your functions and use eg `NInvert((x+1)^2-1)` rather than `NInvert(x^2+2x)`. * See also NInvert Command, Eigenvalues Command, Eigenvectors Command, SVD Command, Transpose Command, JordanDiagonalization Command
search_text: Invert | [functions_and_calculus] | Invert( <Function> ): Gives the inverse of the function. | Examples: Invert(sin(x))

description: Inverts the given matrix.
notes: * In the CAS View undefined variables are allowed too.

No account is taken of domain or range, for example for f(x) = x^2^ or f(x) = sin(x). The command works faster for functions that only contain one x. To make your construction more efficient you may want to rearrange your functions and use eg `NInvert((x+1)^2-1)` rather than `NInvert(x^2+2x)`. * See also NInvert Command, Eigenvalues Command, Eigenvectors Command, SVD Command, Transpose Command, JordanDiagonalization Command
search_text: Invert | [functions_and_calculus] | Invert( <Matrix> ): Inverts the given matrix. | Examples: Invert({{1, 2}, {3, 4}}); Invert({{a, b}, {c, d}})

description: Returns true or false depending on whether the object is defined or not.
search_text: IsDefined | [logic] | IsDefined( <Object> ): Returns true or false depending on whether the object is defined or not. | Examples: IsDefined(Circle((1,1), -2))

description: Returns ''true'' if the polynomial is factored in (formula) and ''false'' otherwise. In general, in order to consider a polynomial decomposition as factored, the coefficient of the leading term of each factor needs to be positive.
notes: This command differs among variants of English: IsFactored (US) IsFactorised (UK + Aus)
search_text: IsFactored | [algebra] | IsFactored( <Polynomial> ): Returns ''true'' if the polynomial is factored in (formula) and ''false'' otherwise. In general, in order to consider a polynomial decomposition as factored, the coefficient of the leading term of each factor needs to be positive.

description: Returns true if the point is in given region and false otherwise.
search_text: IsInRegion | [geometry] | IsInRegion( <Point>, <Region> ): Returns true if the point is in given region and false otherwise. | Examples: IsInRegion((1,2), Polygon((0,0), (2,0), (1,3)))

description: Returns true or false depending whether the number is an integer or not.
search_text: IsInteger | [algebra] | IsInteger( <Number> ): Returns true or false depending whether the number is an integer or not. | Examples: IsInteger(972 / 9)

description: Gives true or false depending on whether the number is prime or not.
search_text: IsPrime | [algebra] | IsPrime( <Number> ): Gives true or false depending on whether the number is prime or not. | Examples: IsPrime(10); IsPrime(11)

description: Gives true or false depending on whether the number is prime or not.
search_text: IsPrime | [algebra] | IsPrime( <Number> ): Gives true or false depending on whether the number is prime or not. | Examples: IsPrime(10); IsPrime(11)

description: Decides if the line is tangent to the conic. Normally this command computes the result numerically. This behavior can be changed by using the Prove command.
notes: See also AreCollinear, AreConcurrent, AreCongruent, AreConcyclic, AreEqual, AreParallel, ArePerpendicular commands.
search_text: IsTangent | [geometry] | IsTangent( <Line>, <Conic> ): Decides if the line is tangent to the conic. Normally this command computes the result numerically. This behavior can be changed by using the Prove command. | Examples: IsTangent(Line((0,0),(1,0)),Circle((0,1),1))

description: Checks if a function is written in vertex form.
search_text: IsVertexForm | [functions_and_calculus] | IsVertexForm(<function>): Checks if a function is written in vertex form. | Examples: IsVertexForm((x+2/3)^2-(2/3)^2); IsVertexForm(2*(3 x-2)^(2)+1)

description: Iterates the expression n times (n = number of iterations) using the given start value. The result is then the last element of the output of IterationList Command, with the same parameters.
notes: See IterationList Command for further details.
search_text: Iteration | [functions_and_calculus] | Iteration( <Expression>, <Variable Name>, ..., <Start Values>, <Number of Iterations> ): Iterates the expression n times (n = number of iterations) using the given start value. The result is then the last element of the output of IterationList Command, with the same parameters. | Examples: Iteration(a^2+1,a,{(1+ί)/(sqrt(2))},5)

description: Iterates the function n times (n = number of iterations) using the given start value.
notes: See IterationList Command for further details.
search_text: Iteration | [functions_and_calculus] | Iteration( <Function>, <Start Value>, <Number of Iterations> ): Iterates the function n times (n = number of iterations) using the given start value. | Examples: f(x) = x^2; Iteration(f, 3, 2); g(x) = x + 7

description: Gives you a list of length n+1 (n = number of iterations) whose elements are iterations of the expression starting with the given start value. In each iteration the variables in the expression are substituted by last elements of the list. There should be at least as many start values as there are variables, otherwise the result is undefined.
notes: Only the first syntax is supported in the CAS currently
See also Iteration Command.
search_text: IterationList | [functions_and_calculus] | IterationList( <Expression>, <Variable Name>, ..., <Start Values>, <Number of Iterations> ): Gives you a list of length n+1 (n = number of iterations) whose elements are iterations of the expression starting with the given start value. In each iteration the variables in the expression are substituted by last elements of the list. There should be at least as many start values as there are variables, otherwise the result is undefined. | Examples: IterationList(Midpoint(A, C), C, {B}, 3); IterationList(a + b, a, b, {f_0, f_1}, 5)

description: Gives you a list of length n+1 (n = number of iterations) whose elements are iterations of the function starting with the start value. You can also use this command to define a sequence where a~k+1~ depends on a~k~ and k. If the input function f is a function of two variables and start value is a list of two numbers {s, a~s~}, then the output list consists of numbers a~s~,a~s+1~,....,a~s+n~ where for k>s we have a~k+1~=f(k, a~k~).
notes: Only the first syntax is supported in the CAS currently
See also Iteration Command.
search_text: IterationList | [functions_and_calculus] | IterationList( <Function>, <Start Value>, <Number of Iterations> ): Gives you a list of length n+1 (n = number of iterations) whose elements are iterations of the function starting with the start value. You can also use this command to define a sequence where a~k+1~ depends on a~k~ and k. If the input function f is a function of two variables and start value is a list of two numbers {s, a~s~}, then the output list consists of numbers a~s~,a~s+1~,....,a~s+n~ where for k>s we have a~k+1~=f(k, a~k~). | Examples: f(x) = x^2; IterationList(f, 3, 2); f(k,a)=(k+1)*a

description: Joins the sub-lists into one longer list.
notes: The new list contains all elements of the initial lists even if they are the same. The elements of the new list are not re-ordered.
The new list contains all elements of the initial lists even if they are the same. The elements of the new list are not re-ordered.
search_text: Join | [list] | Join( <List of Lists> ): Joins the sub-lists into one longer list. | Examples: Join({{1, 2}}); Join({{1, 2, 3}, {3, 4}, {8, 7}})

description: Joins the two (or more) lists.
notes: The new list contains all elements of the initial lists even if they are the same. The elements of the new list are not re-ordered.
The new list contains all elements of the initial lists even if they are the same. The elements of the new list are not re-ordered.
search_text: Join | [list] | Join( <List>, <List>, ... ): Joins the two (or more) lists. | Examples: Join({5, 4, 3}, {1, 2, 3})

description: Decomposes the given matrix into the form S J S⁻¹ where J is in https://mathworld.wolfram.com/JordanCanonicalForm.html[Jordan Canonical Form]

search_text: JordanDiagonalization | [vector_and_matrix] | JordanDiagonalization( <Matrix> ): Decomposes the given matrix into the form S J S⁻¹ where J is in https://mathworld.wolfram.com/JordanCanonicalForm.html[Jordan Canonical Form] | Examples: JordanDiagonalization({{1, 2}, {3, 4}})

description: Creates a new list that only contains those elements of the initial list that fulfil the condition.
notes: For list of numbers arbitrary condition may be used. For list of other objects one can use only conditions of the form x==constant or x!=constant.

description: This syntax allows a more flexible condition.
notes: For list of numbers arbitrary condition may be used. For list of other objects one can use only conditions of the form x==constant or x!=constant.
search_text: KeepIf | [list] | KeepIf( <Condition>, <Variable>, <List> ): This syntax allows a more flexible condition. | Examples: KeepIf(x(A) < 3, A, {P, Q, R})

description: Gives a new list that contains the last element of the initial list. Last( <List>, <Number of elements> ) Gives a new list that contains just the last n elements of the initial list.

description: Yields the length of the curve between the two points that lie on the curve.

description: Computes the limit of the expression for the given value of the main function variable.
notes: Not all limits can be calculated by GeoGebra, so undefined will be returned in those cases (as well as when the correct result is undefined).

Not all limits can be calculated by GeoGebra, so ? will be returned in those cases (as well as when the correct result is undefined). If you want the limit of a piecewise-defined function you need to use LimitAbove or LimitBelow, for example LimitAbove(If(x>1, x^2, -2x), 1) * See also Asymptote Command, LimitAbove Command and LimitBelow Command.
search_text: Limit | [functions_and_calculus] | Limit( <Expression>, <Value> ): Computes the limit of the expression for the given value of the main function variable. | Examples: Limit(a sin(x) / x, 0)

description: Computes the limit of the expression for the given value of the given function variable.

description: Calculates the linear eccentricity of the conic section. For ellipses or hyperbolas the command gives the distance between the conic's center and one of its foci, for circles it gives 0, and for parabolas gives the distance between its focus and the vertex.
search_text: LinearEccentricity | [conic] | LinearEccentricity( <Conic> ): Calculates the linear eccentricity of the conic section. For ellipses or hyperbolas the command gives the distance between the conic's center and one of its foci, for circles it gives 0, and for parabolas gives the distance between its focus and the vertex. | Examples: LinearEccentricity(4x^2 - y^2 + 16x + 20 = 0)

search_text: Locus | [geometry] | Locus( <Point Creating Locus Line Q>, <Point P>): Returns the locus curve of the point Q, which depends on the point P.

description: Returns the locus curve which equates to the slopefield at the given point.
notes: Point P needs to be a point on an object (e. g. line, segment, circle).
See also Locus tool.
search_text: Locus | [geometry] | Locus( <Slopefield>, <Point> ): Returns the locus curve which equates to the slopefield at the given point.

description: Returns the locus curve which equates to the solution of the differential equation (formula) in the given point. The solution is calculated numerically. Loci are specific object types, and appear as auxiliary objects. Besides Locus command, they are the result of some Discrete Math Commands and SolveODE Command. Loci are paths and can be used within path-related commands such as Point. Their properties depend on how they were obtained, see e.g. Perimeter Command and First Command. Warning A locus is undefined when the dependent point is the result of a Point Command with two parameters, or a PathParameter Command.
notes: Point P needs to be a point on an object (e. g. line, segment, circle).
See also Locus tool.
search_text: Locus | [geometry] | Locus( <f(x, y)>, <Point> ): Returns the locus curve which equates to the solution of the differential equation (formula) in the given point. The solution is calculated numerically. Loci are specific object types, and appear as auxiliary objects. Besides Locus command, they are the result of some Discrete Math Commands and SolveODE Command. Loci are paths and can be used within path-related commands such as Point. Their properties depend on how they were obtained, see e.g. Perimeter Command and First Command. Warning A locus is undefined when the dependent point is the result of a Point Command with two parameters, or a PathParameter Command.

description: Calculates the locus of the free point such that the boolean condition is satisified.
notes: The Locus must be made from a Point (not from a Slider) Works only for a restricted set of geometric loci, i.e. using points, lines, circles, conics. (Rays and line segments will be treated as (infinite) lines.) If the locus is too complicated then it will return 'undefined'. If there is no locus then the implicit curve is the empty set 0=1. If the locus is the whole plane then the implicit curve is the equation 0=0. The calculation is done using https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gr%C3%B6bner_basis[Gröbner bases], so sometimes extra branches of the curve will appear that were not in the original locus. Further informations and examples on https://www.geogebra.org/m/KZVzqVEM[geogebra.org]. A https://www.geogebra.org/m/mbXQuvUV[collection of implicit locus examples] is also available. See also Locus command and https://github.com/kovzol/gg-art-doc/tree/master/pdf/english.pdf[GeoGebra Automated Reasoning Tools: A Tutorial].
search_text: LocusEquation | [geometry] | LocusEquation( <Boolean Expression>, <Free Point> ): Calculates the locus of the free point such that the boolean condition is satisified. | Examples: LocusEquation(AreCollinear(A, B, C), A)

description: Calculates the equation of a Locus and plots this as an Implicit Curve.
notes: The Locus must be made from a Point (not from a Slider) Works only for a restricted set of geometric loci, i.e. using points, lines, circles, conics. (Rays and line segments will be treated as (infinite) lines.) If the locus is too complicated then it will return 'undefined'. If there is no locus then the implicit curve is the empty set 0=1. If the locus is the whole plane then the implicit curve is the equation 0=0. The calculation is done using https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gr%C3%B6bner_basis[Gröbner bases], so sometimes extra branches of the curve will appear that were not in the original locus. Further informations and examples on https://www.geogebra.org/m/KZVzqVEM[geogebra.org]. A https://www.geogebra.org/m/mbXQuvUV[collection of implicit locus examples] is also available. See also Locus command and https://github.com/kovzol/gg-art-doc/tree/master/pdf/english.pdf[GeoGebra Automated Reasoning Tools: A Tutorial].
search_text: LocusEquation | [geometry] | LocusEquation( <Locus> ): Calculates the equation of a Locus and plots this as an Implicit Curve.

syntax: LocusEquation( <Point Creating Locus Line Q>, <Point P> )
description: Calculates the equation of a Locus by using inputs tracer point Q and mover point P, and plots this as an Implicit Curve.

(Rays and line segments will be treated as (infinite) lines.) If the locus is too complicated then it will return 'undefined'. If there is no locus then the implicit curve is the empty set 0=1. If the locus is the whole plane then the implicit curve is the equation 0=0. The calculation is done using https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gr%C3%B6bner_basis[Gröbner bases], so sometimes extra branches of the curve will appear that were not in the original locus. Further informations and examples on https://www.geogebra.org/m/KZVzqVEM[geogebra.org]. A https://www.geogebra.org/m/mbXQuvUV[collection of implicit locus examples] is also available. See also Locus command and https://github.com/kovzol/gg-art-doc/tree/master/pdf/english.pdf[GeoGebra Automated Reasoning Tools: A Tutorial].
search_text: LocusEquation | [geometry] | LocusEquation( <Point Creating Locus Line Q>, <Point P> ): Calculates the equation of a Locus by using inputs tracer point Q and mover point P, and plots this as an Implicit Curve. | Examples: LocusEquation(Q,P)

description: Evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a log-normal distribution at variable value, i.e. calculates the probability P(X ≤ v) where X is a random variable with a log-normal distribution defined by the given parameters mean and standard deviation.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given value, that is the area under the log-normal distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: LogNormal | [probability] | LogNormal( <Mean>, <Standard Deviation>, <Variable Value> ): Evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a log-normal distribution at variable value, i.e. calculates the probability P(X ≤ v) where X is a random variable with a log-normal distribution defined by the given parameters mean and standard deviation.

description: If Cumulative = true, evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a log-normal distribution with given mean and standard deviation at the given variable value, otherwise it evaluates the probability density function of the distribution at variable value.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given value, that is the area under the log-normal distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: LogNormal | [probability] | LogNormal( <Mean>, <Standard Deviation>, <Variable Value>, <Boolean Cumulative> ): If Cumulative = true, evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a log-normal distribution with given mean and standard deviation at the given variable value, otherwise it evaluates the probability density function of the distribution at variable value.

description: If Cumulative = true, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of a log-normal distribution with given mean and standard deviation, otherwise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given value, that is the area under the log-normal distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: LogNormal | [probability] | LogNormal( <Mean>, <Standard Deviation>, x, <Boolean Cumulative> ): If Cumulative = true, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of a log-normal distribution with given mean and standard deviation, otherwise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.

search_text: Logistic | [probability] | Logistic( <Mean>, <Scale>, <Variable Value>, <Boolean Cumulative> ): If Cumulative = true, evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a logistic distribution with given mean and scale at the given variable value, otherwise it evaluates the probability density function of the distribution at variable value.

description: If Cumulative = true, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of a logistic distribution with given mean and scale, otherwise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.

description: Calculates (numerically) the local maximum point of the function in the given interval. The function should be continuous and have only one local maximum point in the interval (and no local minimum).
notes: If the input consists of non-numeric objects, then this command considers the numbers associated with those objects. If you have a list of segments for example, the command Max( <List> ) will yield the maximum segment length.
Open and closed intervals are treated the same.
For polynomials you should use the Extremum Command.
If you want the maximum of two functions f(x) and g(x) then you can define (f(x) + g(x) + abs(f(x) - g(x)))/2 See also Extremum Command, Min Command and Function Inspector Tool.
search_text: Max | [algebra] | Max( <Function>, <Start x-Value>, <End x-Value> ): Calculates (numerically) the local maximum point of the function in the given interval. The function should be continuous and have only one local maximum point in the interval (and no local minimum). | Examples: Max(exp(x)x^2,-3,-1)

description: Calculates the position of the point which gives the maximal value of the dependent number. The point must be a point on a path and the path will be used as the search interval. The relationship should be continuous and have only one local maximum point in the interval. If the construction is complicated, this command might return ? to avoid using too much processor time. [Example] ==== Let c be a circle, C a point on it and D a point outside the circle. If f = Segment(C,D) then Maximize(f,C) creates the point on c having maximum distance from D. ====
notes: This command differs among variants of English: Maximize (US) Maximise (UK + Aus)
See also Minimize command.
search_text: Maximize | [optimization] | Maximize( <Dependent Number>, <Point on Path> ): Calculates the position of the point which gives the maximal value of the dependent number. The point must be a point on a path and the path will be used as the search interval. The relationship should be continuous and have only one local maximum point in the interval. If the construction is complicated, this command might return ? to avoid using too much processor time. [Example] ==== Let c be a circle, C a point on it and D a point outside the circle. If f = Segment(C,D) then Maximize(f,C) creates the point on c having maximum distance from D. ====

description: Calculates the free number which gives the maximal value of the dependent number. The free number must be a slider and the slider interval will be used as the search interval. The relationship should be continuous and have only one local maximum point in the interval. If the construction is complicated, this command might return ? to avoid using too much processor time. [Example] ==== Let a be a slider in [0,10] and t1 the right triangle with vertices C=(a, 0), A=(0, 0) and B=(0,10 - a). Maximize(t1,a) gives 5, the value of a that maximizes the area of t1. ====
notes: This command differs among variants of English: Maximize (US) Maximise (UK + Aus)
See also Minimize command.
search_text: Maximize | [optimization] | Maximize( <Dependent number>, <Free number> ): Calculates the free number which gives the maximal value of the dependent number. The free number must be a slider and the slider interval will be used as the search interval. The relationship should be continuous and have only one local maximum point in the interval. If the construction is complicated, this command might return ? to avoid using too much processor time. [Example] ==== Let a be a slider in [0,10] and t1 the right triangle with vertices C=(a, 0), A=(0, 0) and B=(0,10 - a). Maximize(t1,a) gives 5, the value of a that maximizes the area of t1. ====
