search_text: Median | [statistics] | Median( <List of Raw Data> ): Determines the median of the list elements. | Examples: Median({1, 2, 3}); Median({1, 1, 8, 8})

description: Returns the center of the conic.
notes: See also Midpoint or Center tool.
search_text: Midpoint | [3d] | Midpoint( <Conic> ): Returns the center of the conic. | Examples: Midpoint(x^2 + y^2 = 4)

description: Returns the midpoint of the interval (as number).
notes: See also Midpoint or Center tool.
search_text: Midpoint | [3d] | Midpoint( <Interval> ): Returns the midpoint of the interval (as number). | Examples: Midpoint(2 < x < 4)

description: Returns the midpoint of two points.
notes: See also Midpoint or Center tool.
search_text: Midpoint | [3d] | Midpoint( <Point>, <Point> ): Returns the midpoint of two points. | Examples: Midpoint((1, 1), (5, 1))

description: Returns the midpoint of the given quadric (e.g. sphere, cone, etc.)
notes: See also Midpoint or Center tool.
search_text: Midpoint | [3d] | Midpoint( <Quadric> ): Returns the midpoint of the given quadric (e.g. sphere, cone, etc.) | Examples: Midpoint(x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1)

description: Returns the midpoint of the segment.
notes: See also Midpoint or Center tool.
search_text: Midpoint | [3d] | Midpoint( <Segment> ): Returns the midpoint of the segment. | Examples: s = Segment((1, 1), (1, 5)); Midpoint(s)

description: Calculates (numerically) the local minimum point for function in the given interval. Function should be continuous and have only one local minimum point in the interval (and no local maximum).
notes: If the input consists of non-numeric objects, then this command considers the numbers associated with those objects. If you have a list of segments for example, the command Min( <List> ) will yield the minimum segment length.
Open and closed intervals are not distinguished.
For polynomials you should use the Extremum Command.
If you want the minimum of two functions f(x) and g(x) then you can define (f(x) + g(x) - abs(f(x) - g(x)))/2 See also Max Command, Extremum Command and Function Inspector Tool.
search_text: Min | [algebra] | Min( <Function>, <Start x-Value>, <End x-Value> ): Calculates (numerically) the local minimum point for function in the given interval. Function should be continuous and have only one local minimum point in the interval (and no local maximum). | Examples: Min(exp(x) x^3,-4,-2)

description: Unlike in the Algebra View, this syntax will give the minimum over the interval, including endpoints
notes: If the input consists of non-numeric objects, then this command considers the numbers associated with those objects. If you have a list of segments for example, the command Min( <List> ) will yield the minimum segment length.
Open and closed intervals are not distinguished.
For polynomials you should use the Extremum Command.
If you want the minimum of two functions f(x) and g(x) then you can define (f(x) + g(x) - abs(f(x) - g(x)))/2 See also Max Command, Extremum Command and Function Inspector Tool.
search_text: Min | [algebra] | Min( <Function>, <Start x-Value>, <End x-Value> ): Unlike in the Algebra View, this syntax will give the minimum over the interval, including endpoints | Examples: Min(x^2,-1,2); Min(-x^2,-1,2)

description: Returns the lower bound of the interval.
notes: If the input consists of non-numeric objects, then this command considers the numbers associated with those objects. If you have a list of segments for example, the command Min( <List> ) will yield the minimum segment length.
Open and closed intervals are not distinguished.
For polynomials you should use the Extremum Command.
If you want the minimum of two functions f(x) and g(x) then you can define (f(x) + g(x) - abs(f(x) - g(x)))/2 See also Max Command, Extremum Command and Function Inspector Tool.
search_text: Min | [algebra] | Min( <Interval> ): Returns the lower bound of the interval. | Examples: Min(2 < x < 3)

description: Returns the minimum of the list of data with corresponding frequencies.
notes: If the input consists of non-numeric objects, then this command considers the numbers associated with those objects. If you have a list of segments for example, the command Min( <List> ) will yield the minimum segment length.
Open and closed intervals are not distinguished.
For polynomials you should use the Extremum Command.
If you want the minimum of two functions f(x) and g(x) then you can define (f(x) + g(x) - abs(f(x) - g(x)))/2 See also Max Command, Extremum Command and Function Inspector Tool.
search_text: Min | [algebra] | Min( <List of Data>, <List of Frequencies> ): Returns the minimum of the list of data with corresponding frequencies. | Examples: Min({1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, {0, 3, 4, 2, 3})

description: Returns the minimum of the numbers within the list.
notes: If the input consists of non-numeric objects, then this command considers the numbers associated with those objects. If you have a list of segments for example, the command Min( <List> ) will yield the minimum segment length.
Open and closed intervals are not distinguished.
For polynomials you should use the Extremum Command.
If you want the minimum of two functions f(x) and g(x) then you can define (f(x) + g(x) - abs(f(x) - g(x)))/2 See also Max Command, Extremum Command and Function Inspector Tool.
search_text: Min | [algebra] | Min( <List> ): Returns the minimum of the numbers within the list. | Examples: Min({-2, 12, -23, 17, 15})

description: Returns the minimum of the two given numbers.
notes: If the input consists of non-numeric objects, then this command considers the numbers associated with those objects. If you have a list of segments for example, the command Min( <List> ) will yield the minimum segment length.
Open and closed intervals are not distinguished.
For polynomials you should use the Extremum Command.
If you want the minimum of two functions f(x) and g(x) then you can define (f(x) + g(x) - abs(f(x) - g(x)))/2 See also Max Command, Extremum Command and Function Inspector Tool.
search_text: Min | [algebra] | Min( <Number>, <Number> ): Returns the minimum of the two given numbers. | Examples: Min(12, 15)

description: Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minimal_polynomial_(linear_algebra)[minimal polynomial] of the given matrix.
search_text: MinimalPolynomial | [vector_and_matrix] | MinimalPolynomial( <Matrix> ): Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minimal_polynomial_(linear_algebra)[minimal polynomial] of the given matrix. | Examples: MinimalPolynomial({{1,0},{0,1}})

description: Calculates the position of the point which gives the minimal value of the dependent number. The point must be a point on a path and the path will be used as the search interval. The relationship should be continuous and have only one local minimum point in the interval. If the construction is complicated, this command might return ? to avoid using too much processor time.
notes: This command differs among variants of English: Minimize (US) Minimise (UK + Aus)
See also Maximize command.
search_text: Minimize | [optimization] | Minimize( <Dependent Number>, <Point on Path> ): Calculates the position of the point which gives the minimal value of the dependent number. The point must be a point on a path and the path will be used as the search interval. The relationship should be continuous and have only one local minimum point in the interval. If the construction is complicated, this command might return ? to avoid using too much processor time. | Examples: f = Segment(C,D); Minimize(f,C)

description: Calculates the free number which gives the minimal value of the dependent number. The free number must be a slider and the slider interval will be used as the search interval. The relationship should be continuous and have only one local minimum in the interval. If the construction is complicated, this command might return ? to avoid using too much processor time.
notes: This command differs among variants of English: Minimize (US) Minimise (UK + Aus)
See also Maximize command.
search_text: Minimize | [optimization] | Minimize( <Dependent number>, <Free number> ): Calculates the free number which gives the minimal value of the dependent number. The free number must be a slider and the slider interval will be used as the search interval. The relationship should be continuous and have only one local minimum in the interval. If the construction is complicated, this command might return ? to avoid using too much processor time. | Examples: Minimize(t1, a)

description: Returns the minimum spanning tree of a complete graph on given vertices in which weight of edge (u,v) is the Euclidian distance between u and v. The resulting object is a locus.
search_text: MinimumSpanningTree | [discrete_math] | MinimumSpanningTree( <List of Points> ): Returns the minimum spanning tree of a complete graph on given vertices in which weight of edge (u,v) is the Euclidian distance between u and v. The resulting object is a locus.

description: Returns the equation of the minor axis of the conic section.
notes: See also MajorAxis command.
search_text: MinorAxis | [conic] | MinorAxis( <Conic> ): Returns the equation of the minor axis of the conic section. | Examples: MinorAxis(x^2 / 9 + y^2 / 4 = 1)

description: Converts the given number to a mixed number.
notes: See also Rationalize Command.
search_text: MixedNumber | [algebra] | MixedNumber( <Number> ): Converts the given number to a mixed number. | Examples: MixedNumber(3.5); MixedNumber(12 / 3); MixedNumber(12 / 14)

description: Yields the remainder when dividend number is divided by divisor number.
notes: If you want a function to do this, you can define it yourself, e.g. mod(x, y) = y (x / y - floor(x / y)).
search_text: Mod | [algebra] | Mod( <Dividend Number>, <Divisor Number> ): Yields the remainder when dividend number is divided by divisor number. | Examples: Mod(9, 4)

description: Yields the remainder when the dividend polynomial is divided by the divisor polynomial.
notes: If you want a function to do this, you can define it yourself, e.g. mod(x, y) = y (x / y - floor(x / y)).
search_text: Mod | [algebra] | Mod( <Dividend Polynomial>, <Divisor Polynomial> ): Yields the remainder when the dividend polynomial is divided by the divisor polynomial. | Examples: Mod(x^3 + x^2 + x + 6, x^2 - 3)

description: Determines the mode(s) of the list elements.
search_text: Mode | [statistics] | Mode( <List of Numbers> ): Determines the mode(s) of the list elements. | Examples: Mode({1, 2, 3, 4}); Mode({1, 1, 1, 2, 3, 4}); Mode({1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4})

description: Returns the modular exponent of the given numbers. See also https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Modular_exponentiation[Modular exponentiation] for further details.
search_text: ModularExponent | [algebra] | ModularExponent( <Number>, <Number>, <Number> ): Returns the modular exponent of the given numbers. See also https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Modular_exponentiation[Modular exponentiation] for further details. | Examples: ModularExponent(5,12,13)

description: Plots the 1st derivative of the given function, calculated numerically.
search_text: NDerivative | [functions_and_calculus] | NDerivative( <Function> ): Plots the 1st derivative of the given function, calculated numerically. | Examples: NDerivative(x^4+2x^3-2x+1)

description: Plots the nth derivative of the given function, calculated numerically.
search_text: NDerivative | [functions_and_calculus] | NDerivative( <Function>, <Order n> ): Plots the nth derivative of the given function, calculated numerically.

description: Plots the graph of the indefinite integral (formula) of the given function, with constant of integration c = 0. The equation of the primitive is not shown in Algebra View, because it is computed numerically.
notes: Hint: In the CAS View the following syntax can also be used: NIntegral( <Function>, <Variable>, <Start Value>, <End Value> ):: Computes (numerically) the definite integral (formula) of the given function f, from a (Start value) to b (End value), with respect to the given variable.
search_text: NIntegral | [functions_and_calculus] | NIntegral( <Function> ): Plots the graph of the indefinite integral (formula) of the given function, with constant of integration c = 0. The equation of the primitive is not shown in Algebra View, because it is computed numerically.

description: Computes (numerically) and plots the definite integral (formula) of the given function f, from a (Start x-Value) to b (End x-Value).
notes: Hint: In the CAS View the following syntax can also be used: NIntegral( <Function>, <Variable>, <Start Value>, <End Value> ):: Computes (numerically) the definite integral (formula) of the given function f, from a (Start value) to b (End value), with respect to the given variable.
search_text: NIntegral | [functions_and_calculus] | NIntegral( <Function>, <Start x-Value>, <End x-Value> ): Computes (numerically) and plots the definite integral (formula) of the given function f, from a (Start x-Value) to b (End x-Value). | Examples: NIntegral(ℯ^(-x^2), 0, 1)

description: Computes (numerically) the indefinite integral of the given function, and plots the graph of that function through (Start x-Value, Start y-Value), with end point at (End x-Value).
notes: Hint: In the CAS View the following syntax can also be used: NIntegral( <Function>, <Variable>, <Start Value>, <End Value> ):: Computes (numerically) the definite integral (formula) of the given function f, from a (Start value) to b (End value), with respect to the given variable.
search_text: NIntegral | [functions_and_calculus] | NIntegral( <Function>, <Start x-Value>, <Start y-Value>, <End x-Value> ): Computes (numerically) the indefinite integral of the given function, and plots the graph of that function through (Start x-Value, Start y-Value), with end point at (End x-Value). | Examples: NIntegral(sin(x)/x, π, 1, 2π); NIntegral(ℯ^(-a^2), a, 0, 1)

description: Gives the inverse of the function without showing the inverted formula. If you want to get the formula, use the Invert Command instead.
notes: No account is taken of domain or range, for example for f(x) = x^2^ or f(x) = sin(x). The command works faster for functions that only contain one x. To make your construction more efficient you may want to rearrange your functions and use eg `NInvert((x+1)^2-1)` rather than `NInvert(x^2+2x)`.
search_text: NInvert | [functions_and_calculus] | NInvert( <Function> ): Gives the inverse of the function without showing the inverted formula. If you want to get the formula, use the Invert Command instead. | Examples: NInvert(sin(x))

description: Attempts (numerically) to find a solution for the equation for the main variable. For non-polynomials you should always specify a starting value (see below)
notes: If you don't give a starting point like a=3 or {x = 3, y = 1.5} the numerical algorithm may find it hard to find a solution (and giving a starting point doesn't guarantee that a solution will be found) The number of decimals depends on the choosen in global rounding. NSolutions won't work for functions that are asymptotic to the x-axis. They can often be reformulated though. NSolutions will work only if the function is continuous * See also Solutions Command and NSolve Command.
search_text: NSolutions | [algebra] | NSolutions( <Equation> ): Attempts (numerically) to find a solution for the equation for the main variable. For non-polynomials you should always specify a starting value (see below) | Examples: NSolutions(x^6 - 2x + 1 = 0)

description: Finds numerically the list of solutions to the given equation for the given unknown variable with its starting value.
notes: If you don't give a starting point like a=3 or {x = 3, y = 1.5} the numerical algorithm may find it hard to find a solution (and giving a starting point doesn't guarantee that a solution will be found) The number of decimals depends on the choosen in global rounding. NSolutions won't work for functions that are asymptotic to the x-axis. They can often be reformulated though. NSolutions will work only if the function is continuous * See also Solutions Command and NSolve Command.
search_text: NSolutions | [algebra] | NSolutions( <Equation>, <Variable = starting value> ): Finds numerically the list of solutions to the given equation for the given unknown variable with its starting value. | Examples: NSolutions(cos(x) = x, x = 0); NSolutions(a^4 + 34a^3 = 34, a = 3)

description: Attempts (numerically) to find a solution of the equation for the given unknown variable. For non-polynomials you should always specify a starting value (see below)
notes: If you don't give a starting point like a=3 or {x = 3, y = 1.5} the numerical algorithm may find it hard to find a solution (and giving a starting point doesn't guarantee that a solution will be found) The number of decimals depends on the choosen in global rounding. NSolutions won't work for functions that are asymptotic to the x-axis. They can often be reformulated though. NSolutions will work only if the function is continuous * See also Solutions Command and NSolve Command.
search_text: NSolutions | [algebra] | NSolutions( <Equation>, <Variable> ): Attempts (numerically) to find a solution of the equation for the given unknown variable. For non-polynomials you should always specify a starting value (see below) | Examples: NSolutions(a^4 + 34a^3 = 34, a)

description: Attempts (numerically) to find a solution of the set of equations for the given set of unknown variables.
examples: NSolutions({pi / x = cos(x - 2y), 2 y - pi = sin(x)}, {x = 3, y = 1.5})
notes: If you don't give a starting point like a=3 or {x = 3, y = 1.5} the numerical algorithm may find it hard to find a solution (and giving a starting point doesn't guarantee that a solution will be found) The number of decimals depends on the choosen in global rounding. NSolutions won't work for functions that are asymptotic to the x-axis. They can often be reformulated though. NSolutions will work only if the function is continuous * See also Solutions Command and NSolve Command.
search_text: NSolutions | [algebra] | NSolutions( <List of Equations>, <List of Variables> ): Attempts (numerically) to find a solution of the set of equations for the given set of unknown variables. | Examples: NSolutions({pi / x = cos(x - 2y), 2 y - pi = sin(x)}, {x = 3, y = 1.5})

description: Attempts (numerically) to find a solution for the equation for the main variable. For non-polynomials you should always specify a starting value (see below).
notes: If you don't give a starting point like a=3 or {x = 3, y = 1.5} the numerical algorithm may find it hard to find a solution (and giving a starting point doesn't guarantee that a solution will be found) The number of decimals depends on the choosen in global rounding. NSolve won't work for functions that are asymptotic to the x-axis or other extreme examples. They can often be reformulated though. NSolve will work only if the function is continuous! * See also Solve Command and NSolutions Command.
search_text: NSolve | [algebra] | NSolve( <Equation> ): Attempts (numerically) to find a solution for the equation for the main variable. For non-polynomials you should always specify a starting value (see below). | Examples: NSolve(x^6 - 2x + 1 = 0)

description: Finds numerically the list of solutions to the given equation for the given unknown variable with its starting value.
notes: If you don't give a starting point like a=3 or {x = 3, y = 1.5} the numerical algorithm may find it hard to find a solution (and giving a starting point doesn't guarantee that a solution will be found) The number of decimals depends on the choosen in global rounding. NSolve won't work for functions that are asymptotic to the x-axis or other extreme examples. They can often be reformulated though. NSolve will work only if the function is continuous! * See also Solve Command and NSolutions Command.
search_text: NSolve | [algebra] | NSolve( <Equation>, <Variable = starting value> ): Finds numerically the list of solutions to the given equation for the given unknown variable with its starting value. | Examples: NSolve(cos(x) = x, x = 0); NSolve(a^4 + 34a^3 = 34, a = 3)

description: Attempts (numerically) to find a solution of the equation for the given unknown variable. For non-polynomials you should always specify a starting value (see below).
notes: If you don't give a starting point like a=3 or {x = 3, y = 1.5} the numerical algorithm may find it hard to find a solution (and giving a starting point doesn't guarantee that a solution will be found) The number of decimals depends on the choosen in global rounding. NSolve won't work for functions that are asymptotic to the x-axis or other extreme examples. They can often be reformulated though. NSolve will work only if the function is continuous! * See also Solve Command and NSolutions Command.
search_text: NSolve | [algebra] | NSolve( <Equation>, <Variable> ): Attempts (numerically) to find a solution of the equation for the given unknown variable. For non-polynomials you should always specify a starting value (see below). | Examples: NSolve(a^4 + 34a^3 = 34, a)

description: Attempts (numerically) to find a solution of the set of equations for the given set of unknown variables.
examples: NSolve({pi / x = cos(x - 2y), 2 y - pi = sin(x)}, {x = 3, y = 1.5})
notes: If you don't give a starting point like a=3 or {x = 3, y = 1.5} the numerical algorithm may find it hard to find a solution (and giving a starting point doesn't guarantee that a solution will be found) The number of decimals depends on the choosen in global rounding. NSolve won't work for functions that are asymptotic to the x-axis or other extreme examples. They can often be reformulated though. NSolve will work only if the function is continuous! * See also Solve Command and NSolutions Command.
search_text: NSolve | [algebra] | NSolve( <List of Equations>, <List of Variables> ): Attempts (numerically) to find a solution of the set of equations for the given set of unknown variables. | Examples: NSolve({pi / x = cos(x - 2y), 2 y - pi = sin(x)}, {x = 3, y = 1.5})

syntax: NSolveODE( <List of Derivatives>, <Initial x-coordinate>, <List of Initial y-coordinates>, <Final x-coordinate> )
description: Solves (numerically) the system of differential equations
examples: h'(t, f, g, h) = -t h + 3t g + 2f + t
c = Slider(0, 1, 1 / len, 1, 100, false, true, true, false)
notes: See also SlopeField command, SolveODE command.
search_text: NSolveODE | [functions_and_calculus] | NSolveODE( <List of Derivatives>, <Initial x-coordinate>, <List of Initial y-coordinates>, <Final x-coordinate> ): Solves (numerically) the system of differential equations | Examples: f'(t, f, g, h) = g; g'(t, f, g, h) = h; h'(t, f, g, h) = -t h + 3t g + 2f + t

description: Returns the name of an object as a text in the Graphics View.
notes: This command works properly only in dynamic text for objects (so that they work after objects are renamed). The Name command is the opposite of the Object command.
search_text: Name | [geogebra_general] | Name( <Object> ): Returns the name of an object as a text in the Graphics View.

description: Creates the net of a convex polyhedron, on the plane containing the face used for its construction. The number is used to define the progress of the unfolding procedure, and needs to be between 0 and 1. The net is totally unfold when the given number is 1.
notes: The net of a cube is displayed as Latin cross.
To explore the different configurations of the net of a cube, see http://geogebra.org/material/show/id/136596[this example file] on GeoGebra. See also Net tool.
search_text: Net | [3d] | Net( <Polyhedron> , <Number> ): Creates the net of a convex polyhedron, on the plane containing the face used for its construction. The number is used to define the progress of the unfolding procedure, and needs to be between 0 and 1. The net is totally unfold when the given number is 1.

description: Applicable only to cubes (for the moment), allows you to create different nets of a cube, by specifying the face and edges that need to be cut to create the net.
notes: The net of a cube is displayed as Latin cross.
To explore the different configurations of the net of a cube, see http://geogebra.org/material/show/id/136596[this example file] on GeoGebra. See also Net tool.
search_text: Net | [3d] | Net( <Polyhedron>, <Number>, <Face>, <Edge>, <Edge>, ... ): Applicable only to cubes (for the moment), allows you to create different nets of a cube, by specifying the face and edges that need to be cut to create the net.

description: Returns the smallest prime greater than the entered number.
notes: See also PreviousPrime Command.
search_text: NextPrime | [algebra] | NextPrime( <Number> ): Returns the smallest prime greater than the entered number. | Examples: NextPrime(10000)

syntax: Normal( <Mean>, <Standard Deviation>, <Variable Value u> , <Variable Value v>)
description: Computes the probability of a normal random variable in the interval [u, v], given the mean and the standard deviation. In other words, the syntax `Normal(m, s, u, v)` is equivalent to `Normal(m, s, v, true) - Normal(m, s, u, true)`
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given value, that is the area under the normal distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: Normal | [probability] | Normal( <Mean>, <Standard Deviation>, <Variable Value u> , <Variable Value v>): Computes the probability of a normal random variable in the interval [u, v], given the mean and the standard deviation. In other words, the syntax `Normal(m, s, u, v)` is equivalent to `Normal(m, s, v, true) - Normal(m, s, u, true)`

description: Evaluates the function (formula) at variable value v, where Φ is the cumulative distribution function of N(0,1) with given mean μ and standard deviation σ.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given value, that is the area under the normal distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: Normal | [probability] | Normal( <Mean>, <Standard Deviation>, <Variable Value> ): Evaluates the function (formula) at variable value v, where Φ is the cumulative distribution function of N(0,1) with given mean μ and standard deviation σ. | Examples: Normal(2, 0.5, 1)

description: If Cumulative = true, evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Normal_distribution[normal distribution] with given mean and standard deviation at the given variable value, otherwise it evaluates the probability density function of the distribution at variable value.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given value, that is the area under the normal distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: Normal | [probability] | Normal( <Mean>, <Standard Deviation>, <Variable Value>, <Boolean Cumulative> ): If Cumulative = true, evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Normal_distribution[normal distribution] with given mean and standard deviation at the given variable value, otherwise it evaluates the probability density function of the distribution at variable value.

description: If Cumulative = true, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of a normal distribution with given mean and standard deviation, otherwise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given value, that is the area under the normal distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: Normal | [probability] | Normal( <Mean>, <Standard Deviation>, x, <Boolean Cumulative> ): If Cumulative = true, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of a normal distribution with given mean and standard deviation, otherwise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.

description: Creates a normal quantile plot from the given list of data and draws a line through the points showing the ideal plot for exactly normal data. Points are formed by plotting data values on the x-axis against their expected normal score (Z-score) on the y-axis. More precisely the y-values are computed using Filliben's estimate.
search_text: NormalQuantilePlot | [chart] | NormalQuantilePlot( <List of Raw Data> ): Creates a normal quantile plot from the given list of data and draws a line through the points showing the ideal plot for exactly normal data. Points are formed by plotting data values on the x-axis against their expected normal score (Z-score) on the y-axis. More precisely the y-values are computed using Filliben's estimate.

description: Returns a list containing the normalized form of the given numbers.
notes: This command differs among variants of English: Normalize (US) Normalise (UK + Aus)

If you are doing calculations using big or small numbers (eg using FitGrowth) then normalizing them might avoid rounding/overflow errors This command is not applicable to 3D points. * The operation of normalization maps a value x to the interval [0, 1] using the linear function (formula).
search_text: Normalize | [algebra] | Normalize( <List of Numbers> ): Returns a list containing the normalized form of the given numbers. | Examples: Normalize({1, 2, 3, 4, 5})

description: Returns a list containing the normalized form of the given points.
notes: This command differs among variants of English: Normalize (US) Normalise (UK + Aus)

If you are doing calculations using big or small numbers (eg using FitGrowth) then normalizing them might avoid rounding/overflow errors This command is not applicable to 3D points. * The operation of normalization maps a value x to the interval [0, 1] using the linear function (formula).
search_text: Normalize | [algebra] | Normalize( <List of Points> ): Returns a list containing the normalized form of the given points. | Examples: Normalize({(1,5), (2,4), (3,3), (4,2), (5,1)})

description: Returns the numerator of a rational number or expression.
notes: See also Denominator Command and FractionText Command.
search_text: Numerator | [algebra] | Numerator( <Expression> ): Returns the numerator of a rational number or expression. | Examples: Numerator(2/3 + 1/15); Numerator(a/b); Numerator(Simplify(a + b/c))

description: Returns the numerator of the function.
notes: See also Denominator Command and FractionText Command.
search_text: Numerator | [algebra] | Numerator( <Function> ): Returns the numerator of the function. | Examples: Numerator((3x² + 1) / (2x - 1))

description: For a rational number returns its (simplified) numerator. It uses a numerical method, which limits this command to numbers with small denominator. For irrational input the numerator of its continued fraction is returned.
notes: See also Denominator Command and FractionText Command.
search_text: Numerator | [algebra] | Numerator( <Number> ): For a rational number returns its (simplified) numerator. It uses a numerical method, which limits this command to numbers with small denominator. For irrational input the numerator of its continued fraction is returned. | Examples: Numerator(5 / 3); Numerator(10 / 6); Numerator(15 / 3)

description: Tries to determine a numerical approximation of the given expression. The number of decimals depends on the global rounding you choose in the Options Menu.
notes: If you don't specify enough digits then you can get an apparently wrong answer due to http://docs.oracle.com/cd/E19957-01/806-3568/ncg_goldberg.html[floating point cancelation].
See also Numeric tool.
search_text: Numeric | [algebra] | Numeric( <Expression> ): Tries to determine a numerical approximation of the given expression. The number of decimals depends on the global rounding you choose in the Options Menu. | Examples: Numeric(3 / 2)

description: Tries to determine a numerical approximation of the given expression, using the entered number of significant figures.
notes: If you don't specify enough digits then you can get an apparently wrong answer due to http://docs.oracle.com/cd/E19957-01/806-3568/ncg_goldberg.html[floating point cancelation].
See also Numeric tool.
search_text: Numeric | [algebra] | Numeric( <Expression>, <Significant Figures> ): Tries to determine a numerical approximation of the given expression, using the entered number of significant figures. | Examples: Numeric(sin(1), 20); Numeric(-500000000/785398163*sin(785398163/500000000)*1258025227.19^2+500000000/785398163*1258025227.19^2,10); Numeric(-500000000/785398163*sin(785398163/500000000)*1258025227.19^2+500000000/785398163*1258025227.19^2,30)

description: Returns the object for a given name. The result is always a dependent object.
notes: The Object command is the opposite of the Name command. Object command cannot be used in Custom Tools Make sure that the objects you refer to are earlier* in the Construction_Protocol than this command
search_text: Object | [geogebra_general] | Object( <Name of Object as Text> ): Returns the object for a given name. The result is always a dependent object. | Examples: Object("A" + n)

description: Creates an octahedron having as base the given equilateral triangle.
notes: `Octahedron(A, B)` is a shortcut for `Octahedron(A, B, C)` with `C = Point(Circle(Midpoint(A, B), Distance(A, B) sqrt(3) / 2, Segment(A, B)))`.
See also Cube, Tetrahedron, Icosahedron, Dodecahedron commands.
search_text: Octahedron | [3d] | Octahedron( <Equilateral Triangle> ): Creates an octahedron having as base the given equilateral triangle.

description: Creates an octahedron with the two points of the first face, and the third point automatically created on a circle, so that the octahedron can rotate around its first edge.
notes: `Octahedron(A, B)` is a shortcut for `Octahedron(A, B, C)` with `C = Point(Circle(Midpoint(A, B), Distance(A, B) sqrt(3) / 2, Segment(A, B)))`.
See also Cube, Tetrahedron, Icosahedron, Dodecahedron commands.
search_text: Octahedron | [3d] | Octahedron( <Point>, <Point>): Creates an octahedron with the two points of the first face, and the third point automatically created on a circle, so that the octahedron can rotate around its first edge.

description: Creates an octahedron having the segment between the two points as an edge. The other vertices are univocally determined by the given direction, that needs to be: a vector, a segment, a line, a ray orthogonal to the segment, or a polygon, a plane parallel to the segment. + The created octahedron will have: a face with the segment as an edge in a plane orthogonal to the given vector/segment/line/ray, or a face with the segment as an edge in a plane parallel to the polygon/plane.
notes: `Octahedron(A, B)` is a shortcut for `Octahedron(A, B, C)` with `C = Point(Circle(Midpoint(A, B), Distance(A, B) sqrt(3) / 2, Segment(A, B)))`.
See also Cube, Tetrahedron, Icosahedron, Dodecahedron commands.
search_text: Octahedron | [3d] | Octahedron( <Point>, <Point>, <Direction> ): Creates an octahedron having the segment between the two points as an edge. The other vertices are univocally determined by the given direction, that needs to be: a vector, a segment, a line, a ray orthogonal to the segment, or a polygon, a plane parallel to the segment. + The created octahedron will have: a face with the segment as an edge in a plane orthogonal to the given vector/segment/line/ray, or a face with the segment as an edge in a plane parallel to the polygon/plane.

description: Creates an octahedron with the three points of the first face. The points have to draw an equilateral triangle for the octahedron to be defined.
notes: `Octahedron(A, B)` is a shortcut for `Octahedron(A, B, C)` with `C = Point(Circle(Midpoint(A, B), Distance(A, B) sqrt(3) / 2, Segment(A, B)))`.
See also Cube, Tetrahedron, Icosahedron, Dodecahedron commands.
search_text: Octahedron | [3d] | Octahedron( <Point>, <Point>, <Point>): Creates an octahedron with the three points of the first face. The points have to draw an equilateral triangle for the octahedron to be defined.

description: Turns a number into an ordinal (as a text).
search_text: Ordinal | [text] | Ordinal( <Integer> ): Turns a number into an ordinal (as a text). | Examples: Ordinal(5)

description: Returns a list, whose i-th element is the rank of i-th element of list L (rank of element is its position in Sort(L)). If there are more equal elements in L which occupy positions from k to l in Sort[L], ranks from k to l are associated with these elements.
notes: Also see command: TiedRank
search_text: OrdinalRank | [list] | OrdinalRank( <List> ): Returns a list, whose i-th element is the rank of i-th element of list L (rank of element is its position in Sort(L)). If there are more equal elements in L which occupy positions from k to l in Sort[L], ranks from k to l are associated with these elements. | Examples: OrdinalRank({4, 1, 2, 3, 4, 2}); OrdinalRank({3, 2, 2, 1})

description: Yields the osculating circle of the curve in the given point.
notes: * This command is for 2D objects only. For 3D, you can make a custom tool for example https://www.geogebra.org/m/tan7dxjt
search_text: OsculatingCircle | [conic] | OsculatingCircle( <Point>, <Curve> ): Yields the osculating circle of the curve in the given point. | Examples: OsculatingCircle((1, 0), Curve(cos(t), sin(2t), t, 0, 2π))

description: Yields the osculating circle of the function in the given point.
notes: * This command is for 2D objects only. For 3D, you can make a custom tool for example https://www.geogebra.org/m/tan7dxjt
search_text: OsculatingCircle | [conic] | OsculatingCircle( <Point>, <Function> ): Yields the osculating circle of the function in the given point. | Examples: OsculatingCircle((0, 0), x^2)

description: Yields the osculating circle of the object (function, curve, conic) in the given point.
notes: * This command is for 2D objects only. For 3D, you can make a custom tool for example https://www.geogebra.org/m/tan7dxjt
search_text: OsculatingCircle | [conic] | OsculatingCircle( <Point>, <Object> ): Yields the osculating circle of the object (function, curve, conic) in the given point. | Examples: OsculatingCircle((0, 0), x^2); OsculatingCircle((1, 0), Curve(cos(t), sin(2t), t, 0, 2π)); OsculatingCircle((-1, 0), Conic({1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3}))

description: Shifts the active view by x pixels to the right and y pixels upwards.
notes: If multiple Graphics Views are visible, the command is applied to the active one. See also ZoomIn, ZoomOut, SetActiveView commands.
search_text: Pan | [other] | Pan( <x>, <y> ): Shifts the active view by x pixels to the right and y pixels upwards.

description: Shifts the active view by (x, y, z) pixels if it is a 3D View, or by (x, y) pixels if it is 2D View
notes: If multiple Graphics Views are visible, the command is applied to the active one. See also ZoomIn, ZoomOut, SetActiveView commands.
search_text: Pan | [other] | Pan( <x>, <y>, <z> ): Shifts the active view by (x, y, z) pixels if it is a 3D View, or by (x, y) pixels if it is 2D View

description: Returns a parabola with focal point and the line as directrix.
notes: See also Parabola tool .
search_text: Parabola | [conic] | Parabola( <Point>, <Line> ): Returns a parabola with focal point and the line as directrix. | Examples: Parabola((3, 3), a)

description: Returns the parameter of the parabola, which is the distance between the directrix and the focus.
search_text: Parameter | [conic] | Parameter( <Parabola> ): Returns the parameter of the parabola, which is the distance between the directrix and the focus. | Examples: Parameter(y = x^2 - 3x + 5)

description: Returns a new parametric curve given by (formula).
search_text: ParametricDerivative | [functions_and_calculus] | ParametricDerivative( <Curve> ): Returns a new parametric curve given by (formula). | Examples: ParametricDerivative(Curve(2t, t², t, 0, 10))

description: Parses the string and stores the result to a function f, which must be defined and free before the command is used.
notes: See also ParseToNumber command.
search_text: ParseToFunction | [functions_and_calculus] | ParseToFunction( <Function>, <Text> ): Parses the string and stores the result to a function f, which must be defined and free before the command is used. | Examples: f(x) = 3x² + 2; text1 = "f(x) = 3x + 1"; ParseToFunction(f, text1)

description: Parses the text containing the function definition and creates the corresponding function.
notes: See also ParseToNumber command.
search_text: ParseToFunction | [functions_and_calculus] | ParseToFunction( <Text> ): Parses the text containing the function definition and creates the corresponding function. | Examples: ParseToFunction("x^2"); ParseToFunction("t+2/t")

description: Parses the text containing the function definition and creates the corresponding function of the variables defined in the list.
notes: See also ParseToNumber command.
search_text: ParseToFunction | [functions_and_calculus] | ParseToFunction( <Text>, <List of variables> ): Parses the text containing the function definition and creates the corresponding function of the variables defined in the list. | Examples: ParseToFunction("2u+3v",{"u", "v"})

description: Parses the text and stores the result to a number a, which must be defined and free before the command is used.
notes: This is a scripting command which only sets the value of a number once. To convert a text text1 into a number which is updated dynamically, use FromBase(text1,10).
See also ParseToFunction command.
search_text: ParseToNumber | [algebra] | ParseToNumber( <Number>, <Text> ): Parses the text and stores the result to a number a, which must be defined and free before the command is used. | Examples: a = 3; text1 = "6"; ParseToNumber(a, text1)

description: Parses the text and stores the result to a number.
notes: This is a scripting command which only sets the value of a number once. To convert a text text1 into a number which is updated dynamically, use FromBase(text1,10).
See also ParseToFunction command.
search_text: ParseToNumber | [algebra] | ParseToNumber( <Text> ): Parses the text and stores the result to a number. | Examples: ParseToNumber("1+2+5-pi")

description: Yields, if possible, the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Partial_fraction[partial fraction] of the given function for the main function variable. The graph of the function is plotted in the Graphics View.
notes: Hint: In the CAS View you can also use the following syntax:
search_text: PartialFractions | [algebra] | PartialFractions( <Function> ): Yields, if possible, the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Partial_fraction[partial fraction] of the given function for the main function variable. The graph of the function is plotted in the Graphics View. | Examples: PartialFractions(x^2 / (x^2 - 2x + 1))

description: Yields, if possible, the partial fraction of the given function for the given function variable.
notes: Hint: In the CAS View you can also use the following syntax:
search_text: PartialFractions | [algebra] | PartialFractions( <Function>, <Variable> ): Yields, if possible, the partial fraction of the given function for the given function variable. | Examples: PartialFractions(a^2 / (a^2 - 2a + 1), a)

description: Returns a bar graph of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Negative_binomial_distribution[Pascal distribution]. The Pascal distribution models the number of failures before the _n__th success in repeated mutually independent Bernoulli trials, each with probability of success _p.
notes: This command also works in the CAS View.
search_text:
Pascal | [probability] | Pascal( ,

): Returns a bar graph of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Negative_binomial_distribution[Pascal distribution]. The Pascal distribution models the number of failures before the _n__th success in repeated mutually independent Bernoulli trials, each with probability of success _p.

description: Returns a bar graph of a Pascal distribution when Cumulative = false. Returns a graph of a cumulative Pascal distribution when Cumulative = true. First two parameters are same as above.
notes: This command also works in the CAS View.
search_text:
Pascal | [probability] | Pascal( ,

, ): Returns a bar graph of a Pascal distribution when Cumulative = false. Returns a graph of a cumulative Pascal distribution when Cumulative = true. First two parameters are same as above.

description: Let X be a Pascal random variable and v the variable value. Returns P( X = v) when Cumulative = false. Returns P( X ≤ v) when Cumulative = true. First two parameters are same as above.
notes: This command also works in the CAS View.
search_text:
Pascal | [probability] | Pascal( ,

, , ): Let X be a Pascal random variable and v the variable value. Returns P( X = v) when Cumulative = false. Returns P( X ≤ v) when Cumulative = true. First two parameters are same as above. | Examples: Pascal(1, 1/6, 2, false)

description: Returns the parameter (i.e. a number ranging from 0 to 1) of the point that belongs to a path. In the following table (formula) is a function used to map all real numbers into interval (-1,1) and (formula) is a linear map from line AB to reals which sends A to 0 and B to 1. [cols=",",] |=== |Line AB |(formula) |Ray AB |(formula) |Segment AB |(formula) |Circle with center C and radius r |Point (formula), where (formula)-\pi,\pi\]] has path parameter (formula) |Ellipse with center C and semiaxes (formula), (formula) |Point (formula) , where (formula)-\pi,\pi\]] has path parameter (formula) |Hyperbola |Point (formula) has path parameter (formula) or (formula) |Parabola with vertex V and direction of axis (formula). |Point (formula) has path parameter (formula). |Polyline A~1~...A~n~ |If X lies on A~k~A~k+1~, path parameter of X is (formula) |Polygon A~1~...A~n~ |If X lies on A~k~A~k+1~ (using A~n+1~=A~1~), path parameter of X is (formula) |List of paths L={p~1~,...,p~n~} |If X lies on p~k~ and path parameter of X w.r.t. p~k~ is t, path parameter of X w.r.t.L is (formula) |List of points L={A~1~,...,A~n~} |Path parameter of A~k~ is (formula). Point[L,t] returns (formula). |Locus | |Implicit polynomial |No formula available. |===
search_text: PathParameter | [conic] | PathParameter( <Point On Path> ): Returns the parameter (i.e. a number ranging from 0 to 1) of the point that belongs to a path. In the following table (formula) is a function used to map all real numbers into interval (-1,1) and (formula) is a linear map from line AB to reals which sends A to 0 and B to 1. [cols=",",] |=== |Line AB |(formula) |Ray AB |(formula) |Segment AB |(formula) |Circle with center C and radius r |Point (formula), where (formula)-\pi,\pi\]] has path parameter (formula) |Ellipse with center C and semiaxes (formula), (formula) |Point (formula) , where (formula)-\pi,\pi\]] has path parameter (formula) |Hyperbola |Point (formula) has path parameter (formula) or (formula) |Parabola with vertex V and direction of axis (formula). |Point (formula) has path parameter (formula). |Polyline A~1~...A~n~ |If X lies on A~k~A~k+1~, path parameter of X is (formula) |Polygon A~1~...A~n~ |If X lies on A~k~A~k+1~ (using A~n+1~=A~1~), path parameter of X is (formula) |List of paths L={p~1~,...,p~n~} |If X lies on p~k~ and path parameter of X w.r.t. p~k~ is t, path parameter of X w.r.t.L is (formula) |List of points L={A~1~,...,A~n~} |Path parameter of A~k~ is (formula). Point[L,t] returns (formula). |Locus | |Implicit polynomial |No formula available. |=== | Examples: f(x) = x² + x - 1; PathParameter(A)

description: Calculates the payment for a loan based on constant payments and a constant interest rate. <Rate> Interest rate per period. <Number of Periods> Total number of payments for the loan. <Present Value> Total amount that a series of future payments is worth now. <Future Value (optional)> A cash balance you want to attain after the last payment. If you do not enter a future value, it is assumed to be 0. <Type (optional)>* Indicates when payments are due. If you do not enter a value or you enter 0 the payment is due at the end of the period. If you enter 1 it is due at the beginning of the period.
notes: Make sure that you are consistent about the units you use for <Rate> and <Number of Periods>. If you make monthly payments on a four-year loan at an annual interest rate of 6 percent, use 6%/12 for rate and 4*12 for number of payments.
For all arguments, cash paid out is represented by negative numbers and cash received by positive numbers.
See also Rate, Periods, Present Value and Future Value commands.
search_text: Payment | [financial] | Payment( <Rate>, <Number of Periods>, <Present Value>, <Future Value (optional)>, <Type (optional)> ): Calculates the payment for a loan based on constant payments and a constant interest rate. <Rate> Interest rate per period. <Number of Periods> Total number of payments for the loan. <Present Value> Total amount that a series of future payments is worth now. <Future Value (optional)> A cash balance you want to attain after the last payment. If you do not enter a future value, it is assumed to be 0. <Type (optional)>* Indicates when payments are due. If you do not enter a value or you enter 0 the payment is due at the end of the period. If you enter 1 it is due at the beginning of the period. | Examples: Payment(6%/12, 10, 10000, 0,1)

description: Let P equal the given Percent. Returns the value that cuts off the first P percent of the list of numbers, when the list is sorted in ascending order. Percent must be a number in the interval 0 < P ≤ 1.
notes: The commands Quartile and Percentile use different rules and do not always return matching results.
search_text: Percentile | [statistics] | Percentile( <List of Numbers>, <Percent> ): Let P equal the given Percent. Returns the value that cuts off the first P percent of the list of numbers, when the list is sorted in ascending order. Percent must be a number in the interval 0 < P ≤ 1. | Examples: Percentile({1, 2, 3, 4}, 0.25); Q1({1, 2, 3, 4}); Percentile({1, 2, 3, 4}, 0.25)

description: If the given conic is a circle or ellipse, this command returns its perimeter. Otherwise the result is undefined.
notes: See also Circumference command.
search_text: Perimeter | [3d] | Perimeter( <Conic> ): If the given conic is a circle or ellipse, this command returns its perimeter. Otherwise the result is undefined. | Examples: Perimeter(x^2 + 2y^2 = 1)

description: If the given locus is finite, this command returns its approximate perimeter. Otherwise the result is undefined.
notes: See also Circumference command.
search_text: Perimeter | [3d] | Perimeter( <Locus> ): If the given locus is finite, this command returns its approximate perimeter. Otherwise the result is undefined.

description: Returns the perimeter of the polygon.
notes: See also Circumference command.
search_text: Perimeter | [3d] | Perimeter( <Polygon> ): Returns the perimeter of the polygon. | Examples: Perimeter(Polygon((1, 2), (3, 2), (4, 3)))

description: Returns the number of periods for an annuity based on periodic, fixed payments and a fixed interest rate. <Rate> Interest rate per period. <Payment> The amount paid in each period. <Present Value> Total amount that a series of future payments is worth now. <Future Value (optional)> A cash balance you want to attain after the last payment. If you do not enter a future value, it is assumed to be 0. <Type (optional)>* Indicates when payments are due. If you do not enter a value or you enter 0 the payment is due at the end of the period. If you enter 1 it is due at the beginning of the period.
notes: If you make monthly payments on an annual interest rate of 10 percent, use 10%/12 for <Rate>.
For all arguments, cash paid out is represented by negative numbers and cash received by positive numbers.
See also Payment, Rate, Present Value and Future Value commands.
search_text: Periods | [financial] | Periods( <Rate>, <Payment>, <Present Value>, <Future Value (optional)>, <Type (optional)> ): Returns the number of periods for an annuity based on periodic, fixed payments and a fixed interest rate. <Rate> Interest rate per period. <Payment> The amount paid in each period. <Present Value> Total amount that a series of future payments is worth now. <Future Value (optional)> A cash balance you want to attain after the last payment. If you do not enter a future value, it is assumed to be 0. <Type (optional)>* Indicates when payments are due. If you do not enter a value or you enter 0 the payment is due at the end of the period. If you enter 1 it is due at the beginning of the period. | Examples: Periods(10%/12, -200, -400, 10000); Periods(10%/12, -200, -400, 10000, 1)

description: Yields the perpendicular bisector of a line segment between two points.
notes: See also Perpendicular Bisector tool.
search_text: PerpendicularBisector | [3d] | PerpendicularBisector( <Point>, <Point> ): Yields the perpendicular bisector of a line segment between two points.

description: Yields the perpendicular bisector of a line segment between two points which is perpendicular to the direction. <Direction> can either be a vector, an axis, a line or a segment.
notes: See also Perpendicular Bisector tool.
search_text: PerpendicularBisector | [3d] | PerpendicularBisector( <Point>, <Point>, <Direction>): Yields the perpendicular bisector of a line segment between two points which is perpendicular to the direction. <Direction> can either be a vector, an axis, a line or a segment.

description: Yields the perpendicular bisector of a segment.
notes: See also Perpendicular Bisector tool.
search_text: PerpendicularBisector | [3d] | PerpendicularBisector( <Segment> ): Yields the perpendicular bisector of a segment.

description: Creates a perpendicular line to the given lines through the intersection point of the two lines.
notes: For 3D objects a third argument is added to this command to specify the behavior: if 2D view is active, plane z=0 is used as third argument, if 3D view is active, space is used instead. See PerpendicularLine( <Point>, <Line>, <Context> ) further below for details.
See also Perpendicular Line tool.
search_text: PerpendicularLine | [3d] | PerpendicularLine( <Line> , <Line> ): Creates a perpendicular line to the given lines through the intersection point of the two lines.

description: Creates a perpendicular line to the given directions (that can be lines or vectors) through the given point.
notes: For 3D objects a third argument is added to this command to specify the behavior: if 2D view is active, plane z=0 is used as third argument, if 3D view is active, space is used instead. See PerpendicularLine( <Point>, <Line>, <Context> ) further below for details.
See also Perpendicular Line tool.
search_text: PerpendicularLine | [3d] | PerpendicularLine( <Point>, <Direction>, <Direction> ): Creates a perpendicular line to the given directions (that can be lines or vectors) through the given point.

description: Creates a line through the point perpendicular to the given line.
notes: For 3D objects a third argument is added to this command to specify the behavior: if 2D view is active, plane z=0 is used as third argument, if 3D view is active, space is used instead. See PerpendicularLine( <Point>, <Line>, <Context> ) further below for details.
See also Perpendicular Line tool.
search_text: PerpendicularLine | [3d] | PerpendicularLine( <Point>, <Line> ): Creates a line through the point perpendicular to the given line. | Examples: c: -3x + 4y = -6; A = (-2, -3); PerpendicularLine(A, c)

description: Creates a perpendicular line to the line through the point and depending on the context. PerpendicularLine( <Point>, <Line>, <Plane> ) creates a perpendicular line to the given line through the point and parallel to the plane. PerpendicularLine( <Point>, <Line>, space ) creates a perpendicular line to the given line through the point. The two lines have an intersection point. This command yields undefined if the point is on the line in 3D.
notes: For 3D objects a third argument is added to this command to specify the behavior: if 2D view is active, plane z=0 is used as third argument, if 3D view is active, space is used instead. See PerpendicularLine( <Point>, <Line>, <Context> ) further below for details.
See also Perpendicular Line tool.
search_text: PerpendicularLine | [3d] | PerpendicularLine( <Point>, <Line>, <Context> ): Creates a perpendicular line to the line through the point and depending on the context. PerpendicularLine( <Point>, <Line>, <Plane> ) creates a perpendicular line to the given line through the point and parallel to the plane. PerpendicularLine( <Point>, <Line>, space ) creates a perpendicular line to the given line through the point. The two lines have an intersection point. This command yields undefined if the point is on the line in 3D.

description: Creates a perpendicular line to the plane through the given point.
notes: For 3D objects a third argument is added to this command to specify the behavior: if 2D view is active, plane z=0 is used as third argument, if 3D view is active, space is used instead. See PerpendicularLine( <Point>, <Line>, <Context> ) further below for details.
See also Perpendicular Line tool.
search_text: PerpendicularLine | [3d] | PerpendicularLine( <Point>, <Plane> ): Creates a perpendicular line to the plane through the given point.

description: Creates a line through the point perpendicular to the given segment.
notes: For 3D objects a third argument is added to this command to specify the behavior: if 2D view is active, plane z=0 is used as third argument, if 3D view is active, space is used instead. See PerpendicularLine( <Point>, <Line>, <Context> ) further below for details.
See also Perpendicular Line tool.
search_text: PerpendicularLine | [3d] | PerpendicularLine( <Point>, <Segment> ): Creates a line through the point perpendicular to the given segment. | Examples: PerpendicularLine(A, c)

description: Creates a line through the point perpendicular to the given vector.
notes: For 3D objects a third argument is added to this command to specify the behavior: if 2D view is active, plane z=0 is used as third argument, if 3D view is active, space is used instead. See PerpendicularLine( <Point>, <Line>, <Context> ) further below for details.
See also Perpendicular Line tool.
search_text: PerpendicularLine | [3d] | PerpendicularLine( <Point>, <Vector> ): Creates a line through the point perpendicular to the given vector. | Examples: u = Vector((5, 3), (1, 1)); A = (-2, 0); PerpendicularLine(A, u)

description: Creates a plane through the given point, perpendicular to the given line.
notes: See also Perpendicular Plane Tool.
search_text: PerpendicularPlane | [3d] | PerpendicularPlane( <Point>, <Line> ): Creates a plane through the given point, perpendicular to the given line.

description: Creates a plane through the given point, perpendicular to the given vector.
notes: See also Perpendicular Plane Tool.
search_text: PerpendicularPlane | [3d] | PerpendicularPlane( <Point>, <Vector> ): Creates a plane through the given point, perpendicular to the given vector.

description: Returns one of the perpendicular vector to the line.
notes: The components of the perpendicular vector to a line of equation ax + by = c are (a, b).
If a point is specified in the definition of the line, segment, or vector, the perpendicular vector will originate from that point. Otherwise, the origin will be at (0, 0).
In the CAS View undefined variables are allowed as well.
See also UnitPerpendicularVector Command.
search_text: PerpendicularVector | [vector_and_matrix] | PerpendicularVector( <Line> ): Returns one of the perpendicular vector to the line. | Examples: Line((1, 4), (5, -3)); PerpendicularVector(j)

description: Creates a vector orthogonal to the plane, with starting point at (0,0,0).
notes: The components of the perpendicular vector to a line of equation ax + by = c are (a, b).
If a point is specified in the definition of the line, segment, or vector, the perpendicular vector will originate from that point. Otherwise, the origin will be at (0, 0).
In the CAS View undefined variables are allowed as well.
See also UnitPerpendicularVector Command.
search_text: PerpendicularVector | [vector_and_matrix] | PerpendicularVector( <Plane> ): Creates a vector orthogonal to the plane, with starting point at (0,0,0). | Examples: PerpendicularVector(xOyPlane)

description: Returns one of the perpendicular vector to the segment, having the same length.
notes: The components of the perpendicular vector to a line of equation ax + by = c are (a, b).
If a point is specified in the definition of the line, segment, or vector, the perpendicular vector will originate from that point. Otherwise, the origin will be at (0, 0).
In the CAS View undefined variables are allowed as well.
See also UnitPerpendicularVector Command.
search_text: PerpendicularVector | [vector_and_matrix] | PerpendicularVector( <Segment> ): Returns one of the perpendicular vector to the segment, having the same length. | Examples: Segment((3, 2), (14, 5)); PerpendicularVector(k)

description: Returns one of the perpendicular vector to the given vector.
notes: The components of the perpendicular vector to a line of equation ax + by = c are (a, b).
If a point is specified in the definition of the line, segment, or vector, the perpendicular vector will originate from that point. Otherwise, the origin will be at (0, 0).
In the CAS View undefined variables are allowed as well.
See also UnitPerpendicularVector Command.
search_text: PerpendicularVector | [vector_and_matrix] | PerpendicularVector( <Vector> ): Returns one of the perpendicular vector to the given vector. | Examples: Vector((-12, 8)); PerpendicularVector(u); Line(A, B)

description: Creates a pie chart using a list of frequencies. The whole pie gives 100%, the provided data is shown as pie slices.
notes: Select the Color tab of the Properties dialog window of the pie chart to choose among a wide selection of colors to customize each slice, and the Style tab to set the filling of each slice.
search_text: PieChart | [chart] | PieChart(< List of Frequencies >): Creates a pie chart using a list of frequencies. The whole pie gives 100%, the provided data is shown as pie slices. | Examples: PieChart({20, 15, 40, 5, 10, 20})

syntax: PieChart(< List of Frequencies >, < Center >, < Radius >)
description: Creates a pie chart with a given center and radius using a list of frequencies. The whole pie gives 100%, the provided data is shown as pie slices.
notes: Select the Color tab of the Properties dialog window of the pie chart to choose among a wide selection of colors to customize each slice, and the Style tab to set the filling of each slice.
search_text: PieChart | [chart] | PieChart(< List of Frequencies >, < Center >, < Radius >): Creates a pie chart with a given center and radius using a list of frequencies. The whole pie gives 100%, the provided data is shown as pie slices. | Examples: PieChart({20, 15, 40, 5, 10, 20},(3,3),5)

description: Creates the plane containing the conic.
notes: See also Plane through 3 Points and Plane tools.
search_text: Plane | [3d] | Plane( <Conic> ): Creates the plane containing the conic.

description: Creates the plane through the lines (that must be intersecting or parallel to each other).
notes: See also Plane through 3 Points and Plane tools.
search_text: Plane | [3d] | Plane( <Line> , <Line> ): Creates the plane through the lines (that must be intersecting or parallel to each other).

description: Creates the plane through the given point and line.
notes: See also Plane through 3 Points and Plane tools.
search_text: Plane | [3d] | Plane( <Point>, <Line> ): Creates the plane through the given point and line.

description: Creates the plane through the given point, parallel to the given plane.
notes: See also Plane through 3 Points and Plane tools.
search_text: Plane | [3d] | Plane( <Point>, <Plane> ): Creates the plane through the given point, parallel to the given plane.

description: Creates the plane through the given points.
notes: See also Plane through 3 Points and Plane tools.
search_text: Plane | [3d] | Plane( <Point>, <Point>, <Point> ): Creates the plane through the given points.

description: Creates the plane through the given point and having as normal vector the cross product of the given vectors (that must not be collinear).
notes: See also Plane through 3 Points and Plane tools.
search_text: Plane | [3d] | Plane( <Point>, <Vector>, <Vector> ): Creates the plane through the given point and having as normal vector the cross product of the given vectors (that must not be collinear).

description: Creates the plane containing the given polygon.
notes: See also Plane through 3 Points and Plane tools.
search_text: Plane | [3d] | Plane( <Polygon> ): Creates the plane containing the given polygon.

description: Creates the plane orthogonal bisector between the two points.
search_text: PlaneBisector | [3d] | PlaneBisector( <Point> , <Point> ): Creates the plane orthogonal bisector between the two points.

description: Creates the plane orthogonal bisector of the segment.
search_text: PlaneBisector | [3d] | PlaneBisector( <Segment> ): Creates the plane orthogonal bisector of the segment.

description: Pause or resume play (not MP3 files) PlaySound(true) = play, PlaySound(false) = pause.
notes: To work on iOS (and also if you want immediate playback) then you can encode the .mp3 as an inline base64-encoded data: URL, see https://www.geogebra.org/m/wztkqxuv for an example. It must start with the exact string data:audio/mp3;base64, to work in GeoGebra Classic 5. You can use this utility to convert .MP3s into the syntax needed in GeoGebra https://test.geogebra.org/~mike/utils/base64Encode.html
search_text: PlaySound | [other] | PlaySound( <Boolean Play> ): Pause or resume play (not MP3 files) PlaySound(true) = play, PlaySound(false) = pause.

description: Plays a sound generated by Function, a time-valued function with range [-1,1]. The time units are seconds and the sound is played from time Min Value to Max Value. Sound is generated by 8-bit samples taken at a rate of 8000 samples per second. This plays a pure sine wave tone at 440 Hz (musical note A) for one second.
notes: To work on iOS (and also if you want immediate playback) then you can encode the .mp3 as an inline base64-encoded data: URL, see https://www.geogebra.org/m/wztkqxuv for an example. It must start with the exact string data:audio/mp3;base64, to work in GeoGebra Classic 5. You can use this utility to convert .MP3s into the syntax needed in GeoGebra https://test.geogebra.org/~mike/utils/base64Encode.html
search_text: PlaySound | [other] | PlaySound( <Function>, <Min Value>, <Max Value> ): Plays a sound generated by Function, a time-valued function with range [-1,1]. The time units are seconds and the sound is played from time Min Value to Max Value. Sound is generated by 8-bit samples taken at a rate of 8000 samples per second. This plays a pure sine wave tone at 440 Hz (musical note A) for one second. | Examples: PlaySound(sin(440 2Pi x), 0, 1)

description: Plays a sound generated by Function, a time-valued function with range [-1,1]. The time units are seconds and the sound is played from time Min Value to Max Value. The sampling method is specified by "Sample Depth" and "Sample Rate". "Sample Rate" is the number of sample function values taken each second. Allowable values are 8000, 11025, 16000, 22050, or 44100 "Sample Depth" is the data size of a sample in bits. Allowable values are 8 and 16.
notes: To work on iOS (and also if you want immediate playback) then you can encode the .mp3 as an inline base64-encoded data: URL, see https://www.geogebra.org/m/wztkqxuv for an example. It must start with the exact string data:audio/mp3;base64, to work in GeoGebra Classic 5. You can use this utility to convert .MP3s into the syntax needed in GeoGebra https://test.geogebra.org/~mike/utils/base64Encode.html
search_text: PlaySound | [other] | PlaySound( <Function>, <Min Value>, <Max Value>, <Sample Rate>, <Sample Depth> ): Plays a sound generated by Function, a time-valued function with range [-1,1]. The time units are seconds and the sound is played from time Min Value to Max Value. The sampling method is specified by "Sample Depth" and "Sample Rate". "Sample Rate" is the number of sample function values taken each second. Allowable values are 8000, 11025, 16000, 22050, or 44100 "Sample Depth" is the data size of a sample in bits. Allowable values are 8 and 16.

description: Plays a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MIDI[MIDI] note. Note is an integer from 0 to 127 that represents a musical note given by the table below. When note = 60 a Middle C is played. Duration is the time to play the note in seconds. Instrument is an integer that represents the synthesized instrument used to play the note. See https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_MIDI#Program_change_events[technical specifications] for possible instruments. Most instruments are supported, but there are differences between computer platforms. notes: To work on iOS (and also if you want immediate playback) then you can encode the .mp3 as an inline base64-encoded data: URL, see https://www.geogebra.org/m/wztkqxuv for an example. It must start with the exact string data:audio/mp3;base64, to work in GeoGebra Classic 5. You can use this utility to convert .MP3s into the syntax needed in GeoGebra https://test.geogebra.org/~mike/utils/base64Encode.html

Note is an integer from 0 to 127 that represents a musical note given by the table below. When note = 60 a Middle C is played. Duration is the time to play the note in seconds. Instrument is an integer that represents the synthesized instrument used to play the note. notes: To work on iOS (and also if you want immediate playback) then you can encode the .mp3 as an inline base64-encoded data: URL, see https://www.geogebra.org/m/wztkqxuv for an example. It must start with the exact string data:audio/mp3;base64, to work in GeoGebra Classic 5. You can use this utility to convert .MP3s into the syntax needed in GeoGebra https://test.geogebra.org/~mike/utils/base64Encode.html

description: Converts a list containing two numbers into a Point.
notes: See also Point tool. See also Points and vectors
search_text: Point | [3d] | Point( <List> ): Converts a list containing two numbers into a Point. | Examples: Point({1, 2})

notes: See also Point tool. See also Points and vectors

description: Returns a bar graph of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poisson_distribution[Poisson distribution] with given mean λ.
notes: A simplified syntax is available to calculate P(u ≤ X ≤ v): e.g. Poisson(1, 1..5) yields 0.63153, that is the same as Poisson(1, {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}).
search_text: Poisson | [probability] | Poisson( <Mean> ): Returns a bar graph of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poisson_distribution[Poisson distribution] with given mean λ.

description: Returns a bar graph of a Poisson distribution when Cumulative = false. Returns a graph of a cumulative Poisson distribution when Cumulative = true. The first parameter is same as above.
notes: A simplified syntax is available to calculate P(u ≤ X ≤ v): e.g. Poisson(1, 1..5) yields 0.63153, that is the same as Poisson(1, {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}).
search_text: Poisson | [probability] | Poisson( <Mean>, <Boolean Cumulative> ): Returns a bar graph of a Poisson distribution when Cumulative = false. Returns a graph of a cumulative Poisson distribution when Cumulative = true. The first parameter is same as above.

description: Let X be a Poisson random variable. Returns P( X = v) when Cumulative = false. Returns P( X ≤ v) when Cumulative = true. First parameter is same as above.
notes: A simplified syntax is available to calculate P(u ≤ X ≤ v): e.g. Poisson(1, 1..5) yields 0.63153, that is the same as Poisson(1, {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}).
search_text: Poisson | [probability] | Poisson( <Mean>, <Variable Value v>, <Boolean Cumulative> ): Let X be a Poisson random variable. Returns P( X = v) when Cumulative = false. Returns P( X ≤ v) when Cumulative = true. First parameter is same as above. | Examples: Poisson(3, 1, true); Poisson(3, 1, false)

See also Polygon command.

notes: The polygonal chain length is displayed in the Algebra View.

description: Returns the total amount of payments of an investment. <Rate> Interest rate per period. <Number of Periods> Total number of payments for the loan. <Payment> The amount paid in each period. <Future Value (optional)> A cash balance you want to attain after the last payment. If you do not enter a future value, it is assumed to be 0. <Type (optional)>* Indicates when payments are due. If you do not enter a value or you enter 0 the payment is due at the end of the period. If you enter 1 it is due at the beginning of the period.
notes: Make sure that you are consistent about the units you use for <Rate> and <Number of Periods>. If you make monthly payments on a four-year loan at an annual interest rate of 12 percent, use 12%/12 for rate and 4*12 for number of payments.
For all arguments, cash paid out is represented by negative numbers and cash received by positive numbers.
See also Payment, Periods, Rate and Future Value commands.
search_text: PresentValue | [financial] | PresentValue( <Rate>, <Number of Periods>, <Payment>, <Future Value (optional)>, <Type (optional)> ): Returns the total amount of payments of an investment. <Rate> Interest rate per period. <Number of Periods> Total number of payments for the loan. <Payment> The amount paid in each period. <Future Value (optional)> A cash balance you want to attain after the last payment. If you do not enter a future value, it is assumed to be 0. <Type (optional)>* Indicates when payments are due. If you do not enter a value or you enter 0 the payment is due at the end of the period. If you enter 1 it is due at the beginning of the period. | Examples: PresentValue(12%/12, 4*12, -100, 5000, 0); PresentValue(12%/12, 4*12, -100, 5000, 1)

description: Returns whether the given boolean expression is true or false in general. Normally, GeoGebra decides whether a boolean expression is true or not by using numerical computations. However, the Prove command uses https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symbolic_computation[symbolic methods] to determine whether a statement is true or false in general. If GeoGebra cannot determine the answer, the result is undefined.
notes: See also ProveDetails command, Boolean values, and https://github.com/kovzol/gg-art-doc/tree/master/pdf/english.pdf[GeoGebra Automated Reasoning Tools: A Tutorial].
search_text: Prove | [geometry] | Prove( <Boolean Expression> ): Returns whether the given boolean expression is true or false in general. Normally, GeoGebra decides whether a boolean expression is true or not by using numerical computations. However, the Prove command uses https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symbolic_computation[symbolic methods] to determine whether a statement is true or false in general. If GeoGebra cannot determine the answer, the result is undefined. | Examples: A=(1,2); B=(3,4); C=(5,6)

description: Returns some details of the result of the automated proof. Normally, GeoGebra decides whether a boolean expression is true or not by using numerical computations. However, the ProveDetails command uses https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symbolic_computation[symbolic methods] to determine whether a statement is true or false in general. This command works like the Prove command, but also returns some details of the result as a list: An empty list {} if GeoGebra cannot determine the answer. A list with one element: {false}, if the statement is not true in general. A list with one element: {true}, if the statement is always true (in all cases when the diagram can be constructed). A list with more elements, containing the boolean value true and another list for some so-called non-degeneracy conditions, if the statement is true under certain conditions, e.g. {true, {"AreCollinear(A,B,C),AreEqual(C,D)"}}. This means that if none of the conditions are true (and the diagram can be constructed), then the statement will be true. A list {true,{"..."}}*, if the statement is true under certain conditions, but these conditions cannot be translated to human readable form for some reasons. It is possible that the list of the non-degeneracy conditions is not the simplest possible set. For the above example, the simplest set would be the empty set.
notes: See also Prove command, Boolean values, https://github.com/kovzol/gg-art-doc/tree/master/pdf/english.pdf[GeoGebra Automated Reasoning Tools: A Tutorial] and http://dev.geogebra.org/trac/wiki/TheoremProving[technical details of the algorithms].
search_text: ProveDetails | [geometry] | ProveDetails( <Boolean Expression> ): Returns some details of the result of the automated proof. Normally, GeoGebra decides whether a boolean expression is true or not by using numerical computations. However, the ProveDetails command uses https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symbolic_computation[symbolic methods] to determine whether a statement is true or false in general. This command works like the Prove command, but also returns some details of the result as a list: An empty list {} if GeoGebra cannot determine the answer. A list with one element: {false}, if the statement is not true in general. A list with one element: {true}, if the statement is always true (in all cases when the diagram can be constructed). A list with more elements, containing the boolean value true and another list for some so-called non-degeneracy conditions, if the statement is true under certain conditions, e.g. {true, {"AreCollinear(A,B,C),AreEqual(C,D)"}}. This means that if none of the conditions are true (and the diagram can be constructed), then the statement will be true. A list {true,{"..."}}*, if the statement is true under certain conditions, but these conditions cannot be translated to human readable form for some reasons. It is possible that the list of the non-degeneracy conditions is not the simplest possible set. For the above example, the simplest set would be the empty set. | Examples: D=MidPoint(B,C); E=MidPoint(A,C); p=Line(A,B)
