description: Returns the interest rate per period of an annuity. <Number of Periods> Total number of payments for the loan. <Payment> The amount paid in each period. <Present Value> Total amount that a series of future payments is worth now. <Future Value (optional)> A cash balance you want to attain after the last payment. If you do not enter a future value, it is assumed to be 0. <Type (optional)> Indicates when payments are due. If you do not enter a value or you enter 0 the payment is due at the end of the period. If you enter 1 it is due at the beginning of the period. <Guess (optional)> Your guess for what the rate will be.
notes: If you make monthly payments on a five-year loan use 5*12 for <Number of Periods>.
For all arguments, cash paid out is represented by negative numbers and cash received by positive numbers..
See also Payment, Periods, Present Value and Future Value commands.
search_text: Rate | [financial] | Rate( <Number of Periods>, <Payment>, <Present Value>, <Future Value (optional)>, <Type (optional)>, <Guess (optional)> ): Returns the interest rate per period of an annuity. <Number of Periods> Total number of payments for the loan. <Payment> The amount paid in each period. <Present Value> Total amount that a series of future payments is worth now. <Future Value (optional)> A cash balance you want to attain after the last payment. If you do not enter a future value, it is assumed to be 0. <Type (optional)> Indicates when payments are due. If you do not enter a value or you enter 0 the payment is due at the end of the period. If you enter 1 it is due at the beginning of the period. <Guess (optional)> Your guess for what the rate will be. | Examples: Rate(5*12, -300, 10000)

description: Tells the screen reader to read given text immediately.

syntax: RectangleSum( <Function>, <Start x-Value>, <End x-Value>, <Number of Rectangles>, <Position for rectangle start> )
description: Calculates between the Start x-Value and the End x-Value the sum of rectangles with left height starting at a fraction d (0 ≤ d ≤ 1) of each interval, using n rectangles. When d = 0 it is equivalent to the LeftSum command, and when d = 1 it computes the right sum of the given function.
notes: This command draws the rectangles of the left sum as well. This command is designed as a visual aid so won't give accurate answers if the number of rectangles is too large. * See also the commands: UpperSum, LowerSum, LeftSum , TrapezoidalSum.
search_text: RectangleSum | [functions_and_calculus] | RectangleSum( <Function>, <Start x-Value>, <End x-Value>, <Number of Rectangles>, <Position for rectangle start> ): Calculates between the Start x-Value and the End x-Value the sum of rectangles with left height starting at a fraction d (0 ≤ d ≤ 1) of each interval, using n rectangles. When d = 0 it is equivalent to the LeftSum command, and when d = 1 it computes the right sum of the given function.

description: Shows a message box that gives you information about the relation between two or more (up to 4) objects.

description: Shows a message box that gives you information about the relation between two objects. This command allows you to find out whether two lines are perpendicular two lines are parallel two (or more) objects are equal a point lies on a line or conic a line is tangent or a passing line to a conic three points are collinear three lines are concurrent (or parallel) four points are concyclic (or collinear). Some of these checks can also be performed symbolically. If GeoGebra supports symbolic check for a certain property, the "More" button appears. By clicking it, GeoGebra may provide more information whether the property is true in general (eventually under certain conditions).
notes: See also Relation tool.
search_text: Relation | [logic] | Relation( <Object>, <Object> ): Shows a message box that gives you information about the relation between two objects. This command allows you to find out whether two lines are perpendicular two lines are parallel two (or more) objects are equal a point lies on a line or conic a line is tangent or a passing line to a conic three points are collinear three lines are concurrent (or parallel) four points are concyclic (or collinear). Some of these checks can also be performed symbolically. If GeoGebra supports symbolic check for a certain property, the "More" button appears. By clicking it, GeoGebra may provide more information whether the property is true in general (eventually under certain conditions).

search_text: ResidualPlot | [chart] | ResidualPlot( <List of Points>, <Function> ): Returns a list of points whose x-coordinates are equal to the x-coordinates of the elements of the given list, and y-coordinates are the residuals with respect to f. If the i-th element of the given list is a point (a,b) then i-th element of the result is (a,b-f(a)). | Examples: list = {(-1, 1), (-0.51, 2), (0, 0.61), (0.51, -1.41), (0.54, 1.97), (1.11, 0.42), (1.21, 2.53), (-0.8, -0.12)}; f(x) = x^5 + x^4 - x - 1; ResidualPlot(list, f )

search_text: RootList | [functions_and_calculus] | RootList( <List> ): Converts a given list of numbers {a~1~,a~2~,...,a~n~} to a list of points {(a~1~,0),(a~2~,0),...,(a~n~,0)}, which is also displayed in the Graphics View. | Examples: RootList({3, 4, 5, 2, 1, 3})

description: Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Root_mean_square[root mean square] of given list of numbers.
search_text: RootMeanSquare | [statistics] | RootMeanSquare( <List of Numbers> ): Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Root_mean_square[root mean square] of given list of numbers. | Examples: RootMeanSquare({3, 4, 5, 3, 2, 3, 4})

description: Calculates the roots for function in the given interval. The function must be continuous on that interval. Because this algorithm is numeric, it may not find all the roots in some cases.
notes: See also Root command
search_text: Roots | [functions_and_calculus] | Roots( <Function>, <Start x-Value>, <End x-Value> ): Calculates the roots for function in the given interval. The function must be continuous on that interval. Because this algorithm is numeric, it may not find all the roots in some cases. | Examples: Roots(f, -2, 1); f(x) = 3x³ + 3x² - x; A = (-1.264, 0), B = (0, 0), C = (0.264, 0)

description: Rotates the geometric object by the angle around the axis origin.
notes: Vectors are not rotated around axis origin, but around their initial point. When a polygon, segment, arc, etc. is rotated, also images of the vertices / endpoints and sides (in case of polygon) are created. This command also rotates images. For text rotation use RotateText Command. * See also Rotate around Point and Rotate around Line tools.
search_text: Rotate | [transformation] | Rotate( <Object>, <Angle> ): Rotates the geometric object by the angle around the axis origin.

description: Rotates the geometric object by the angle around the given axis of rotation.
notes: Vectors are not rotated around axis origin, but around their initial point. When a polygon, segment, arc, etc. is rotated, also images of the vertices / endpoints and sides (in case of polygon) are created. This command also rotates images. For text rotation use RotateText Command. * See also Rotate around Point and Rotate around Line tools.
search_text: Rotate | [transformation] | Rotate( <Object>, <Angle>, <Axis of Rotation>): Rotates the geometric object by the angle around the given axis of rotation.

syntax: Rotate( <Object>, <Angle>, <Point on Axis>, <Axis Direction or Plane> )
description: Rotates the geometric object by the angle around the axis defined by the given point and the axis direction or plane.
notes: Vectors are not rotated around axis origin, but around their initial point. When a polygon, segment, arc, etc. is rotated, also images of the vertices / endpoints and sides (in case of polygon) are created. This command also rotates images. For text rotation use RotateText Command. * See also Rotate around Point and Rotate around Line tools.
search_text: Rotate | [transformation] | Rotate( <Object>, <Angle>, <Point on Axis>, <Axis Direction or Plane> ): Rotates the geometric object by the angle around the axis defined by the given point and the axis direction or plane.

description: Rotates the geometric object by the angle around the given point.
notes: Vectors are not rotated around axis origin, but around their initial point. When a polygon, segment, arc, etc. is rotated, also images of the vertices / endpoints and sides (in case of polygon) are created. This command also rotates images. For text rotation use RotateText Command. * See also Rotate around Point and Rotate around Line tools.
search_text: Rotate | [transformation] | Rotate( <Object>, <Angle>, <Point> ): Rotates the geometric object by the angle around the given point.

description: Creates a new LaTeX text, rotated by the given angle.
notes: The text needs to be enclosed in double quotes ". The text is rotated around the top left corner (also known as Corner 4) of the box containing it, and placed at the origin of the coordinate system. * The default setting for the angle is radians. Use the degree symbol ° for rotation angles measured in degrees.
search_text: RotateText | [text] | RotateText( <Text>, <Angle> ): Creates a new LaTeX text, rotated by the given angle. | Examples: RotateText("a = 5", 45°); Text(RotateText("GeoGebra", 42°), (6, 6),true,true)

description: Returns the row number of the spreadsheet cell (starting at 1).
search_text: Row | [spreadsheet] | Row( <Spreadsheet Cell> ): Returns the row number of the spreadsheet cell (starting at 1). | Examples: r = Row(B3)

description: Runs the click script associated with the Object (if it has one).
notes: See also RunUpdateScript command.
search_text: RunClickScript | [other] | RunClickScript( <Object> ): Runs the click script associated with the Object (if it has one). | Examples: SetValue(B,(1,1)); RunClickScript(B)

description: Runs the update script associated with the Object (if it has one).
notes: See also RunClickScript command.
search_text: RunUpdateScript | [other] | RunUpdateScript( <Object> ): Runs the update script associated with the Object (if it has one).

This command has also the equivalent syntax `stdevp( <List of Numbers>, <List of Frequencies> )`.

notes: This command has also the equivalent syntax `stdevp( <List of Raw Data> )`.
This command has also the equivalent syntax `stdevp( <List of Numbers>, <List of Frequencies> )`.
This command has also the equivalent syntax `stdevp( <List of Raw Data> )`.

description: Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_deviation[standard deviation] of the x-coordinates of the points in the given list.
search_text: SDX | [statistics] | SDX( <List of Points> ): Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_deviation[standard deviation] of the x-coordinates of the points in the given list. | Examples: SDX({(1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 1), (3, 3), (4, 2), (3, -1)})

description: Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_deviation[standard deviation] of the y-coordinates of the points in the given list.
search_text: SDY | [statistics] | SDY( <List of Points> ): Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_deviation[standard deviation] of the y-coordinates of the points in the given list. | Examples: SDY({(1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 1), (3, 3), (4, 2), (3, -1)})

description: Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singular_value_decomposition[Singular Value Decomposition] of the matrix (as a list of 3 matrices).
notes: This command is also supported in the CAS View. The numbers in the answer may vary in order between the Algebra View and CAS View. See also Eigenvalues Command, Eigenvectors Command, Invert Command, Transpose Command, JordanDiagonalization Command
search_text: SVD | [vector_and_matrix] | SVD( <Matrix> ): Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singular_value_decomposition[Singular Value Decomposition] of the matrix (as a list of 3 matrices). | Examples: SVD({{3, 1, 1}, {-1, 3, 1}})

description: Returns list of n randomly chosen elements of a list; elements can be chosen several times.
notes: In the CAS View the input list can contain different types of objects: Sample({-5, 2, a, 7, c}, 3) yields for example {a, 7, -5}. The list can include lists as well: Let List1 be {1, 2, 3}: Sample({List1, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8}, 3, false) yields for example {6, {1, 2, 3}, 4}.
search_text: Sample | [list] | Sample( <List>, <Size> ): Returns list of n randomly chosen elements of a list; elements can be chosen several times. | Examples: Sample({1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, 5)

description: Returns list of n randomly chosen elements of a list. Elements can be chosen several times if and only if the last parameter is true.
notes: In the CAS View the input list can contain different types of objects: Sample({-5, 2, a, 7, c}, 3) yields for example {a, 7, -5}. The list can include lists as well: Let List1 be {1, 2, 3}: Sample({List1, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8}, 3, false) yields for example {6, {1, 2, 3}, 4}.
search_text: Sample | [list] | Sample( <List>, <Size>, <With Replacement> ): Returns list of n randomly chosen elements of a list. Elements can be chosen several times if and only if the last parameter is true. | Examples: Sample({1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, 5, true)

description: Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_deviation#Estimation[sample standard deviation] of the numbers in the given list, having the specified frequencies.
notes: This command has also the equivalent syntax `stdev( <List of Raw Data> )`.
This command has also the equivalent syntax `stdev( <List of Numbers>, <List of Frequencies> )`.
If the list contains undefined variables in the CAS View, the command yields a formula for the sample standard deviation.
search_text: SampleSD | [statistics] | SampleSD( <List of Numbers>, <List of Frequencies> ): Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_deviation#Estimation[sample standard deviation] of the numbers in the given list, having the specified frequencies. | Examples: SampleSD({1, 2, 3, 4},{1, 1, 1, 2}); stdev({1, 2, 3, 4},{1, 1, 1, 2}); SampleSD({1, 2, a})

description: Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_deviation#Estimation[sample standard deviation] of the given list of numbers.
notes: This command has also the equivalent syntax `stdev( <List of Raw Data> )`.
This command has also the equivalent syntax `stdev( <List of Numbers>, <List of Frequencies> )`.
If the list contains undefined variables in the CAS View, the command yields a formula for the sample standard deviation.
search_text: SampleSD | [statistics] | SampleSD( <List of Raw Data> ): Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_deviation#Estimation[sample standard deviation] of the given list of numbers. | Examples: SampleSD({1, 2, 3}); stdev({1, 2, 3})

description: Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_deviation#Estimation[sample standard deviation] of the x-coordinates of the points in the given list.
search_text: SampleSDX | [statistics] | SampleSDX( <List of Points> ): Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_deviation#Estimation[sample standard deviation] of the x-coordinates of the points in the given list. | Examples: SampleSDX({(2, 3), (1, 5), (3, 6), (4, 2), (1, 1), (2, 5)})

description: Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_deviation#Estimation[sample standard deviation] of the y-coordinates of the points in the given list.
search_text: SampleSDY | [statistics] | SampleSDY( <List of Points> ): Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_deviation#Estimation[sample standard deviation] of the y-coordinates of the points in the given list. | Examples: SampleSDY({(2, 3), (1, 5), (3, 6), (4, 2), (1, 1), (2, 5)})

description: Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sample_variance#Population_variance_and_sample_variance[sample variance] of the given list of numbers with the specified frequencies.
notes: If the list in the CAS View contains undefined variables, this command yields a formula for the sample variance.
search_text: SampleVariance | [statistics] | SampleVariance( <List of Numbers>, <List of Frequencies> ): Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sample_variance#Population_variance_and_sample_variance[sample variance] of the given list of numbers with the specified frequencies. | Examples: SampleVariance({1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, {3, 2, 4, 4, 1}); SampleVariance({a, b, c})

description: Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sample_variance#Population_variance_and_sample_variance[sample variance] of the given list of numbers.
notes: If the list in the CAS View contains undefined variables, this command yields a formula for the sample variance.
search_text: SampleVariance | [statistics] | SampleVariance( <List of Raw Data> ): Returns the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sample_variance#Population_variance_and_sample_variance[sample variance] of the given list of numbers. | Examples: SampleVariance({1, 2, 3, 4, 5})

description: Creates a text displaying the given number in https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_notation[scientific notation].
notes: This command differs among variants of English: StandardForm (UK) ScientificText (US + Aus)
The text is placed with the top left corner (Corner 4) of its box at the origin of the coordinate system.
search_text: ScientificText | [text] | ScientificText( <Number> ): Creates a text displaying the given number in https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_notation[scientific notation]. | Examples: ScientificText(0.002)

description: Creates a text displaying the given number in https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_notation[scientific notation], rounded to the number of significant digits specified by precision.
notes: This command differs among variants of English: StandardForm (UK) ScientificText (US + Aus)
The text is placed with the top left corner (Corner 4) of its box at the origin of the coordinate system.
search_text: ScientificText | [text] | ScientificText( <Number>, <Precision> ): Creates a text displaying the given number in https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_notation[scientific notation], rounded to the number of significant digits specified by precision. | Examples: ScientificText(e,5)

description: Yields a conic sector between two parameter values between 0 and 2π on the conic section and calculates its area.
notes: This works only for a circle or ellipse.

Internally the following parametric forms are used: Circle: (r cos(t), r sin(t)) where r is the circle's radius. Ellipse: (a cos(t), b sin(t)) where a and b are the lengths of the semimajor and semiminor axes.
search_text: Sector | [conic] | Sector( <Conic>, <Parameter Value>, <Parameter Value> ): Yields a conic sector between two parameter values between 0 and 2π on the conic section and calculates its area. | Examples: c: x^2 + y^2 = 9; Sector(c, 0, 3/4 π)

description: Yields a conic sector between two points on the conic section and calculates its area.
notes: This works only for a circle or ellipse.

Internally the following parametric forms are used: Circle: (r cos(t), r sin(t)) where r is the circle's radius. Ellipse: (a cos(t), b sin(t)) where a and b are the lengths of the semimajor and semiminor axes.
search_text: Sector | [conic] | Sector( <Conic>, <Point>, <Point> ): Yields a conic sector between two points on the conic section and calculates its area. | Examples: c: x^2 + 2y^2 = 8; D = (-2.83, 0); E = (0, -2)

description: Creates a segment with the given starting point and length, as well as the end point of the segment.
notes: When computing intersections with other objects, only intersections lying on the segment are considered. To change this, you can use Outlying Intersections option. See also Segment and Segment_with_Given_Length tools.
search_text: Segment | [3d] | Segment( <Point>, <Length> ): Creates a segment with the given starting point and length, as well as the end point of the segment.

description: Creates a segment between two points.
notes: When computing intersections with other objects, only intersections lying on the segment are considered. To change this, you can use Outlying Intersections option. See also Segment and Segment_with_Given_Length tools.
search_text: Segment | [3d] | Segment( <Point>, <Point> ): Creates a segment between two points.

description: Deselects all selected objects.
notes: This command now cancels any drag that is in progress (useful in scripts).
search_text: SelectObjects | [other] | SelectObjects( ): Deselects all selected objects.

description: Deselects all objects and selects objects passed as parameters. All parameters must be labeled objects.
notes: This command now cancels any drag that is in progress (useful in scripts).
search_text: SelectObjects | [other] | SelectObjects( <Object>, <Object>, ... ): Deselects all objects and selects objects passed as parameters. All parameters must be labeled objects. | Examples: SelectObjects(A, B, C); SelectObjects(Midpoint(A, B))

description: Returns the selected element in a drop-down list.
notes: See also SelectedIndex command
search_text: SelectedElement | [list] | SelectedElement( <List> ): Returns the selected element in a drop-down list.

description: Returns the index of the selected element of a drop-down list.
notes: See also SelectedElement command
SetValue( <drop-down list>, <Number n > ):: Set n as [.mw-selflink .selflink]#the index of the selected element# in the drop-down list.
search_text: SelectedIndex | [list] | SelectedIndex( <List> ): Returns the index of the selected element of a drop-down list.

description: Returns the length of the semimajor axis (half of the major axis) of the conic section.
notes: See also SemiMinorAxisLength command.
search_text: SemiMajorAxisLength | [conic] | SemiMajorAxisLength( <Conic> ): Returns the length of the semimajor axis (half of the major axis) of the conic section. | Examples: SemiMajorAxisLength((x - 1)^2 + (y - 2)^2 = 4)

description: Returns the length of the semiminor axis (half of the minor axis) of the conic section.
notes: See also SemiMajorAxisLength command.
search_text: SemiMinorAxisLength | [conic] | SemiMinorAxisLength( <Conic> ): Returns the length of the semiminor axis (half of the minor axis) of the conic section. | Examples: SemiMinorAxisLength(x^2 + 2y^2 - 2x - 4y = 5)

description: Creates a semicircle above the segment between the two points and displays its length in Algebra View.
notes: See also Semicircle tool.
search_text: Semicircle | [conic] | Semicircle( <Point>, <Point> ): Creates a semicircle above the segment between the two points and displays its length in Algebra View.

description: Creates a list of integers from 1 to the given end value.
notes: This syntax can be further simplified: instead of using e.g. the formal Sequence(7,13) it is possible to obtain the same result by typing in the input bar 7..13 .
Since the parameters a and b are dynamic you could use slider variables in both cases above as well. See Lists for more information on list operations.
search_text: Sequence | [list] | Sequence( <End Value > ): Creates a list of integers from 1 to the given end value. | Examples: Sequence(4); 2^Sequence(4)

syntax: Sequence( <Expression>, <Variable k>, <Start Value a>, <End Value b> )
description: Yields a list of objects created using the given expression and the index k that ranges from start value a to end value b.
notes: This syntax can be further simplified: instead of using e.g. the formal Sequence(7,13) it is possible to obtain the same result by typing in the input bar 7..13 .
Since the parameters a and b are dynamic you could use slider variables in both cases above as well. See Lists for more information on list operations.
search_text: Sequence | [list] | Sequence( <Expression>, <Variable k>, <Start Value a>, <End Value b> ): Yields a list of objects created using the given expression and the index k that ranges from start value a to end value b. | Examples: Sequence((2, k), k, 1, 5); Sequence(x^k, k, 1, 10)

syntax: Sequence( <Expression>, <Variable k>, <Start Value a>, <End Value b>, <Increment> )
description: Yields a list of objects created using the given expression and the index k that ranges from start value a to end value b with given increment.
notes: This syntax can be further simplified: instead of using e.g. the formal Sequence(7,13) it is possible to obtain the same result by typing in the input bar 7..13 .
Since the parameters a and b are dynamic you could use slider variables in both cases above as well. See Lists for more information on list operations.
search_text: Sequence | [list] | Sequence( <Expression>, <Variable k>, <Start Value a>, <End Value b>, <Increment> ): Yields a list of objects created using the given expression and the index k that ranges from start value a to end value b with given increment. | Examples: Sequence((2, k), k, 1, 3, 0.5); Sequence(x^k, k, 1, 10, 2)

description: Creates a list of integers from k to n (increasing or decreasing).
notes: This syntax can be further simplified: instead of using e.g. the formal Sequence(7,13) it is possible to obtain the same result by typing in the input bar 7..13 .
Since the parameters a and b are dynamic you could use slider variables in both cases above as well. See Lists for more information on list operations.
search_text: Sequence | [list] | Sequence( <Start value k >, <End value n > ): Creates a list of integers from k to n (increasing or decreasing). | Examples: Sequence(7,13); Sequence(18,14); Sequence(-5, 5)

description: Creates a list of integers from k to n using the given increment.
notes: This syntax can be further simplified: instead of using e.g. the formal Sequence(7,13) it is possible to obtain the same result by typing in the input bar 7..13 .
Since the parameters a and b are dynamic you could use slider variables in both cases above as well. See Lists for more information on list operations.
search_text: Sequence | [list] | Sequence( <Start value k>, <End value n>, <Increment> ): Creates a list of integers from k to n using the given increment. | Examples: Sequence(7,13,2); Sequence(7,13,4)

description: Makes given View active. 1 or "G" for Graphics View 2 or "D" for Graphics View 2 -1 or "T" for 3D Graphics View "A" for Algebra View "S" for Spreadsheet View "C" for CAS View
notes: * See also ZoomIn, ZoomOut, Pan, SetPerspective commands.
search_text: SetActiveView | [other] | SetActiveView( <View> ): Makes given View active. 1 or "G" for Graphics View 2 or "D" for Graphics View 2 -1 or "T" for 3D Graphics View "A" for Algebra View "S" for Spreadsheet View "C" for CAS View

description: Changes the axes ratio of active Graphics View so that X units on x-axis correspond to the same number of pixels as Y units on y-axis and point (0,0) stays on its coordinates. If a unitary ratio is used, the related axis is fixed with the unit value, and the other one is adjusted as indicated.
search_text: SetAxesRatio | [other] | SetAxesRatio( <Number>, <Number> ): Changes the axes ratio of active Graphics View so that X units on x-axis correspond to the same number of pixels as Y units on y-axis and point (0,0) stays on its coordinates. If a unitary ratio is used, the related axis is fixed with the unit value, and the other one is adjusted as indicated. | Examples: SetAxesRatio(1,2); SetAxesRatio(2,1)

description: Similar to above syntax, works with 3D Graphics View.
search_text: SetAxesRatio | [other] | SetAxesRatio( <Number>, <Number>, <Number> ): Similar to above syntax, works with 3D Graphics View.

description: Changes the background color of the active Graphics View Black Dark Gray Gray Dark Blue Blue Dark Green Green Maroon Crimson Red Magenta Indigo Purple Brown Orange Gold Lime Cyan Turquoise Light Blue Aqua Silver Light Gray Pink Violet Yellow Light Yellow Light Orange Light Violet Light Purple Light Green White
notes: This command differs among variants of English: SetBackgroundColor (US) SetBackgroundColour (UK + Aus)
If you use this command in a GeoGebraScript, you must use the English color names
If you use this command in a GeoGebraScript, you must use the English color names
search_text: SetBackgroundColor | [other] | SetBackgroundColor( <"Color"> ): Changes the background color of the active Graphics View Black Dark Gray Gray Dark Blue Blue Dark Green Green Maroon Crimson Red Magenta Indigo Purple Brown Orange Gold Lime Cyan Turquoise Light Blue Aqua Silver Light Gray Pink Violet Yellow Light Yellow Light Orange Light Violet Light Purple Light Green White

description: Changes the background color of given object. This is used for Texts and for objects in the Spreadsheet. The color is entered as text, that may be: an English color name (see the list of colors). Some of them can be also used in national languages and are listed below. an hexadecimal string of the type #AARRGGBB or #RRGGBB, where AA defines transparency (00 full transparency to FF full opacity), RR defines the red component, GG the green one and BB the blue one.
notes: This command differs among variants of English: SetBackgroundColor (US) SetBackgroundColour (UK + Aus)
If you use this command in a GeoGebraScript, you must use the English color names
If you use this command in a GeoGebraScript, you must use the English color names
search_text: SetBackgroundColor | [other] | SetBackgroundColor( <Object>, <"Color"> ): Changes the background color of given object. This is used for Texts and for objects in the Spreadsheet. The color is entered as text, that may be: an English color name (see the list of colors). Some of them can be also used in national languages and are listed below. an hexadecimal string of the type #AARRGGBB or #RRGGBB, where AA defines transparency (00 full transparency to FF full opacity), RR defines the red component, GG the green one and BB the blue one. | Examples: SetBackgroundColor(text1, "#80FF0000")

description: Changes the background color of given object. This is used for Texts and for objects in the Spreadsheet. The red, green and blue represent amount of corresponding color component, 0 being minimum and 1 maximum. Number t exceeding this interval is mapped to it using function (formula).
notes: This command differs among variants of English: SetBackgroundColor (US) SetBackgroundColour (UK + Aus)
If you use this command in a GeoGebraScript, you must use the English color names
If you use this command in a GeoGebraScript, you must use the English color names
search_text: SetBackgroundColor | [other] | SetBackgroundColor( <Object>, <Red>, <Green>, <Blue> ): Changes the background color of given object. This is used for Texts and for objects in the Spreadsheet. The red, green and blue represent amount of corresponding color component, 0 being minimum and 1 maximum. Number t exceeding this interval is mapped to it using function (formula).

description: Changes the background color of the active Graphics View
notes: This command differs among variants of English: SetBackgroundColor (US) SetBackgroundColour (UK + Aus)
If you use this command in a GeoGebraScript, you must use the English color names
If you use this command in a GeoGebraScript, you must use the English color names
search_text: SetBackgroundColor | [other] | SetBackgroundColor( <Red>, <Green>, <Blue> ): Changes the background color of the active Graphics View

description: Changes the caption of the given object. Text must be enclosed in double quotes [.kcode]#"#.
search_text: SetCaption | [other] | SetCaption( <Object>, <Text> ): Changes the caption of the given object. Text must be enclosed in double quotes [.kcode]#"#.

description: Changes the color of given object. The color is entered as text, that may be: an English color name (see the list of colors). Some of them can be also used in national languages and are listed below. an hexadecimal string of the type #AARRGGBB or #RRGGBB, where AA defines transparency (01 full transparency to FF full opacity), RR defines the red component, GG the green one and BB the blue one. Black Dark Gray Gray Dark Blue Blue Dark Green Green Maroon Crimson Red Magenta Indigo Purple Brown Orange Gold Lime Cyan Turquoise Light Blue Aqua Silver Light Gray Pink Violet Yellow Light Yellow Light Orange Light Violet Light Purple Light Green White
notes: This command differs among variants of English: SetColor (US) SetColour (UK + Aus)
If you use this command in a GeoGebraScript, you must use the English color names
search_text: SetColor | [other] | SetColor( <Object>, <"Color"> ): Changes the color of given object. The color is entered as text, that may be: an English color name (see the list of colors). Some of them can be also used in national languages and are listed below. an hexadecimal string of the type #AARRGGBB or #RRGGBB, where AA defines transparency (01 full transparency to FF full opacity), RR defines the red component, GG the green one and BB the blue one. Black Dark Gray Gray Dark Blue Blue Dark Green Green Maroon Crimson Red Magenta Indigo Purple Brown Orange Gold Lime Cyan Turquoise Light Blue Aqua Silver Light Gray Pink Violet Yellow Light Yellow Light Orange Light Violet Light Purple Light Green White | Examples: SetColor(text1, "#80FF0000")

description: Changes the color of given object. The red, green and blue represent amount of corresponding color component, 0 being minimum and 1 maximum. Number t exceeding this interval is mapped to it using function (formula).
notes: This command differs among variants of English: SetColor (US) SetColour (UK + Aus)
If you use this command in a GeoGebraScript, you must use the English color names
search_text: SetColor | [other] | SetColor( <Object>, <Red>, <Green>, <Blue> ): Changes the color of given object. The red, green and blue represent amount of corresponding color component, 0 being minimum and 1 maximum. Number t exceeding this interval is mapped to it using function (formula).

description: Sets the condition to show given object.

description: Changes the construction step to given value. You can use this command to create buttons that replace or enhance the Navigation Bar.
search_text: SetConstructionStep | [geogebra_general] | SetConstructionStep( <Number> ): Changes the construction step to given value. You can use this command to create buttons that replace or enhance the Navigation Bar.

description: Sets the cartesian coordinates of free objects in a 2D View as the given coordinates. This command uses the coordinates values, not their definitions, therefore the object stays free.
notes: This also works for points on paths and in regions. The point will be moved to the closest possible position. This command works also for sliders, buttons, checkboxes, input Boxes and Images. * If the option "Absolute Screen Position" is selected, then x, y and z are in screen pixels.
search_text: SetCoords | [other] | SetCoords( <Object>, <x>, <y> ): Sets the cartesian coordinates of free objects in a 2D View as the given coordinates. This command uses the coordinates values, not their definitions, therefore the object stays free.

description: Sets the cartesian coordinates of free objects in the 3D View as the given coordinates. This command uses the coordinates values, not their definitions, therefore the object stays free.
notes: This also works for points on paths and in regions. The point will be moved to the closest possible position. This command works also for sliders, buttons, checkboxes, input Boxes and Images. * If the option "Absolute Screen Position" is selected, then x, y and z are in screen pixels.
search_text: SetCoords | [other] | SetCoords( <Object>, <x>, <y>, <z> ): Sets the cartesian coordinates of free objects in the 3D View as the given coordinates. This command uses the coordinates values, not their definitions, therefore the object stays free.

description: Sets the aspect of the line start and end of a segment. In order to view the decoration, it's necessary to hide the two points that define the vertices of the segment. The segment decoration can also be set in the Style tab of the Properties window of the segment. The first number used as command parameter defines the style of the line segment start, and the second number sets the style of the line segment end, as described in the following table. [cols=",",options="header",] |=== |Value |Decoration |0 |No decoration |1 |vertical bar |2 |empty square |3 |filled square |4 |arrow |5 |filled arrow |6 |empty dot |7 |filled dot |===

description: Sets the dynamic color of the object.

All numbers are on a scale from 0 (off/transparent) to 1 (on/opaque).

description: Sets the dynamic color and opacity of the object.
notes: This command differs among variants of English: SetDynamicColor (US) SetDynamicColour (UK + Aus)
All numbers are on a scale from 0 (off/transparent) to 1 (on/opaque).
search_text: SetDynamicColor | [other] | SetDynamicColor( <Object>, <Red>, <Green>, <Blue>, <Opacity> ): Sets the dynamic color and opacity of the object.

description: Changes the opacity of given object. Number must be from interval [0,1], where 0 means transparent and 1 means 100% opaque. Other numbers are ignored.
search_text: SetFilling | [other] | SetFilling( <Object>, <Number> ): Changes the opacity of given object. Number must be from interval [0,1], where 0 means transparent and 1 means 100% opaque. Other numbers are ignored.

description: Fills the given object with an image.

description: Changes the size of the vertices of an object. The object can be any 2D or 3D one with vertices, e.g. a polygon, a polyhedron, a net...
notes: The command is also applicable to lists of (unlabeled) points, e.g. if ++list={(1, 2), (3, 4)}++, then SetPointSize(list,5) changes the size of the listed points.

search_text: SetPointSize | [other] | SetPointSize( <Point>, <Number> ): Changes the size of the given point to the given number.

description: Seeds the random number generator so that subsequent random numbers will be determined by the seed.
notes: See also RandomBetween command, RandomElement command, RandomBinomial command, RandomNormal command, RandomPoisson command, RandomUniform command.
search_text: SetSeed | [other] | SetSeed( <Integer> ): Seeds the random number generator so that subsequent random numbers will be determined by the seed. | Examples: SetSeed(33)

description: Sets the rotational speed of 3D view about the axis currently displayed in vertical position. The sign and value of the entered Number define the rotation as follows: if Number is greater than 1, then the 3D view rotates counter clockwise. if Number is less than -1, then the 3D view rotates clockwise. * if Number is between -1 and 1 then the rotation will be cancelled.
search_text: SetSpinSpeed | [other] | SetSpinSpeed( <Number> ): Sets the rotational speed of 3D view about the axis currently displayed in vertical position. The sign and value of the entered Number define the rotation as follows: if Number is greater than 1, then the 3D view rotates counter clockwise. if Number is less than -1, then the 3D view rotates clockwise. * if Number is between -1 and 1 then the rotation will be cancelled.

description: Let A be the first and B the second object. If A is a free object or a Point restricted to Path or Region, its value is set to current value of B (i.e. A doesn't change value if B is changed afterwards).

description: Sets the direction of the 3D view orientation to the default position.
notes: The view direction can be set towards a line, segment, plane, etc. If you do eg SetViewDirection(x + y + z = 1) twice then there are two possible outcomes and the second one will rotate the view 180°. To avoid ambiguity use eg SetViewDirection(Vector((0, 0, 1))) * See also View in front of tool.
search_text: SetViewDirection | [other] | SetViewDirection( ): Sets the direction of the 3D view orientation to the default position. | Examples: SetViewDirection()

description: Sets the direction of the 3D view orientation depending on the given object.
notes: The view direction can be set towards a line, segment, plane, etc. If you do eg SetViewDirection(x + y + z = 1) twice then there are two possible outcomes and the second one will rotate the view 180°. To avoid ambiguity use eg SetViewDirection(Vector((0, 0, 1))) * See also View in front of tool.
search_text: SetViewDirection | [other] | SetViewDirection( <Direction> ): Sets the direction of the 3D view orientation depending on the given object. | Examples: SetViewDirection(Vector((0, 0, 1))); SetViewDirection((0, 0, 1)); SetViewDirection(x + y + z = 1)

description: Sets the direction of the 3D view orientation depending on the given object, with optional animation.
notes: The view direction can be set towards a line, segment, plane, etc. If you do eg SetViewDirection(x + y + z = 1) twice then there are two possible outcomes and the second one will rotate the view 180°. To avoid ambiguity use eg SetViewDirection(Vector((0, 0, 1))) * See also View in front of tool.
search_text: SetViewDirection | [other] | SetViewDirection( <Direction>, <Boolean animated> ): Sets the direction of the 3D view orientation depending on the given object, with optional animation. | Examples: SetViewDirection(Vector((1; α; -30°)), false)

description: Makes object visible or hidden in given Graphics View. Use -1 for the 3D View
notes: You can also use these special object names: xAxis, yAxis, zAxis, xOyPlane
search_text: SetVisibleInView | [other] | SetVisibleInView( <Object>, <View Number 1|2|-1>, <Boolean> ): Makes object visible or hidden in given Graphics View. Use -1 for the 3D View

description: Shears the object so that points on the line stay fixed. points at distance d from the line are shifted by d (formula) ratio in direction of the line (direction of the shift is different for halfplanes with respect to the line). A sheared plane figure maintains its original area.
search_text: Shear | [transformation] | Shear( <Object>, <Line>, <Ratio> ): Shears the object so that points on the line stay fixed. points at distance d from the line are shifted by d (formula) ratio in direction of the line (direction of the shift is different for halfplanes with respect to the line). A sheared plane figure maintains its original area.

syntax: ShortestDistance( <List of Segments>, <Start Point>, <End Point>, <Boolean Weighted> )
description: Finds shortest path between start point and endpoint in a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graph_(mathematics)[graph] given by list of segments. If weighted is false, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weighted_graph#Weighted_graphs_and_networks[weight] of each edge is supposed to be 1 (i.e. we are looking for the path with least number of edges), otherwise it is the length of given segment (we are looking for the geometrically shortest path).
search_text: ShortestDistance | [discrete_math] | ShortestDistance( <List of Segments>, <Start Point>, <End Point>, <Boolean Weighted> ): Finds shortest path between start point and endpoint in a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graph_(mathematics)[graph] given by list of segments. If weighted is false, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weighted_graph#Weighted_graphs_and_networks[weight] of each edge is supposed to be 1 (i.e. we are looking for the path with least number of edges), otherwise it is the length of given segment (we are looking for the geometrically shortest path).

description: Simplifies the terms of the given function, if possible.
notes: The FormulaText Command normally produces better results and is simpler.
This command needs to load the Computer Algebra System, so can be slow on some computers. Try using the Polynomial Command instead.
See also Factor Command, Assume Command, PartialFractions Command, Expand Command, Polynomial Command.
search_text: Simplify | [algebra] | Simplify( <Function> ): Simplifies the terms of the given function, if possible. | Examples: Simplify(x + x + x)

description: Simplifies the terms of the given function, if possible. Undefined variables can be included in the terms.
notes: The FormulaText Command normally produces better results and is simpler.
This command needs to load the Computer Algebra System, so can be slow on some computers. Try using the Polynomial Command instead.
See also Factor Command, Assume Command, PartialFractions Command, Expand Command, Polynomial Command.
search_text: Simplify | [algebra] | Simplify( <Function> ): Simplifies the terms of the given function, if possible. Undefined variables can be included in the terms. | Examples: Simplify(3 * x + 4 * x + a * x); a x + 7x; Assume(x<2,Simplify(sqrt(x-2sqrt(x-1))))

description: Attempts to tidy up text expressions by removing repeated negatives etc.
notes: The FormulaText Command normally produces better results and is simpler.
This command needs to load the Computer Algebra System, so can be slow on some computers. Try using the Polynomial Command instead.
See also Factor Command, Assume Command, PartialFractions Command, Expand Command, Polynomial Command.
search_text: Simplify | [algebra] | Simplify( <Text> ): Attempts to tidy up text expressions by removing repeated negatives etc. | Examples: Simplify("f(x) = " + a + "x² + " + b + "x + " + c)

description: Creates a slider. The parameters settings can be as follows: Min, Max: set the range of the slider - These parameters are compulsory. Increment: set the increment of the slider's value - default: 0.1 Speed: set the slider speed during animations - default: 1 Width: sets the slider width in pixels - default: 100 Is Angle: sets if the slider is related to an angle. This parameter can be true or false - default: false Horizontal: sets whether the slider is shown as an horizontal (true) or vertical (false) segment - default: true Animating: sets the automatic animation of the slider - default: false Random: sets if the slider assumes continuous values in the [Min, Max] range (false), or random values in the same interval (true) - default: false
notes: See also the Slider tool.
search_text: Slider | [other] | Slider( <Min>, <Max>, <Increment>, <Speed>, <Width>,<Is Angle>, <Horizontal>, <Animating>, <Boolean Random>): Creates a slider. The parameters settings can be as follows: Min, Max: set the range of the slider - These parameters are compulsory. Increment: set the increment of the slider's value - default: 0.1 Speed: set the slider speed during animations - default: 1 Width: sets the slider width in pixels - default: 100 Is Angle: sets if the slider is related to an angle. This parameter can be true or false - default: false Horizontal: sets whether the slider is shown as an horizontal (true) or vertical (false) segment - default: true Animating: sets the automatic animation of the slider - default: false Random: sets if the slider assumes continuous values in the [Min, Max] range (false), or random values in the same interval (true) - default: false

description: Returns the slope of the given line.

description: Creates an animated graph of the given function, that is plotted from left to right. The animation is controlled by a slider, which is also created by this command: if Repeat is false, the graph is plotted only once - corresponding to the slider setting Increasing (once), if Repeat is true or omitted, the graph is plotted continuously - corresponding to the slider setting Increasing.
search_text: SlowPlot | [geogebra_general] | SlowPlot( <Function >, <Boolean Repeat >): Creates an animated graph of the given function, that is plotted from left to right. The animation is controlled by a slider, which is also created by this command: if Repeat is false, the graph is plotted only once - corresponding to the slider setting Increasing (once), if Repeat is true or omitted, the graph is plotted continuously - corresponding to the slider setting Increasing.

description: Solves a given equation for the main variable and returns a list of all solutions.
notes: Starting from version 823 this command behaves as Solve command, except for the format of the result: command Solutions returns a list of values, while command Solve returns a list of equations in the form variable name = value.

Sometimes you need to do some manipulation to allow the automatic solver to work, for example Solutions(TrigExpand(sin(5/4 π + x) - cos(x - 3/4 π) = sqrt(6) cos(x) - sqrt(2))) * See also Solve Command.
search_text: Solutions | [algebra] | Solutions( <Equation> ): Solves a given equation for the main variable and returns a list of all solutions. | Examples: Solutions(x^2 = 4x)

description: Solves an equation for a given unknown variable and returns a list of all solutions.

search_text: Solutions | [algebra] | Solutions( <Equation>, <Variable> ): Solves an equation for a given unknown variable and returns a list of all solutions. | Examples: Solutions(x * a^2 = 4a, a)

examples: Solutions({x = 4 x + y , y + x = 2}, {x, y})
notes: Starting from version 823 this command behaves as Solve command, except for the format of the result: command Solutions returns a list of values, while command Solve returns a list of equations in the form variable name = value.

Sometimes you need to do some manipulation to allow the automatic solver to work, for example Solutions(TrigExpand(sin(5/4 π + x) - cos(x - 3/4 π) = sqrt(6) cos(x) - sqrt(2))) * See also Solve Command.
search_text: Solutions | [algebra] | Solutions( <List of Equations>, <List of Variables> ): Solves a set of equations for a given set of unknown variables and returns a list of all solutions. | Examples: Solutions({x = 4 x + y , y + x = 2}, {x, y}); Solutions({2a^2 + 5a + 3 = b, a + b = 3}, {a, b})

description: Solves a given equation for the main variable and returns a list of all solutions.
notes: Commands Solve and Solutions solve an equation or a system of equations over the real numbers symbolically. To solve equations numerically, use the NSolve Command. For solving equations in complex numbers see CSolve Command.

The right hand side of equations (in any of the above syntaxes) can be omitted. If the right hand side is missing, it is treated as 0. Sometimes you need to do some manipulation to allow the automatic solver to work, for example Solve(TrigExpand(sin(5/4 π + x) - cos(x - 3/4 π) = sqrt(6) cos(x) - sqrt(2))). For piecewise-defined functions, you will need to use NSolve
search_text: Solve | [algebra] | Solve( <Equation in x> ): Solves a given equation for the main variable and returns a list of all solutions. | Examples: Solve(x^2 = 4x)

The right hand side of equations (in any of the above syntaxes) can be omitted. If the right hand side is missing, it is treated as 0. Sometimes you need to do some manipulation to allow the automatic solver to work, for example Solve(TrigExpand(sin(5/4 π + x) - cos(x - 3/4 π) = sqrt(6) cos(x) - sqrt(2))). For piecewise-defined functions, you will need to use NSolve
search_text: Solve | [algebra] | Solve( <Equation in x>, <Assumption> ): Solves an equation x, conditional on the assumption | Examples: Solve(x^2=1, x>0)

description: Solves a given https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cubic_function[cubic polynomial] and returns a list of all solutions.
notes: Often the answers are very cumbersome, e.g. SolveCubic(x³ + x² + x + 2) in which case Solve(x³ + x² + x + 2) or CSolve(x³ + x² + x + 2) may work better for you.
search_text: SolveCubic | [algebra] | SolveCubic( <Cubic Polynomial> ): Solves a given https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cubic_function[cubic polynomial] and returns a list of all solutions. | Examples: SolveCubic(x³ - 1)

description: Attempts to find the exact solution of the first or second order ODE. For first and second derivative of y you can use y' and y'' respectively.
notes: (formula) will be created as an auxiliary object with a corresponding slider.

Length( <Locus> ) allows you to find out how many points are in the computed locus. First( <Locus>, <Number> ) allows you to extract the points as a list. To find the "reverse" solution, just enter a negative value for End x, for example SolveODE(-xy, x(A), y(A), -5, 0.1)
To find the "reverse" solution, just enter a negative value for End t, for example SolveODE(-x, y, x(A), y(A), -5, 0.1).
Always returns the result as locus. The algorithms are currently based on https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Runge-Kutta_methods[Runge-Kutta numeric methods].
See also SlopeField command.
For compatibility with input bar, if the first parameter is just an expression without y' or y'', it is supposed to be right hand side of ODE with left hand side y'.
search_text: SolveODE | [functions_and_calculus] | SolveODE( <Equation> ): Attempts to find the exact solution of the first or second order ODE. For first and second derivative of y you can use y' and y'' respectively. | Examples: SolveODE(y' = y / x)

description: Creates a cubic https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spline_(mathematics)[spline] through all points.
notes: The choice of default makes the result behave nicely when transformed, making sure that Rotate(Spline(list), a) gives the same as Spline(Rotate(list, a)).
search_text: Spline | [functions_and_calculus] | Spline( <List of Points> ): Creates a cubic https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spline_(mathematics)[spline] through all points.

description: Creates a spline with given order through all points.
notes: The choice of default makes the result behave nicely when transformed, making sure that Rotate(Spline(list), a) gives the same as Spline(Rotate(list, a)).
search_text: Spline | [functions_and_calculus] | Spline( <List of Points>, <Order ≥ 3> ): Creates a spline with given order through all points.

description: Creates a spline with given order through all points. The weight function says what should be the difference of t values for point _P_~i~ and _P_~i+1~ given their difference _P_~i+1~ - _P_~i~ = (x, y). To get the spline you expect from "function" algorithm you should use abs(x)+0*y, to get the GeoGebra's default spline you can use sqrt(x^2+y^2).
notes: The choice of default makes the result behave nicely when transformed, making sure that Rotate(Spline(list), a) gives the same as Spline(Rotate(list, a)).
search_text: Spline | [functions_and_calculus] | Spline( <List of Points>, <Order ≥ 3>, <Weight Function> ): Creates a spline with given order through all points. The weight function says what should be the difference of t values for point _P_~i~ and _P_~i+1~ given their difference _P_~i+1~ - _P_~i~ = (x, y). To get the spline you expect from "function" algorithm you should use abs(x)+0*y, to get the GeoGebra's default spline you can use sqrt(x^2+y^2).

syntax: Split( <Text>, <List of Texts to split on>)
description: Creates the list of texts obtained by splitting the given text at the given separators (not included in the list).
search_text: Split | [text] | Split( <Text>, <List of Texts to split on>): Creates the list of texts obtained by splitting the given text at the given separators (not included in the list). | Examples: Split("3cos(t)cos(2y)", {"cos"}); {"3", "(t)", "(2y)"}

description: Resumes all animations if they are paused.
notes: See also Animation.
search_text: StartAnimation | [other] | StartAnimation( ): Resumes all animations if they are paused.

description: When the boolean is false, pauses all animations, otherwise resumes them.
notes: See also Animation.
search_text: StartAnimation | [other] | StartAnimation( <Boolean> ): When the boolean is false, pauses all animations, otherwise resumes them.

description: Starts animating given points and sliders, the points must be on paths.
notes: See also Animation.
search_text: StartAnimation | [other] | StartAnimation( <Point or Slider>, <Point or Slider>, .... ): Starts animating given points and sliders, the points must be on paths.

description: Starts (for boolean = true) or permanently stops (for boolean = false) animating given points and sliders, the points must be on paths.
notes: See also Animation.
search_text: StartAnimation | [other] | StartAnimation( <Point or Slider>, <Point or Slider>, ...., <Boolean> ): Starts (for boolean = true) or permanently stops (for boolean = false) animating given points and sliders, the points must be on paths.

description: Resumes all recording to spreadsheet if paused (and stores a value for each object).
search_text: StartRecord | [other] | StartRecord( ): Resumes all recording to spreadsheet if paused (and stores a value for each object).

description: When the boolean is false, pauses all recording to the spreadsheet, otherwise resumes it (and stores a value for each object).
search_text: StartRecord | [other] | StartRecord( <Boolean> ): When the boolean is false, pauses all recording to the spreadsheet, otherwise resumes it (and stores a value for each object).

description: Returns a stem plot of the given list of numbers. Outliers are removed from the plot and listed separately. An outlier is defined as a value outside the interval [ Q1 - 1.5 (Q3 - Q1) , Q3 + 1.5 (Q3 - Q1) ].
notes: This command differs among variants of English: StemPlot (US) StemAndLeaf (UK + Aus)
search_text: StemPlot | [chart] | StemPlot( <List> ): Returns a stem plot of the given list of numbers. Outliers are removed from the plot and listed separately. An outlier is defined as a value outside the interval [ Q1 - 1.5 (Q3 - Q1) , Q3 + 1.5 (Q3 - Q1) ].

description: Returns a stem plot of the given list of numbers. If Adjustment = -1 the default stem unit is divided by 10 If Adjustment = 0 nothing is changed * If Adjustment = 1 the default stem unit is multiplied by 10
notes: This command differs among variants of English: StemPlot (US) StemAndLeaf (UK + Aus)
search_text: StemPlot | [chart] | StemPlot( <List>, <Adjustment -1|0|1> ): Returns a stem plot of the given list of numbers. If Adjustment = -1 the default stem unit is divided by 10 If Adjustment = 0 nothing is changed * If Adjustment = 1 the default stem unit is multiplied by 10

description: Draws a step graph of the given list of points. Each point is connected to the next point in the list by a horizontal line segment.
search_text: StepGraph | [chart] | StepGraph( <List of Points> ): Draws a step graph of the given list of points. Each point is connected to the next point in the list by a horizontal line segment. | Examples: StepGraph({(1, 1), (3, 2), (4, 5), (5, 7)})

description: Draws a step graph of the given list of points. If Join = false, then a horizontal line segment is drawn towards the x-coordinate of the next point, but a vertical line segment is not drawn. If Join = true, then each point is connected to the next point in the list by a horizontal and a vertical line segment.
search_text: StepGraph | [chart] | StepGraph( <List of Points>, <Boolean Join> ): Draws a step graph of the given list of points. If Join = false, then a horizontal line segment is drawn towards the x-coordinate of the next point, but a vertical line segment is not drawn. If Join = true, then each point is connected to the next point in the list by a horizontal and a vertical line segment. | Examples: StepGraph({(1, 1), (3, 2), (4, 5), (5, 7)}, true)

description: Draws a step graph as described above.
search_text: StepGraph | [chart] | StepGraph( <List of Points>, <Boolean Join>, <Point Style> ): Draws a step graph as described above. | Examples: StepGraph({(1, 1), (3, 2), (4, 5), (5, 7)}, false, 1)

description: Draws a step graph of a list of points created from the given lists of coordinates. Each point is connected to the next point in the list by a horizontal line segment.
search_text: StepGraph | [chart] | StepGraph( <List of x-coordinates>, <List of y-coordinates> ): Draws a step graph of a list of points created from the given lists of coordinates. Each point is connected to the next point in the list by a horizontal line segment. | Examples: StepGraph({1, 3, 4, 5}, {1, 2, 5, 7})

syntax: StepGraph( <List of x-coordinates>, <List of y-coordinates>, <Boolean Join> )
description: Draws a step graph of a list of points created from the given lists of coordinates. If Join = false, then a horizontal line segment is drawn towards the x-coordinate of the next point, but a vertical line segment is not drawn. If Join = true, then each point is connected to the next point in the list by a horizontal and a vertical line segment.
search_text: StepGraph | [chart] | StepGraph( <List of x-coordinates>, <List of y-coordinates>, <Boolean Join> ): Draws a step graph of a list of points created from the given lists of coordinates. If Join = false, then a horizontal line segment is drawn towards the x-coordinate of the next point, but a vertical line segment is not drawn. If Join = true, then each point is connected to the next point in the list by a horizontal and a vertical line segment. | Examples: StepGraph({1, 3, 4, 5}, {1, 2, 5, 7}, true)

syntax: StepGraph( <List of x-coordinates>, <List of y-coordinates>, <Boolean Join>, <Point Style> )
description: Draws a step graph as described above. Point style values of -2, -1, 0, 1, -1 determine how points are drawn as follows: 0 = no points are drawn 1 = solid points on the right 2 = solid points on the right, open points on the left -1 = solid points on the left * -2 = solid points on the left, open points on the right
search_text: StepGraph | [chart] | StepGraph( <List of x-coordinates>, <List of y-coordinates>, <Boolean Join>, <Point Style> ): Draws a step graph as described above. Point style values of -2, -1, 0, 1, -1 determine how points are drawn as follows: 0 = no points are drawn 1 = solid points on the right 2 = solid points on the right, open points on the left -1 = solid points on the left * -2 = solid points on the left, open points on the right | Examples: StepGraph({1, 3, 4, 5}, {1, 2, 5, 7}, false, 1)

description: Draws a stick graph of the given points. For each point a vertical line segment is drawn from the x-axis to the point.

If Horizontal = false, then vertical line segments are drawn from the x-axis to each point. | Examples: StickGraph({(1, 1), (3, 2), (4, 5), (5, 7)}, false)

For each point a vertical line segment is drawn from the x-axis to the point. | Examples: StickGraph({1, 3, 4, 5}, {1, 2, 5, 7})

description: Draws a stick graph of points created from the two lists of coordinates. If Horizontal = true, then horizontal line segments are drawn from the y-axis to each point. If Horizontal = false, then vertical line segments are drawn from the x-axis to each point.

If Horizontal = true, then horizontal line segments are drawn from the y-axis to each point. If Horizontal = false, then vertical line segments are drawn from the x-axis to each point. | Examples: StickGraph({1, 3, 4, 5}, {1, 2, 5, 7}, true)

description: The object is stretched perpendicular to the line by the given ratio (i.e. points on the line aren't moved and the distance of other points from the line is multiplied by given ratio.)
search_text: Stretch | [transformation] | Stretch( <xref:/Geometric_Objects.adoc[Object]>, <Line>, <Ratio> ): The object is stretched perpendicular to the line by the given ratio (i.e. points on the line aren't moved and the distance of other points from the line is multiplied by given ratio.)

description: The object is stretched parallel to the given vector by the ratio given by the magnitude of the vector (i.e. points on the line perpendicular to the vector (through its startpoint) stay on their place and distance of other points from the line is multiplied by given ratio.)
search_text: Stretch | [transformation] | Stretch( <xref:/Geometric_Objects.adoc[Object]>, <Vector> ): The object is stretched parallel to the given vector by the ratio given by the magnitude of the vector (i.e. points on the line perpendicular to the vector (through its startpoint) stay on their place and distance of other points from the line is multiplied by given ratio.)

description: Replaces in expression every occurrence of the variables in the substitution list with the corresponding terms or values, and evaluates numerical substitutions.
examples: Substitute(2x + 3y - z, {x = a, y = 2, z = b})
Substitute(2x + 3y - z, x = a, y = 2, z = b)
search_text: Substitute | [algebra] | Substitute( <Expression>, <Substitution List> ): Replaces in expression every occurrence of the variables in the substitution list with the corresponding terms or values, and evaluates numerical substitutions. | Examples: Substitute(2x + 3y - z, {x = a, y = 2, z = b}); Substitute(2x + 3y - z, x = a, y = 2, z = b)

description: Replaces in expression all occurrences of from with to and evaluates the result when variables are substituted with values.
search_text: Substitute | [algebra] | Substitute( <Expression>, <from>, <to> ): Replaces in expression all occurrences of from with to and evaluates the result when variables are substituted with values. | Examples: Substitute((3 m - 3)^2 - (m + 3)^2, m, a); Substitute((3 m - 3)^2 - (m + 3)^2, m, 2)

description: Computes the sum (formula). End value can also be infinity.
notes: This command works for numbers, points, vectors, text, and functions. Lists must contain objects of the same type.
search_text: Sum | [algebra] | Sum( <Expression>, <Variable>, <Start Value>, <End Value> ): Computes the sum (formula). End value can also be infinity. | Examples: Sum(n^2, n, 1, 3); Sum(r^k, k, 0, n); Sum((1/3)^n, n, 0, Infinity)

description: Calculates the sum of all the elements in the list.
notes: This command works for numbers, points, vectors, text, and functions. Lists must contain objects of the same type.
search_text: Sum | [algebra] | Sum( <List> ): Calculates the sum of all the elements in the list. | Examples: Sum({1, 2, 3}); Sum({x^2,  x^3}); Sum(Sequence(i, i, 1, 100))

description: Returns the sum of the given list of values, considering the related frequencies.
notes: This command works for numbers, points, vectors, text, and functions. Lists must contain objects of the same type.
search_text: Sum | [algebra] | Sum( <List>, <List of Frequencies> ): Returns the sum of the given list of values, considering the related frequencies. | Examples: Sum({1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, {3, 2, 4, 4, 1})

description: Calculates the sum of the first n elements in the list.
notes: This command works for numbers, points, vectors, text, and functions. Lists must contain objects of the same type.
search_text: Sum | [algebra] | Sum( <List>, <Number of Elements> ): Calculates the sum of the first n elements in the list. | Examples: Sum({1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}, 4)

description: Calculates the sum of squared errors, SSE, between the y-values of the points in the list and the function values of the x-values in the list.
search_text: SumSquaredErrors | [statistics] | SumSquaredErrors( <List of Points>, <Function> ): Calculates the sum of squared errors, SSE, between the y-values of the points in the list and the function values of the x-values in the list. | Examples: L={(1, 2), (3, 5),(2, 2), (5, 2), (5, 5)}; f(x)=FitPoly(L,1); g(x)=FitPoly(L,2)

description: Creates a text representation of the number in the form (formula).
notes: In order to use this command in a Text object, the option LaTeX Formula needs to be enabled in the Text tab of the Properties Dialog of the text. Since this command works with a rounded decimal number as input, in some cases the result might be unexpected. If a suitable answer can't be found, the number will be returned. For example, the command SurdText(1.23456789012345) returns 1.23456789012345. See also the FractionText and ScientificText commands.
search_text: SurdText | [text] | SurdText( <Number> ): Creates a text representation of the number in the form (formula). | Examples: SurdText(2.414213562373095); SurdText(2.439230484541326)

description: Creates a text representation of the number, rewritten as a multiple of the constants in the list. If the list is empty, the command uses a list of common constants.
notes: In order to use this command in a Text object, the option LaTeX Formula needs to be enabled in the Text tab of the Properties Dialog of the text. Since this command works with a rounded decimal number as input, in some cases the result might be unexpected. If a suitable answer can't be found, the number will be returned. For example, the command SurdText(1.23456789012345) returns 1.23456789012345. See also the FractionText and ScientificText commands.
search_text: SurdText | [text] | SurdText( <Number>, <List> ): Creates a text representation of the number, rewritten as a multiple of the constants in the list. If the list is empty, the command uses a list of common constants. | Examples: SurdText(3.718281828459045, {exp(1)}); SurdText(5.382332347441762, {sqrt(2), sqrt(3), sqrt(5)}); SurdText(1.693147180559945, {ln(2)})

description: Creates a text representation of the point, with coordinates in the form (formula).
notes: In order to use this command in a Text object, the option LaTeX Formula needs to be enabled in the Text tab of the Properties Dialog of the text. Since this command works with a rounded decimal number as input, in some cases the result might be unexpected. If a suitable answer can't be found, the number will be returned. For example, the command SurdText(1.23456789012345) returns 1.23456789012345. See also the FractionText and ScientificText commands.
search_text: SurdText | [text] | SurdText( <Point> ): Creates a text representation of the point, with coordinates in the form (formula). | Examples: SurdText((2.414213562373095, 1.414213562373095))

description: Creates a surface of revolution, rotating the Curve from 0 to given Angle around the Line.
notes: End Value must be greater than or equal to Start Value and both must be finite. x, y and z are not allowed as parameter variables.
search_text: Surface | [3d] | Surface( <Curve>, <Angle>, <Line>): Creates a surface of revolution, rotating the Curve from 0 to given Angle around the Line.

syntax: Surface( <Expression>, <Expression>, <Expression>, <Parameter Variable 1>, <Start Value>, <End Value>, <Parameter Variable 2>, <Start Value>, <End Value> )
description: Yields the Cartesian parametric 3D surface for the given x-expression (first <Expression>), y-expression (second <Expression>) and z-expression (third <Expression>), using two <Parameter Variables> within the given intervals [<Start Value>, <End Value>].
examples: Surface((R + r cos( u)) cos(v) , (R + r cos( u)) sin(v) , r sin(u ), u, 0, 2 π , v, 0, 2 π)
notes: End Value must be greater than or equal to Start Value and both must be finite. x, y and z are not allowed as parameter variables.
search_text: Surface | [3d] | Surface( <Expression>, <Expression>, <Expression>, <Parameter Variable 1>, <Start Value>, <End Value>, <Parameter Variable 2>, <Start Value>, <End Value> ): Yields the Cartesian parametric 3D surface for the given x-expression (first <Expression>), y-expression (second <Expression>) and z-expression (third <Expression>), using two <Parameter Variables> within the given intervals [<Start Value>, <End Value>]. | Examples: Surface((R + r cos( u)) cos(v) , (R + r cos( u)) sin(v) , r sin(u ), u, 0, 2 π , v, 0, 2 π)

description: Creates a surface of revolution, rotating the given Function from 0 to given Angle around the x-axis.
notes: End Value must be greater than or equal to Start Value and both must be finite. x, y and z are not allowed as parameter variables.
search_text: Surface | [3d] | Surface( <Function>, <Angle> ): Creates a surface of revolution, rotating the given Function from 0 to given Angle around the x-axis.

description: Evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a t-distribution at variable value v, i.e. calculates the probability P(X ≤ v) where X is a random variable with a t-distribution with the given degrees of freedom.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given value, that is the area under the t-distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: TDistribution | [probability] | TDistribution( <Degrees of Freedom>, <Variable Value> ): Evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a t-distribution at variable value v, i.e. calculates the probability P(X ≤ v) where X is a random variable with a t-distribution with the given degrees of freedom. | Examples: TDistribution(10, 0)

description: Evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a t-distribution at variable value v, i.e. calculates the probability P(X ≤ v) where X is a random variable with a t-distribution with the given degrees of freedom.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given value, that is the area under the t-distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: TDistribution | [probability] | TDistribution( <Degrees of Freedom>, <Variable Value> ): Evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a t-distribution at variable value v, i.e. calculates the probability P(X ≤ v) where X is a random variable with a t-distribution with the given degrees of freedom. | Examples: TDistribution(10, 0)

description: If Cumulative = true, evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a t-distribution with given degrees of freedom at the given variable value, otherwise it evaluates the probability density function of the distribution at variable value.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given value, that is the area under the t-distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: TDistribution | [probability] | TDistribution( <Degrees of Freedom>, <Variable Value>, <Boolean Cumulative> ): If Cumulative = true, evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a t-distribution with given degrees of freedom at the given variable value, otherwise it evaluates the probability density function of the distribution at variable value.

description: If Cumulative = true, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Student%27s_t-distribution[t-distribution] with given degrees of freedom, otherwise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.
notes: This syntax returns the probability at a given value, that is the area under the t-distribution curve to the left of the given x-coordinate.
search_text: TDistribution | [probability] | TDistribution( <Degrees of Freedom>, x, <Boolean Cumulative> ): If Cumulative = true, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Student%27s_t-distribution[t-distribution] with given degrees of freedom, otherwise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.

syntax: TMean2Estimate( <List of Sample Data 1>, <List of Sample Data 2>, <Confidence Level>, <Boolean Pooled> )
description: Calculates a t confidence interval estimate of the difference between two population means using the given sample data sets and confidence level. If Pooled = true, then population variances are assumed equal and sample standard deviations are combined in calculation. If Pooled = false, then population variances are not assumed equal and sample standard deviations are not combined. Results are returned in list form as {lower confidence limit, upper confidence limit}.
search_text: TMean2Estimate | [statistics] | TMean2Estimate( <List of Sample Data 1>, <List of Sample Data 2>, <Confidence Level>, <Boolean Pooled> ): Calculates a t confidence interval estimate of the difference between two population means using the given sample data sets and confidence level. If Pooled = true, then population variances are assumed equal and sample standard deviations are combined in calculation. If Pooled = false, then population variances are not assumed equal and sample standard deviations are not combined. Results are returned in list form as {lower confidence limit, upper confidence limit}.

syntax: TMean2Estimate( <Sample Mean 1>, <Sample Standard Deviation 1>, <Sample Size 1>, <Sample Mean 2>, <Sample Standard Deviation 2>, <Sample Size 2>, <Confidence Level>, <Boolean Pooled> )
description: Calculates a t confidence interval estimate of the difference between two population means using the given sample statistics and confidence level. Pooled is defined as above. Results are returned in list form as {lower confidence limit, upper confidence limit}.
search_text: TMean2Estimate | [statistics] | TMean2Estimate( <Sample Mean 1>, <Sample Standard Deviation 1>, <Sample Size 1>, <Sample Mean 2>, <Sample Standard Deviation 2>, <Sample Size 2>, <Confidence Level>, <Boolean Pooled> ): Calculates a t confidence interval estimate of the difference between two population means using the given sample statistics and confidence level. Pooled is defined as above. Results are returned in list form as {lower confidence limit, upper confidence limit}.
